title: Plan-review criterion: leaf-plan parent-containment (leaf scope must be a subset of its parent; parent wins on conflict)
plan:
## Why
A significant epic failure showed leaf plans DRIFT as leaf tickets are claimed and edited: by epic close, the leaf ACs described a very different plan than what the epic said would be delivered, and nothing caught the divergence until the epic's completion verification. No current criterion checks a leaf plan against its PARENT'S plan — G1G2/E4 ground against the codebase, COH checks internal coherence. IMPORTANT CORRECTION (verified against the code): there is NO existing "ancestor carve-out" that injects a parent's PLAN TEXT — `pass1._ticket_graph_blob` (pass1.py:281) injects only the parent's ticket ID string into context. So the leaf-vs-parent comparison is NEW behavior this ticket adds — implemented by having G7's AGENT read the parent via its show_ticket tool at runtime (no new context-plumbing), not by mirroring a (non-existent) injection carve-out. The review that gates the CLAIM is the right moment to catch drift, ticket by ticket, instead of at epic close.

## What
- Add a new plan-review criterion (working id `G7`, facet parent-containment) to src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json + the registry: `exec: AGENT` (it must read the parent ticket from the store, like the other codebase-grounded criteria), with a rubric prompt at src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ following the existing G-series prompt conventions.
- ROUTING — fires ONLY on leaf tickets WITH a parent: the gate is (ticket has a parent_id) AND (ticket has no children). MECHANISM (the existing schema can't express "has_parent_id" — `applies()` in registry.py:311-339 gates only on `scope` (container/leaf), `suppress_types`, `suppress_when`): add a new boolean axis `require_parent_id` to the `applies_at` schema and to `applies()`, and thread the ticket's `has_parent_id` fact through `route_criteria()` (orchestrator.py:337-375, which calls `registry.applies()`) from the assembled PlanContext (which already knows the parent chain). So G7's routing = `applies_at: {scope: ["leaf"], require_parent_id: true, suppress_types: ["bug","session_log"]}`. The `scope:["leaf"]` gives "no children"; `require_parent_id` gives "has a parent"; bugs/session_logs stay exempt as today; parentless tickets and containers never run it. The `require_parent_id` axis: `_validate_routing_entry` (overlay.py:190-212) currently does NOT reject unknown applies_at keys (so the key would pass unvalidated) — this ticket ADDS an explicit `require_parent_id` boolean-type check there (non-bool raises), and `registry.validate_packaged_routing()` stays green on the packaged G7 entry.
- SEMANTICS: the leaf's What/Scope/ACs must be a SUBSET of the parent's declared scope — a finding fires when the leaf (a) delivers something the parent's plan does not contain or implies is out of scope, (b) contradicts a parent AC/success criterion, or (c) redefines a deliverable the parent specifies differently. Consistent narrowing (a leaf doing PART of the parent) is NOT a finding.
- CONFLICT RULE (the operator's directive, encoded in the rubric AND the Pass-4 coaching move): the PARENT WINS — it holds the full delivery context. Coaching directs the agent to realign the leaf to the parent; if the agent believes the parent is genuinely wrong, the productive move is to update the PARENT first (which stales the parent's own attestation and forces its re-review) and only then re-review the leaf — never to silently diverge the leaf.
- CONTEXT ASSEMBLY — NO new context plumbing (the ticket's original intent, verified against the code): G7 is `exec: AGENT`, and the pydantic_ai runner provides the read-only `show_ticket` tool to EVERY AGENT criterion (src/rebar/llm/pai_tools.py — "`show_ticket` always"; the completion verifier reads tickets the same way, completion.py:18). So G7's agent FETCHES the parent at runtime: its rubric instructs it to call `show_ticket(<parent_id>)` (and optionally the grandparent) and compare the leaf's scope against the parent's plan text. The parent_id is ALREADY in the agent's context: `pass1._ticket_graph_blob` (pass1.py:281) emits a `parent: <parent_id>` line from `ctx.state['parent_id']` (pass1.py:288). So G7's agent reads that parent_id from its ticket-graph context and calls `show_ticket(parent_id)` ONCE for the parent's plan text (and optionally the grandparent) — one hop, via the always-available `show_ticket` tool, nothing else pre-injected. This means NO `parent_plan` field on PlanContext, NO `_assemble_context_uncached` change, NO `plan_review_verify_inputs`/verifier-prompt slot, NO pass1 finder-context change — the agent reads the parent on demand through its existing tool. To bound cost, the rubric directs the agent to read only the parent (and one grandparent), not the whole subtree.
- Pass-2/3 stay untouched: findings flow through the standard verify -> decide pipeline; severity maps onto the existing plan axes (divergent_implementation is the natural home — a leaf diverging from its parent is exactly a divergent-implementation signal).
- POSTURE: ship `default_posture: advisory` at 0.95 per the ADR-0036 expand-contract house pattern, BUT record fire-rate + adjudication from day one. IN-SESSION deliverable (the promotion-decision PATH, not the decision itself), as a COMMITTED REPO ARTIFACT (CI-verifiable that the file exists, not a one-time ticket comment): a new committed file `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/g7-promotion-path.md` documenting (a) the exact query — a **jq** query over the `plan_review_result_v1` REVIEW_RESULT sidecars (`.schema == "plan_review_result_v1"`), computing G7 fire-rate = (count of sidecars whose `.findings[]` has "G7" in `.criteria` and `.decision` != "dropped") / (count of G7-eligible reviews), and validity from those findings' `.validity` field — (b) the promotion trigger — blocking at the plan-review 0.6–0.7 band once the observation window shows an acceptable false-fire rate — and (c) where the actual promotion decision will be recorded. The actual promotion decision depends on post-deployment field data and is explicitly DEFERRED (out of this ticket's close path; it belongs with the epic's deferred measurement follow-up 72b6-b0d2-2f93-456d).
- NOTE (explicit non-goal here, candidate follow-up): a material PARENT edit does not currently stale the CHILDREN'S plan-review attestations, so drift introduced by editing the epic after leaves are reviewed is only caught on the leaf's next re-review. If the observation window shows parent-edit drift, file a follow-up for parent-edit-propagating staleness in attest.compute_validity.

## Scope
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json (G7 routing entry), src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py (register G7 in CANONICAL_LLM + AGENT_TIER (NOT CODEBASE_GROUNDED — that set is for criteria whose VERIFY re-grounds against CODE; G7 grounds against the parent TICKET via the store. Per registry.py:62-65 the tool capability — filesystem + rebar `show_ticket` — is granted by the prompt's agentic `execution_mode`, i.e. AGENT_TIER membership, NOT by CODEBASE_GROUNDED); add the `require_parent_id` axis to `applies()`), src/rebar/llm/criteria/overlay.py (`_validate_routing_entry` at 190-212 currently ignores unknown applies_at keys — add an EXPLICIT `require_parent_id` bool-type validation block so a non-boolean value raises, rather than silently passing), src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py (`route_criteria()` threads the ticket's has_parent_id fact into `applies()`), src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml (add G7 to the static `criteria:` batch list — criteria ARE enumerated there, so this edit is required), a new reviewers/plan_review_G7.md rubric prompt (instructs the agent to `show_ticket(parent_id)` and apply the parent-wins containment check), the coach MOVE_REGISTRY (one parent-realign move in passes.py), the regenerated docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md, and tests (routing: fires on leaf-with-parent only, skipped for parentless/container/bug/session_log; semantics: fixture parent/leaf pairs — contained/exceeding/contradicting; posture pin; validate_packaged_routing()==[]; a test asserting docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/g7-promotion-path.md exists AND contains the fire-rate query + the literal '0.6'/'0.7' band). NO new context-assembly plumbing (G7 reads the parent via its `show_ticket` tool at runtime) and NO kernel decide.py/verify.py math changes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The criterion runs ONLY on tickets that have a parent_id and no children; a routing test proves it is skipped for parentless tickets, containers, bugs, and session_logs.
- [ ] Fixture-driven tests: a leaf narrowing its parent PASSES; a leaf adding out-of-parent-scope deliverables fires; a leaf contradicting a parent AC fires.
- [ ] The rubric and the Pass-4 coaching move state the parent-wins rule. Proving command: `grep -iE 'parent wins|show_ticket' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_G7.md` returns the parent-wins directive + the show_ticket instruction, and the new parent-realign move appears in passes.py MOVE_REGISTRY (asserted by the MOVE_REGISTRY test).
- [ ] The criterion ships advisory (0.95) with fire-rate observable in REVIEW_RESULT sidecars. In-session proof (a COMMITTED repo file, CI-verifiable): the committed file `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/g7-promotion-path.md` exists AND contains the promotion-decision PATH — the fire-rate/validity jq query (referencing the `plan_review_result_v1` schema and the `.criteria`/`.decision`/`.validity` fields), the literal '0.6' and '0.7' band bounds, and the recording location — proven by a unit test (tests/) that asserts the file exists AND greps it for those anchors (content check, not mere existence). The actual promotion decision is explicitly deferred (see 72b6), not required to close this ticket.
- [ ] G7's rubric instructs the AGENT to read the parent (and optionally one grandparent) via its `show_ticket` tool and compare scope — no new context-assembly plumbing is added; a test confirms G7 is registered in AGENT_TIER (NOT CODEBASE_GROUNDED) — AGENT_TIER membership is what grants the show_ticket tool (registry.py:62-65) — and the rubric references reading the parent ticket via show_ticket.
- [ ] make lint passes on the change.
- [ ] make typecheck passes on the change.
- [ ] make test passes on the change.
finding: The plan specifies the rubric instructs the agent to call `show_ticket(<parent_id>)` and compare scope, but does not specify what the agent should do when the parent ticket is unreadable. The `show_ticket` tool in `pai_tools.py:203-204` returns an error string (not raises) on failure, so the agent loop won't crash — but without explicit rubric guidance the agent may either fire a spurious finding (false positive) or silently pass (false negative) when the parent is unavailable (deleted ticket, permissions, store error).
location: What section — CONTEXT ASSEMBLY paragraph / G7 rubric specification

title: op-cert: AWS IaC + deploy of the trusted gate service (zero fixed-cost: existing VM + API Gateway SigV4) + live authenticated integrity test
plan:
# op-cert: AWS IaC + deploy of the trusted gate service (zero fixed-cost: existing VM + API Gateway SigV4) + live authenticated integrity test

## Why
The trusted gate service (the sibling service story) is only a trust anchor once it runs in the
controlled environment with SigV4-authenticated access and a key nobody else can read. This
story declares that as code, **actually deploys it** (an authenticated AWS CLI is provided — the
deploy is in scope), and proves the deployed endpoint end-to-end. Deployment decisions are
settled in the epic's "Producer-signing & compute-runtime design resolution", **cost-verified
against the live account**: rebar's entire AWS footprint is the one t4g.large `rebar-gerrit`
box (+ Route 53 + free SSM parameters), so the binding constraint is **zero increase in fixed
monthly cost** — the service rides the existing box (the compose slot freed by the autolander's
retirement, ADR 0047) behind an API Gateway HTTP API (pay-per-request, no fixed fee), with the
key in free SSM Parameter Store. A dedicated Fargate/NLB/Secrets-Manager/KMS-CMK stack was
rejected (~$50+/mo new spend for isolation ADR 0046 L4 does not require).

**Hard dependency (not yet on `main`):** the sibling service story provides
`src/rebar/opcert_service/` and `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` — neither exists on `main`
yet; this story starts only after that story lands (enforced by the `depends_on` link).

## What
- **Terraform — extend the existing `infra/terraform/`** (the module that already declares the
  Gerrit EC2, its IAM instance profile, and the data volume):
  - an **API Gateway HTTP API** (v2) whose routes set `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` (SigV4
    — a ROUTE attribute; HTTP API v2 has NO separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource for
    IAM), over TLS. The integration URI is **`https://` to the box's HOST-nginx TLS origin**
    (nginx + certbot run as HOST services on the box — `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` header —
    with TLS on 443 at the Elastic-IP-bound hostname; NOT `http://…:80`, which the existing
    config 301-redirects and would break the integration) for the `/opcert/` routes, injecting a shared origin-guard header. Caller restriction is
    identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy): `AWS_IAM` auth requires SigV4,
    and the IaC CREATES the admin role it restricts to — `aws_iam_role` **`rebar-opcert-admin`**
    (no such role pre-exists in `infra/terraform/`), trust policy limited to the principal ARNs
    supplied via a new variable `opcert_admin_principal_arns` (set at deploy to the operator's
    IAM principals), with an inline policy granting `execute-api:Invoke` scoped to exactly this
    API's execution ARN — and no other principal/policy in the IaC grants Invoke;
  - an **SSM Parameter Store SecureString** `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` (declared with a
    placeholder `value` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`, so the out-of-band-seeded
    key is never reverted or clobbered by a later `terraform apply`) holding the
    **passphrase-free Ed25519 private key**. NO new IAM
    grant: the parameter lives under `/rebar/prod/`, which the instance profile's existing
    `rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read` policy (`infra/terraform/iam.tf`) already covers — and
    nothing broader is added;
  - the **origin-guard header provisioning path**: Terraform generates the guard value with
    `random_password` — the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to
    `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers` (today it pins only `hashicorp/aws
    ~> 5.0`; `terraform init` refreshes `.terraform.lock.hcl`) — stores it as SSM SecureString
    `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` (also under the existing grant), and maps it as a static
    request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration. Unlike the KEY
    parameter, the guard parameter is fully Terraform-managed and carries NO
    `lifecycle ignore_changes` (its value IS `random_password.result`; rotation =
    `terraform apply -replace=` on the random resource, which updates the SSM value and the
    integration header together) — the `ignore_changes = [value]` guard applies ONLY to the
    operator-seeded Ed25519 key parameter;
  - NO new fixed-monthly-cost resources: no Fargate/ECS service, no load balancer/VPC link, no
    Secrets Manager secret, no customer KMS CMK, and no `kms:Sign` anywhere (SSHSIG signing is
    performed by the service itself; AWS KMS has no Ed25519 signing).
- **Compose — extend `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`**: an `opcert` service on
  `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (sibling story) with env `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` and the SSM
  parameter names; an nginx route `/opcert/` → the service that rejects requests whose
  `X-Opcert-Guard` header does not match. On the box the guard value is materialized from its
  SSM parameter by `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` into a HOST-nginx map entry
  (nginx runs as a host service, not a container) with NAMED files and NO install-step
  ownership problem: `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` — which today has NO `/opcert/`
  location — gains, IN THE TEMPLATE ITSELF, the guard map with a fail-closed default and a
  GLOB include (`map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; include
  /etc/nginx/opcert-guard*.conf; }` — nginx accepts a zero-match glob include, unlike a
  missing literal include) plus the `location /opcert/ { if ($opcert_guard_ok = 0) { return
  403; } proxy_pass … }` block routing to the compose service. The DENY-ALL default is
  therefore STRUCTURAL in the template: a fresh boot with no materialized file serves 403 on
  /opcert/ and nginx starts cleanly — no separate default-file install step exists to own.
  `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` writes only the one-line map entry
  `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.map.conf` (`"<value>" 1;`) from the SSM parameter and reloads
  nginx; the guard value also lands in the service env. It is WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator
  `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` alongside the existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls,
  running BEFORE `docker compose up` (the precedent ordering), and it finishes with
  `nginx -s reload` so a guard rotation re-run takes effect (at first boot the reload is a
  no-op-safe refresh). Rotation hazard closed explicitly; (b) ROTATION — the documented
  runbook is two steps in order: `terraform apply -replace=random_password.opcert_guard`
  (updates the SSM value + the API Gateway static header), then re-run
  `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (rewrites the nginx map + `nginx -s reload`); between the two
  steps /opcert/ serves 403 — fail-closed, a brief and acceptable rotation window following the existing
  precedent (`infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh` / `materialize-g2p-config.sh` — SSM →
  file). It is WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` alongside the
  existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls, running BEFORE `docker compose up` (the
  precedent ordering), and it finishes with `nginx -s reload` so a guard rotation re-run takes
  effect on the host nginx (at first boot the reload is a no-op-safe refresh) — the same value Terraform maps onto the API Gateway integration, closing
  the loop.
- **Deploy + prove** (authenticated AWS CLI): `terraform apply`; generate the key locally
  (`ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N ""`) and seed the SecureString (the key exists nowhere else).
  **Key-loss recovery runbook (documented in the story's deploy notes):** if the SSM parameter
  is deleted or state is lost, the trust anchor is NOT recoverable and is not meant to be —
  recovery = mint a NEW keypair, re-seed the parameter, and pin the new public key as a NEW
  era entry in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (`added_at_log_position` = current tickets
  tip) while the lost key's entry is revoked with a grandfather pin at the loss point — certs
  already stored under the lost key stay valid (the era-anchor model), and only new certs
  require the new key. No historical re-certification is needed;
  deploy the compose service on the box; pin the environment's public key + `env_id` into
  `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` per the epic's "Authoritative era anchor" resolution
  (`added_at_log_position`, operator-stamped at the current tickets tip); then the live checks — a
  SigV4-signed job round-trip (submit → poll → terminal status), an unsigned request rejected
  (401/403 at API Gateway), and a direct-to-origin request without the guard header rejected.
## Scope
- In: the Terraform extension, compose/nginx wiring, key generation + SSM seeding procedure,
  the real deploy, the live round-trip evidence, the trusted-env pinning.
- Out: the service code and Dockerfile (sibling story); any change to rebar library code; the
  merge-gate consumer (shipped, 4214); resizing or re-homing the existing box.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `infra/terraform/` declares: the API Gateway HTTP API (v2) with
      `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on its routes over TLS,
      `execute-api:Invoke` granted only to the IaC-created `rebar-opcert-admin` role (trust policy
      from `opcert_admin_principal_arns`; the assertion method is STRUCTURED and SELF-FAILING —
      the literal command the test runs exits non-zero on any violation:
      `terraform plan -out=tf.plan && terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e
      '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type == "aws_iam_role_policy" or
      .type == "aws_iam_policy") | {label: .name, s: (.values.policy | fromjson | .Statement[])}
      | select([.s.Action] | flatten | any(. == "execute-api:Invoke"))] | length == 1 and
      .[0].label == "opcert_admin_invoke"'` — where `.name` in `terraform show -json` is the
      Terraform RESOURCE LABEL, so the invoke policy is declared with the pinned label
      `aws_iam_role_policy.opcert_admin_invoke` on the `rebar-opcert-admin` role; exactly one
      statement grants Invoke and it is that resource, else the command exits non-zero), the `hashicorp/random` provider in `required_providers`,
      the box-origin integration with the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping; and
      the two SecureString parameters (the key parameter guarded by
      `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`)
      (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard`) under the existing
      `rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read` path grant with NO new/broader IAM grant. EVERY AC1 sub-claim is asserted with its own literal `jq -e` query against the one
      `terraform show -json tf.plan` output, each exiting non-zero on violation:
      (a) https URI — `.planned_values.root_module.resources[] |
      select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") | .values.integration_uri |
      startswith("https://")`;
      (b) guard header — `... select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") |
      .values.request_parameters | has("append:header.X-Opcert-Guard")`;
      (c+d) parameters — `[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] |
      select(.type=="aws_ssm_parameter") | {n:.values.name, t:.values.type}] | (map(.n) |
      contains(["/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key","/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard"])) and
      all(.t=="SecureString")`;
      (e) route auth — `[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] |
      select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_route") | .values.authorization_type] | length > 0 and
      all(. == "AWS_IAM")` (aggregated with `all()` — a per-route stream would let `jq -e`
      pass on the last route alone);
      (f) key-param `lifecycle.ignore_changes` — `.configuration.root_module.resources[] |
      select(.address=="aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_ed25519_key") |
      .lifecycle_meta_arguments.ignore_changes? // empty | length > 0` (in `terraform show
      -json`'s `configuration` section lifecycle meta-arguments nest under
      `.lifecycle_meta_arguments`, not `.lifecycle`, and are absent from `planned_values`);
      (g) provider — `.configuration.provider_config | has("random")`.
- [ ] `terraform fmt -check` and `terraform validate` pass (asserted by a test or CI step);
      `terraform plan` against the provided account succeeds.
- [ ] `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` + nginx config gain the `opcert` service and the
      guarded `/opcert/` route; a test asserts the service entry, the env wiring
      (`REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`, parameter names), that the nginx route requires the
      `X-Opcert-Guard` header, and that the materialize script (per the `infra/gerrit/materialize-*.sh`
      precedent) renders the guard value from its SSM parameter — tested hermetically with a
      stub `aws` executable prepended to `PATH` that returns a fixture value (no live SSM or
      account access in unit tests). The test also asserts `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` calls
      the materialize script before `docker compose up`, that the script ends with
      `nginx -s reload` (host nginx), that the TEMPLATE itself contains the fail-closed guard
      map (`default 0;` + the zero-match-safe glob include) and the guarded `/opcert/`
      location, and that the Terraform integration URI uses the `https://` scheme.
- [ ] The deployed environment's public key and `env_id` are pinned in
      `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (with `added_at_log_position` per the epic's SUPERSEDING "Authoritative era anchor" resolution — the tickets-branch log-position era fields, not the superseded `added_at_commit` main-SHA form); a
      test asserts the entry parses and the `env_id` matches the compose service's
      `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`.
- [ ] [operator-attested] The service is deployed from `infra/` via the authenticated AWS CLI
      and the live checks pass: a SigV4-signed job round-trip succeeds; an unsigned request is
      rejected at API Gateway; a direct-to-origin request without the guard header is rejected —
      evidence: a comment recording the `terraform apply` outputs (API endpoint id/URL) and the
      captured round-trip + both rejections.
finding: The plan asserts that API Gateway HTTP API can inject a static `X-Opcert-Guard` request header via the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource, but neither the Terraform attribute name nor the required key format is cited or verified. The `request_parameters` attribute on `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` uses a non-obvious key syntax (e.g., `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard`) that differs from REST API mapping semantics; a wrong or missing key silently produces a successful `terraform apply` while never injecting the header — causing the origin-guard inner perimeter to be absent with no deploy-time signal. Zero prior art exists for `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` in `infra/terraform/`.
location: What section — 'maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'
suggested_fix: In the IaC spec, name the exact HCL: `resource "aws_apigatewayv2_integration" … { request_parameters = { "append:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result } }` and add to AC1's jq/plan assertion a check that a `request_parameters` entry whose key matches `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` is present on the integration resource.

title: WS3: overlay_union + merge_findings scripted ops + gates/code-review.yaml (two-round -> kernel Pass-2/3/4)
plan:
# WS3 — Escalation scripted ops + the code-review workflow

## Why
This is the heart of the gate: wire WS1's base + WS2's overlays into the two-round overlay-escalation topology and feed all findings into the shipped kernel Pass-2/3/4. The mechanism was DERISKED on the real v3 engine (offline) — the agent's `recommend_overlays` drove a second batch's membership, bogus ids dropped, the cap bounded fan-out, one-hop held — so this is implementation of a proven design, with NO core-engine change.

## What
- Two deterministic scripted ops: `overlay_union` (compute (glob U base.recommend) - already-run, ONE-HOP, capped at N=3 configurable; emit per-overlay `include_<overlay>` flags + `to_run`) and `merge_findings` (concatenate base + Round-A + Round-B finding lists).
- The thin `gates/code-review.yaml`: assemble_diff -> triggers (overlay_triggers) -> base (agent) -> round_a (batch, when:=triggers) -> union -> round_b (batch, when:=union) -> merge -> verify (Pass-2) -> decide (Pass-3) -> coach_gate/coach (Pass-4).

## Scope
- `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py` (or register in `workflow/steps.py`) — `overlay_union`, `merge_findings`.
- `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/code-review.yaml` — the gate workflow.
- OUT OF SCOPE: the `gate_dispatch` entry + source-separation (WS4); the secrets/security auto-block detectors (WS5).

## Specifics pinned (plan-review remediation 2026-06)
- **Escalation cannot affect the verdict (explicit invariant):** the offline test asserts that flipping `recommend_overlays` changes only WHICH advisory overlays run (Round-B membership), never the Pass-3 decision for an identical finding set — escalation lives on the recall/selection side; the precision/decision side is deterministic.
- **`overlay_union` config:** the cap N (default 3) is read from `gates/code-review.yaml`, not hard-coded; `merge_findings` preserves per-finding provenance (which reviewer/round emitted it) so Pass-2 can re-ground each.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `overlay_union` computes (glob U recommend) - already-run, ONE-HOP, capped at N=3 (configurable), enum-constrained; `merge_findings` concatenates the three finding sources.
- [ ] `gates/code-review.yaml` validates (`rebar workflow validate`) and runs on the v3 engine; Round-A overlays are glob-triggered, Round-B membership = the escalated union, all findings flow into kernel Pass-2/3/4.
- [ ] An OFFLINE (FakeRunner) test asserts: Round-B membership == the escalated set; the cap bounds fan-out; one-hop holds (overlays do not re-escalate); escalation can never change the deterministic verdict.
- [ ] The gate calls the kernel `verify_findings` / `pass3_over_findings` / `coach` directly (no forked passes).

## Plan-review remediation (Round-11, 2026-06-29)
- `merge_findings` CLUSTERS near-duplicate findings (reuse `aggregate.py` clustering by file-anchor + dimension + line-proximity) BEFORE Pass-2, so N+1 overlays reviewing the same location don't quadratically inflate the verifier token budget.
- `overlay_union` receives `already_run` EXPLICITLY via its `with:` block, sourced from the Round-A triggers step output — not reconstructed from internal state.
- `gates/code-review.yaml` operator-facing config (thresholds, cap N, escalation shape) gets a DOCUMENTED schema (fields/types/defaults/valid ranges).
- An ADR documents the base→overlay one-hop ESCALATION design (union op, cap rationale, recall-only/never-verdict-side) — not only the single-pass retirement ADR.

Added ACs:
- [ ] `merge_findings` dedups/clusters near-duplicate findings (file-anchor+dimension+line-proximity) before Pass-2; test asserts N overlays on one location collapse
- [ ] `overlay_union` reads `already_run` from an explicit `with:` input wired to the Round-A triggers output; test asserts the wiring
- [ ] `gates/code-review.yaml` config schema documented (fields/types/defaults/ranges)
- [ ] ADR records the one-hop base→overlay escalation design (union/cap/recall-only)
finding: The offline (FakeAgentRunner) test required by AC3 depends on the base agent step producing both `findings` AND `recommend_overlays` as separately-referenceable named outputs, but FakeAgentRunner (runners.py:44-54) has no mode that produces this dual-output shape — the `else` branch for unknown modes returns `{result: {}, _fake: True}`, so overlay_union would receive no recommend_overlays and the AC would silently pass with an empty escalation set rather than exercising real escalation wiring.
location: AC3 (offline FakeRunner test) + src/rebar/llm/workflow/runners.py:44-54
suggested_fix: In FakeAgentRunner.run(), add: `elif mode == 'structured_overlay': outputs = {'findings': [], 'recommend_overlays': [], 'summary': f'[fake] {ctx.step_id}', '_fake': True}` — or define a PlannedTraceRunner override in the test that injects a canned base-step output with a non-empty recommend_overlays list.

title: WS6: Rewire the Gerrit review-bot to the four-pass code review
plan:
# WS6 — Rewire the Gerrit review-bot to the four-pass code review

## Why
Epic `d251` (AWS Gerrit + rebar MCP) **shipped and merged** — including S4b's complete review-bot:
`src/rebar/review_bot/` (receiver `app.py`, `voter.py`, `gerrit_client.py`, `dedup.py`,
`reconcile.py`) plus the **verdict→label seam `adapter.code_review_decision(diff_text, repo_root,
ref) → {decision, message, findings}`**. That seam is currently implemented over the OLD
single-pass `rebar.llm.review_code(...)` + a blocking-severity heuristic — a deliberate "proven
pipe" placeholder. `adapter.py`'s own docstring says: *"b744-WS6 will REIMPLEMENT this same
signature over `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` — drop-in, with no caller change."*

WS6 is exactly that swap: make Gerrit's `LLM-Review` vote driven by **our new four-pass code
review** (the deterministic Pass-3 blocker), not the single-pass demo.

## Realignment (2026-06-30) — supersedes the earlier standalone-package plan
The pre-merge WS6 plan (a NEW `src/rebar/gerrit/` voter package + REST client + patchset handler)
is **retired as redundant**: d251 already ships all of that in `src/rebar/review_bot/`. WS6 must
NOT build a parallel package — it CONSUMES the shipped receiver and swaps only the decision engine
behind the existing seam. The live dogfood is split out to WS6-b (`0da5`); the GitHub cutover to
WS7.

## What
- **Rewrite `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py`'s `code_review_decision`** over
  `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` (the typed `PASS`/`BLOCK`/`INDETERMINATE` verdict):
  - keep the signature + return shape (`{decision, message, findings}`, plus an additive
    `coverage_gap` flag) so `voter.py` is unchanged;
  - map the verdict fail-closed (below); the four-pass gate's own Pass-3 thresholds
    (`criteria_routing.json`) decide PASS vs BLOCK, so the adapter no longer applies the
    `blocking_severities` severity heuristic (it becomes vestigial for this path);
  - review the receiver's cloned working tree by passing `repo_root` (the security detectors scan
    the changed files there) + the fetched `diff_text`.
- **Add an `enabled: bool | None = None` override to `produce_code_review_verdict`** so the adapter
  can pass `enabled=True` (force-enable): the code-review gate is OFF by default
  (`verify.enable_code_review`), but voter activation is itself the authoritative gate, so the
  per-repo flag would otherwise leave every change with the inert disabled verdict. (ADR 0015.)
- **Update `tests/unit/test_review_bot.py`** adapter tests to drive the four-pass verdict (mock
  `produce_code_review_verdict`) instead of `review_code`.
- **ADR 0015** records the seam reimplementation + the force-enable rationale + the fail-closed
  vote mapping + the coverage-gap message distinction.

## Vote mapping (fail-closed; the gate's Pass-3 owns PASS/BLOCK)
`produce_code_review_verdict` emits three verdicts (PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE) plus an inert
disabled verdict. The adapter collapses them to the receiver's PASS/BLOCK (the voter then casts
`llm_review_max_value` / `llm_review_block_value` from `ReceiverConfig`):

| verdict / state | adapter decision | note |
|---|---|---|
| PASS + full coverage | PASS | advisory findings are surfaced in the message, non-blocking |
| BLOCK (blocking findings) | BLOCK | real finding (`coverage_gap=False`) |
| INDETERMINATE (LLM outage) | BLOCK | fail-closed coverage-gap |
| PASS but `coverage.enabled==false` (inert) | BLOCK | fail-closed coverage-gap (`gate-disabled`) — defense-in-depth |
| fail-closed security-scanner abstain | BLOCK | fail-closed coverage-gap (`scanner`) |
| ANY exception / bad shape | BLOCK | fail-closed coverage-gap (`review-error`) |

## Distinct coverage-gap message (infra vs code)
The vote comment (`ReviewInput.message`, posted by the receiver's `post_vote`) begins with a
machine-parseable tag so an INFRA `-1` is unmistakable from a real-finding `-1`:
`[LLM-Review: PASS]`, `[LLM-Review: BLOCK — finding]`, and
`[LLM-Review: BLOCK — coverage-gap (<gate-disabled|llm-unavailable|scanner|review-error>)]`. The
`coverage_gap` sub-reason is derived deterministically from `verdict.coverage`
(`enabled==false` → gate-disabled; `llm_unavailable` → llm-unavailable; a `security_detectors`
note with `reason=='fail-closed-abstain'` → scanner). A real secret MATCH
(`reason=='detector-finding'`) is a finding, NOT a coverage gap.

## Scope
- IN: the `adapter.py` reimplementation, the `produce_code_review_verdict` `enabled` override, the
  updated `test_review_bot.py` adapter tests, ADR 0015.
- OUT: the receiver/voter/gerrit_client/reconciler (d251 shipped them — unchanged); the live
  end-to-end dogfood (WS6-b, gated on the deployed env); the GitHub cutover (WS7).

## Dependencies
- CONSUMES d251's shipped `review_bot` package + the provisioned `LLM-Review` label / value
  mapping (`ReceiverConfig`). depends_on WS4 (98ff, closed) for `produce_code_review_verdict`.
- No live env needed: the adapter is fully verifiable offline (mock the verdict; the receiver's
  own tests already stub Gerrit).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `review_bot/adapter.py`'s `code_review_decision` is reimplemented over `produce_code_review_verdict` with `enabled=True`; the signature + `{decision, message, findings}` return shape are unchanged (the voter is not touched).
- [ ] Vote mapping is exhaustive + fail-closed over PASS / BLOCK / INDETERMINATE / inert-disabled / scanner-abstain / exception; a test asserts each → the correct decision, and that an inert-disabled verdict never yields PASS.
- [ ] `produce_code_review_verdict` gains an `enabled: bool | None = None` override (default None = read config); a test asserts `enabled=True` forces the gate to run even when `verify.enable_code_review=false`.
- [ ] The vote message distinguishes a coverage-gap `-1` (sub-reason gate-disabled / llm-unavailable / scanner / review-error) from a real-finding `-1`, via the documented first-line tag; a test asserts the PASS tag, the finding tag, and each coverage-gap sub-reason.
- [ ] `tests/unit/test_review_bot.py` adapter tests are updated to the four-pass verdict (mock `produce_code_review_verdict`); the full `pytest -k review_bot` suite passes; ruff + mypy clean.
- [ ] ADR 0015 records the seam reimplementation, the force-enable rationale, the fail-closed vote mapping, and the coverage-gap message vocabulary.
finding: The plan requires the patchset handler to 'assemble the change diff' (AC3) but names no Gerrit REST endpoint for fetching it. Gerrit offers at least three diff surfaces with incompatible formats: GET /changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/patch (base64-encoded unified patch), GET /changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/files/{path}/diff (per-file JSON diff), and GET /changes/{id}/patch (shorthand). The wrong choice produces a diff format incompatible with produce_code_review_verdict's diff_text parameter.
location: AC 3 / 'What' section / REST vote failure behavior section

title: Scope-intent overlay (diff vs union of trailer tickets)
plan:
## Why
Nothing currently checks the DIFF against its ticket. plan-review checks the plan; completion-verifier
checks at close; a diff-time scope check is complementary and catches scope drift in the change itself.

## What
New scope-intent overlay, content-triggered when >=1 ticket resolves from the commit's rebar-ticket:
trailer. Grounded prerequisites from plan review:
- REGISTER `scope-intent` in the closed OVERLAY_IDS tuple in registry.py (unknown ids are dropped).
- assemble_diff (assemble.py) does NOT currently read the rebar-ticket: trailer; add trailer parsing via
  extract_ticket_refs (supports multiple ids: multiple trailer lines AND whitespace-separated tokens) and
  assemble the referenced tickets' scope/AC as this overlay's context.
- Ticket-blindness is NOT free: ticket_context is threaded into the shared reviewer_input, so keeping base
  + other overlays ticket-blind requires explicit plumbing (gate ticket context to the scope-intent
  overlay only; do not leak it to the others). Verify with a test.
Checks the diff against the UNION of all referenced tickets so legitimate multi-ticket commits don't
produce false scope findings. Reuses plan-review's scope-intent criterion vocabulary. Advisory.

## Scope
reviewers/code-review-scope-intent.md, registry.py (OVERLAY_IDS + content trigger), assemble.py (trailer
read + ticket-context assembly), criteria_routing.json (SHARED — coordinate with C, F).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `scope-intent` in OVERLAY_IDS; overlay fires only when a ticket resolves from the trailer.
- [ ] assemble_diff reads the trailer (all ids) and assembles union scope context.
- [ ] A test proves base + other overlays receive NO ticket context (ticket-blind).
- [ ] A multi-ticket commit naming all its tickets produces no false scope-drift finding (eval).
- [ ] Overlay is advisory.

## Data-flow: commit message -> scope-intent overlay (resolves plan-review G6)
The trailer lives in the COMMIT MESSAGE, which is not part of the diff text, so it must be plumbed
end-to-end (default "" everywhere → overlay simply does not fire when absent, per the AC):
- `assemble.py`: `assemble_diff_context(...)` gains `commit_message: str = ""`. It calls
  `extract_ticket_refs(commit_message)`; for each resolved trailer ticket it loads that ticket's
  scope/AC and assembles a UNION scope-context string, exposed as a SEPARATE context field consumed
  ONLY by the scope-intent overlay (base + other overlays stay ticket-blind — assert this).
- `assemble_diff_input` schema: add optional `commit_message`.
- `gates/code-review.yaml`: thread `commit_message` into the `assemble_diff` step; the scope-intent
  overlay's `ticket_context` includes the union scope-context.
- `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict(...)`: accept + forward `commit_message` (default "").
- Call sites (`shim.review_code`, `review_bot/voter.py`): pass the change's commit message when
  available (Gerrit change commit body), default "" otherwise.
scope-intent is content-triggered (fires only when ≥1 trailer ticket resolves) and ADVISORY.

## Per-overlay context injection (resolves plan-review G6, batch-runner tension)
CodeReviewBatchRunner today injects one shared `_context` (the diff) into every overlay
(`batch_runner.py:58`). scope-intent needs a DIFFERENT ticket_context (the union ticket scope). Resolve
by extending the batch runner, not by polluting the shared context:
- `CodeReviewBatchRunner.__init__` gains `context_overrides: dict[str, str] | None = None` (prompt_id ->
  ticket_context). In `run`, when an overlay's `prompt_id` is in `context_overrides`, inject that string
  as its `ticket_context`; every other overlay keeps the shared diff `_context` (base + others stay
  ticket-blind — assert it in a test).
- `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` builds `context_overrides={"code-review-scope-intent":
  <union_scope_context>}` ONLY when the commit-message trailers resolve ≥1 ticket (else omit → overlay
  inert), using the union-scope string assembled in assemble.py.
This is additive and default-None, so existing single-context behaviour is unchanged.
finding: The plan asserts 'ticket_context is threaded into the shared reviewer_input, so keeping base + other overlays ticket-blind requires explicit plumbing' — but the plan never calls out that `CodeReviewBatchRunner.run()` is an INDEPENDENT injection site that also supplies `ticket_context` uniformly to every overlay (batch_runner.py line 58: `inputs={"ticket_context": self._context, ...}`). Achieving per-overlay context isolation requires modifying `CodeReviewBatchRunner`'s interface (e.g., a per-overlay context override map), which the plan does not name as a required change.
location: Data-flow section / What section ('gate ticket context to the scope-intent overlay only') / src/rebar/llm/code_review/batch_runner.py line 58
suggested_fix: Add `CodeReviewBatchRunner.__init__` an optional `overlay_context_overrides: dict[str, str] = {}` parameter; in `run()`, replace `self._context` with `overlay_context_overrides.get(prompt_id, self._context)`. The scope-intent overlay's prompt_id would be mapped to the ticket union context. `gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` constructs the runner with this override populated only when trailer tickets resolve.

title: Persist a code_review artifact for local rebar review-code + session keying (uuid fallback)
plan:
## Why
`rebar review-code` (the local/coding-agent path) reviews a diff but persists NOTHING — only the
Gerrit voter emits a `code_review` artifact. Without a local artifact there is no per-session memory
for the region-gated novelty floor (blameless-grindable-noctule) to converge against. This story
makes local `review-code` emit a session-keyed `code_review` artifact, reusing the payload/reader
shape from revenued-thickset-dassie.

## What
- **Resolve the local memory key.** Use the centralized
  `rebar._commands.session_id.resolve_session_id()` (precedence: `REBAR_SESSION_ID` →
  `CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` → `OPENCODE_SESSION_ID` → `SESSION_ID`; validated shared-across-subagents,
  unique-per-parallel-session).
  - **Fallback (approach A):** when it returns `None` (bare/headless, no SessionStart shim), mint a
    per-invocation `uuid4` (do NOT persist a shared file). This guarantees ISOLATION — no
    local→Gerrit bleed and no cross-session contamination — at the cost of making local convergence
    intentionally INERT for a bare invocation (each run gets a fresh key, so the reader finds no
    prior). Genuine per-session convergence arrives wherever a session lifecycle exists (native
    harness var or a SessionStart shim exporting `REBAR_SESSION_ID`).
- **Set `session_id` on the verdict.** The local path sets `verdict["session_id"] = session_key` so
  the payload (revenued-thickset-dassie's `build_payload`, which reads `verdict.get("session_id")`)
  carries it top-level. The typed memory key for the reader is `session:<session_key>`.
- **Wire the local gate entry to emit + persist.** Thread the resolved key down the local call chain
  `_review_code → shim.review_code → gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict` and emit a session-keyed
  artifact:
  - **Signature changes (in scope):** add `target_ticket: str | None = None` and
    `session_id: str | None = None` parameters to `code_review/shim.py::review_code(...)`, AND add a
    `session_id: str | None = None` field to the `CodeReviewRequest` dataclass in `gate_dispatch.py`
    (alongside the existing `target_ticket`). The shim forwards both onto
    `CodeReviewRequest(target_ticket=…, session_id=…, …)`. Without these the resolved key cannot
    reach the emit path.
  - **Create-or-reuse idempotency (session key = stable convergence point).** Before emitting, a
    local-emit helper resolves ONE artifact ticket per session key, mirroring
    `voter.emit_code_review_artifact` (voter.py ~199–220) EXACTLY:
    `title = f"code-review: session:{session_key}"`; scan
    `rebar.list_tickets(ticket_type="code_review", repo_root=repo_root)` for a title match and REUSE
    that artifact id; else `rebar.create_ticket("code_review", title, …)`. Then `sidecar.emit(verdict,
    target_ticket=artifact_id, …)`. So two sequential local reviews under one session id append
    sidecar events to the SAME artifact (AC4) instead of duplicating.
  - **`relates_to` link (best-effort).** Link the artifact `relates_to` the work ticket resolved
    from the head commit's `rebar-ticket:` trailer (via the existing `extract_ticket_refs` +
    `resolve_ticket_id`, exactly as `voter.emit_code_review_artifact` does) for searchability. No new
    `--ticket` CLI flag is added (out of scope). A trailerless/unresolved/ticketless review STILL
    persists (memory is keyed by session, not by ticket) — the only degradation is no cross-run
    match when the key differs; a missing/unresolved link is logged, never fails the review.
  - **Best-effort throughout:** artifact create/reuse/emit/link failures are logged and swallowed —
    they must NEVER fail the review (mirrors the voter + sidecar posture).

## Scope
- `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` (`_review_code`: resolve key, thread it down),
  `src/rebar/llm/code_review/shim.py` (`review_code`: add `target_ticket`/`session_id` params),
  `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` (local-emit helper: create-or-reuse the session artifact
  + set `verdict["session_id"]`).
- Depends on the payload shape + reader (revenued-thickset-dassie) — specifically `build_payload`
  reading `verdict.get("session_id")`, which lands with c639; this story is reviewed/implemented
  AFTER c639 is committed on the stack, so that dependency exists in the tree.
- OUT of scope: the Gerrit voter path (already emits; inherits the payload extension); the
  region_gated_floor consumer (blameless-grindable-noctule).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Local `rebar review-code` emits a `code_review` artifact keyed `session:<id>` carrying the
      reviewed-file `deps` map and a top-level `session_id`.
- [ ] The session key = `resolve_session_id()`; when `None`, a per-invocation `uuid4` is used (no
      shared-file persistence), making bare-invocation local convergence intentionally inert.
- [ ] `shim.review_code` gains `target_ticket` + `session_id` parameters and `CodeReviewRequest`
      gains a `session_id` field, both forwarded, so the local path can supply the artifact anchor
      and session key.
- [ ] Create-or-reuse idempotency: two sequential local reviews under ONE session id append to the
      SAME artifact (title `code-review: session:{id}`), not duplicate tickets.
- [ ] The artifact `relates_to`-links the ticket resolved from the head commit's `rebar-ticket:`
      trailer when one resolves; a trailerless/unresolved review still persists (keyed by session)
      and never fails the review (no new `--ticket` flag).
- [ ] Tests: a local review with a set `REBAR_SESSION_ID` emits/reuses one session-keyed artifact
      across two runs; a bare review (no session var) mints a uuid key and still persists; artifact
      failures never fail the review.
finding: The plan's local-emit control flow is mechanically ambiguous: placing the 'local-emit helper' (create-or-reuse artifact + sidecar.emit) inside gate_dispatch.produce_code_review_verdict/gate_dispatch.py creates a conflict with the existing `if request.target_ticket: sidecar.emit(...)` block in `_run_code_review_gate`. The plan does not specify whether the helper replaces that existing conditional block (for the local path) or fires in addition to it, nor does it state what happens when both `session_id` (local path) and `target_ticket` (Gerrit voter path) are set simultaneously. Without this clarification an implementer may produce a double-emit on the Gerrit path or miss the emit on the local path.
location: What section / 'Wire the local gate entry to emit + persist' bullet; gate_dispatch.py _run_code_review_gate lines 592-595
suggested_fix: Unify the emit dispatch in `_run_code_review_gate`: replace the existing `if request.target_ticket:` block with a single dispatch that checks `request.session_id` first (runs create-or-reuse helper, then emits to session artifact), else falls through to `request.target_ticket` (Gerrit path). These are mutually exclusive paths (local vs Gerrit voter) so mutual exclusion should be asserted or at minimum documented.

title: Claude Code SessionStart hook shim to populate REBAR_SESSION_ID in the local CLI
plan:
## Why
`CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` is exposed as an env var ONLY in the remote/cloud runner; the LOCAL
Claude Code CLI does not export it. Without a shim the session-provenance feature silently
no-ops for local-CLI users — the most common case. The robust, harness-agnostic capture is a
Claude Code SessionStart hook that reads the hook's `session_id` from stdin and exports it as
the portable `REBAR_SESSION_ID`, plus the harness tag `AI_AGENT=claude-code`.

## What
- **Hook script (`scripts/session-shims/claude-code-session-start.sh`).** Reads the
  SessionStart JSON envelope from stdin (field `session_id`) and, when `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` is
  set, APPENDS `export REBAR_SESSION_ID=<id>` and `export AI_AGENT=claude-code` to it. These
  are the Claude Code hook-contract facts — the SessionStart stdin `session_id` field and the
  `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` env-injection file — per the **Claude Code hooks reference**
  (https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks), cited in the script header + docs page. GRACEFUL
  DEGRADATION: this is the only external-API coupling, and if a future Claude Code renames the
  field/var or changes the mechanism, the shim simply no-ops (no `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` / unparsed
  `session_id` → exit 0) — it never errors or blocks the session, so a wrong assumption fails
  safe. To make that fail-safe OBSERVABLE (not silently inert), the docs ship a **manual
  verification procedure** (`echo "$REBAR_SESSION_ID"` in a fresh session; claim + `show` and
  check `claimed_session`/`claim_harness`) so an operator can confirm the hook is actually
  wired and diagnose an env-mechanism mismatch. This external-integration coupling is
  irreducible — a shim MUST name the harness's env-injection var — and is grounded in the
  cited official reference plus that verification step. The harness tag is the base name
  `claude-code` (the `AI_AGENT` vocabulary permits an optional `_<version>` suffix, but the
  local hook has no reliable version source, so it emits the bare base name). Uses `python3`
  (the interpreter rebar itself runs on — a system prerequisite, `requires-python >= 3.11`, not
  a packaged dependency; if it is somehow absent on PATH the parse yields empty and the hook
  no-ops) to parse the JSON
  robustly — any parse failure, a missing/blank `session_id`, or an absent `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` is
  a silent no-op with exit 0 (fire-and-forget; NEVER blocks the session). Idempotent: re-firing
  on resume/clear/compact first STRIPS this hook's own prior `REBAR_SESSION_ID`/`AI_AGENT`
  exports from the env file (preserving other hooks' lines) then re-writes the current values,
  so the file never accumulates duplicate/stale lines and always reflects the CURRENT session
  (whose id may differ across resume per gotcha G1 — not "the same value").
- **Vocabulary alignment.** Update the `claude-code_<ver>` examples in `docs/config.md`,
  `docs/event-schema.md`, and the `session_id.py` comment to present the harness tag as a base
  name with an OPTIONAL `_<version>` suffix, so bare `claude-code` is consistent with the
  documented vocabulary.
- **Docs (`docs/session-id-shims.md`).** A new install guide: the `settings.json`
  `hooks.SessionStart` snippet pointing at the script, the caveat that the id is NOT stable
  across `--resume`/`--continue`/fork/subagents (epic gotcha G1 — treat it as "the session
  that emitted the claim event"), and guidance to keep it session-scoped (do NOT export
  `REBAR_SESSION_ID` from a shell profile — gotcha G3). Cross-links `docs/config.md`.

## Scope
- IN: the Claude Code SessionStart hook script; the `docs/session-id-shims.md` install page;
  the small vocabulary-alignment edits (`docs/config.md`, `docs/event-schema.md`,
  `session_id.py`); and a NEW `tests/unit/test_session_shims.py` validating the script (valid
  shell + env-file output for mock stdin, malformed JSON, idempotent re-fire, unwritable-file,
  and no-op cases) AND asserting the base-name vocabulary form across all three doc/comment
  targets.
- OUT: Codex/Cursor shims (S6 / 7656, which extend the same docs page); the record/read core
  (S1-S3, S5).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `scripts/session-shims/claude-code-session-start.sh` exists and is valid shell. Proof:
      `bash -n scripts/session-shims/claude-code-session-start.sh` (asserted in
      `tests/unit/test_session_shims.py`).
- [ ] Given stdin `{"session_id":"abc123"}` and a `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` temp path, the script
      writes `export REBAR_SESSION_ID=abc123` AND `export AI_AGENT=claude-code` to that file.
      Proof: `pytest tests/unit/test_session_shims.py -k claude_code_exports -q`.
- [ ] Robust no-op (exit 0, never blocks the session) for each of: a blank/missing
      `session_id`, MALFORMED/unparseable JSON on stdin, an unset `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE`, AND an
      UNWRITABLE `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` (path under a non-existent dir). Proof:
      `pytest tests/unit/test_session_shims.py -k "noop or malformed or unwritable" -q`.
- [ ] `docs/session-id-shims.md` includes a "Verifying the hook is wired" section (the

title: performance overlay enrichment
plan:

Enrich the performance overlay prompt with dso content: recall patterns AND FP-guards (both halves), per the
locked principle that domain rubric lives in the overlay, not the generic Pass-2 verifier. Recall: 8 AI-advantaged concerns (N+1 in loops, unbounded accumulation, blocking ops in async, over-fetch, cache stampede, needless materialization, pool misuse, non-linear complexity on user input). Bright-line reasoning (breaks-under-load / worse-with-scale) recorded as EVIDENCE for Pass-2 to score, not as severity.

## Files
- src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-performance.md
- eval spec + fixtures for the performance overlay (recall + no-fire cases)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Lands with a passing recall + no-fire eval; does not over-suppress valid patterns.
- [ ] Overlay remains advisory; no domain rubric leaks into code-review-verify.md.

title: Code-review near-term nit reduction: suppress docs + llm-prompts advisory noise
plan:
# Code-review near-term nit reduction: suppress docs + llm-prompts advisory noise

## Why
Code-review advisory volume is ~5 findings/review, dominated by `docs` and `llm-prompts` nits
(0/9 high-value in the dogfooded adjudication). That noise buries the findings that matter and
trains reviewers to skim. We want less nit friction on the two lowest-value advisory surfaces
without touching the blocking posture (everything ships advisory in v1).

## Scope note — the landmine detector is split out
This story ORIGINALLY also bundled a grounding-assisted "new-behavior-without-test" detector.
Investigation established that detector needs a BESPOKE seam that does not exist yet (code-review
DET detectors are Engine-B pattern rules that scan code, not diff-parse + cross-file test
correlation; `resolve.extract_references_from_diff` is imports-only, not added-definition
extraction). Building that seam is disproportionate to this near-term nit-reduction change, so it
is SPLIT OUT to the follow-up idea `acoustic-coveting-turaco` (linked `discovered_from` this
story). This story now scopes to the feasible, self-contained down-rank half only.

## What
Introduce a `nit_suppressed` routing flag on the `docs` and `llm-prompts` code-review overlays in
`src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json`, and honour it in the deterministic Pass-3
decide (`code_review_decide`): AFTER `pass3_over_findings` returns the decided findings, the
partition loop re-buckets any finding whose `decision == "advisory"` and whose criteria are ALL
nit-suppressed into `dropped` INSTEAD of `surfaced`, overwriting its `reason` field with the
canonical string `"nit-suppressed"` (a NEW value in `code_review_decide`'s partition, alongside
the kernel `decide.py` reason vocabulary `no-verification` / `veto:cited-reference-inaccurate` /
`low-validity` / `default-advisory` — the kernel is NOT modified; the tag is applied gate-side).
The demotion is POST-pass3 (it changes only the surfaced/dropped partition, never validity/impact/
priority or the block decision). Blocking posture is untouched — the flag only affects advisory
surfacing — and a finding that ALSO maps to a non-suppressed dimension still surfaces (all-criteria
rule). Because the demotion runs before the decide op emits `surfaced`, the downstream
`code_review_coach_inputs` / `code_review_coach` steps (which consume `surfaced`) naturally receive
the reduced set — no coach change is needed.

## Scope / files
- `src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json` — add `"nit_suppressed": true` to the `docs`
  and `llm-prompts` entries (both stay `blocking_enabled: false`).
- `src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py` — a `nit_suppressed_criteria(routing_map=None)` helper
  returning the set of criteria flagged `nit_suppressed` (overlay-aware, mirroring
  `threshold_for` / `effective_routing`).
- `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py` — in `code_review_decide`, demote an advisory
  finding whose criteria are ALL nit-suppressed to `dropped`.
- `tests/unit/test_code_review_nit_routing.py` — the routing + decide-partition tests below.

No LLM-priority-threshold or blocking change here (blocking calibration is the rollout child
`raptorial-galloping-dragon`); this is advisory-surfacing only.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The packaged `src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json` marks BOTH `docs` and
      `llm-prompts` with `"nit_suppressed": true` AND keeps them `"blocking_enabled": false`; no
      other advisory overlay (e.g. `security`, `tests`, `performance`) is `nit_suppressed`. A unit
      test asserts this over the packaged routing.
- [ ] `code_review_decide` DEMOTES an advisory finding whose criteria are ALL nit-suppressed from
      `surfaced` to `dropped`, setting its `reason` to the canonical `"nit-suppressed"`; a finding
      mapping to a nit-suppressed AND a non-suppressed criterion still surfaces; a non-suppressed
      advisory finding still surfaces; and a BLOCK is never demoted. Unit-tested.
- [ ] The `nit_suppressed_criteria` registry helper reads the flag from the routing index
      (overlay-aware) and returns `{docs, llm-prompts}` for the packaged routing.
- [ ] Proving command: `.venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_code_review_nit_routing.py -v`.
- [ ] NO detector code ships in this story: the diff touches only `criteria_routing.json`,
      `registry.py` (the helper), `workflow_ops.py` (the partition demotion), and the test — no
      `detectors.py` change and no `new_behavior_without_test` symbol anywhere. Verifiable:
      `grep -rn new_behavior_without_test src/ tests/` returns NOTHING. (The detector itself is
      split to the `discovered_from` follow-up `acoustic-coveting-turaco`, per the Scope note.)
finding: The plan names `grep-ast` as a current grounding oracle backend alongside `ast-grep` and `ctags` ('ast-grep / ctags / grep-ast, per docs/grounding.md'). The codebase spike experiment (docs/experiments/code-grounding-spike/README.md) definitively established that grep-ast is NOT a repo-wide indexer and was retired from the design — the actual backends are universal-ctags (T1 refutation, resolve.py) and ast-grep/opengrep (Engine B, engine_b.py). Naming a non-existent backend causes the implementer to attempt to use or depend on a tool the architecture has already rejected.
location: Detector mechanism section: 'ast-grep / ctags / grep-ast, per docs/grounding.md'
suggested_fix: Change 'ast-grep / ctags / grep-ast, per docs/grounding.md' to 'universal-ctags (T1 index, resolve.py) / ast-grep (Engine B structural, engine_b.py), per docs/grounding.md — grep-ast was retired (spike RESULT 1)'.

title: Give the completion gate a public context contract (promote operations._assemble_context)
plan:
## Finding (SC1 — verified)
The mandatory completion-verification gate imports a PRIVATE sibling function across a module boundary with no public contract, so refactoring `operations` can silently break the gate.

Citations: `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_ops.py:90-92` (`from rebar.llm import operations` then `operations._assemble_context(...)`); def private at `src/rebar/llm/operations.py:69`. (A prior identical leak — `completion._child_closure_findings` etc. — was fixed by promoting to public names; this one was missed.)

## Remediation
Promote `_assemble_context` to a documented public function (rename to `assemble_context` or add a thin public `build_gate_context` wrapper) with a stated return contract; the gate imports the public name.

cascade_flag: private->public rename — sweep other callers of `_assemble_context`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `src/rebar/llm/operations.py` exposes a public, docstring'd context builder
- [ ] `gate_ops.py` imports the public name (no leading-underscore cross-module import)
- [ ] Existing completion-gate behavior unchanged (tests green)
finding: The plan asserts gate_ops.py is the offender for cross-module private _assemble_context usage, but a grep probe reveals review_workflows.py:53 contains an identical cross-module private call (`operations._assemble_context(...)`) that the plan never names — leaving it outside the remediation scope and AC coverage.
location: Plan section: 'cascade_flag: private->public rename — sweep other callers of _assemble_context' / src/rebar/llm/review_workflows.py:53
suggested_fix: In the Remediation section, enumerate all cross-module callers explicitly: gate_ops.py:92 AND review_workflows.py:53. Add a third AC line: '- [ ] `review_workflows.py` imports the public name (no leading-underscore cross-module import)'.

title: Additive, kind-keyed attestations (plan-review + completion + future coexist)
plan:
## Context
A work ticket carries a single attestation slot (`state["signature"]`), written
last-writer-wins by `process_signature` (`src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py`). The
plan-review attestation (claim time) and the completion verdict (close time) share that
slot, so closing destroys the plan-review attestation (bug grumpy-site-beard /
e6a0-ba9b-ae92-43d7). The two are independent proofs and must coexist; we also expect
further attestation kinds and per-ticket CI-gate consumption later.

## What
Add `state["attestations"]`, a map keyed by attestation kind (per-key LWW, mirroring
`process_workflow_run`): additive across kinds, latest within a kind. Kind is derived from
the already-signed `manifest[0]` (`plan-review` / `completion-verifier`), so no
`PAYLOAD_VERSION` bump and existing signatures still verify. Reuse the `SIGNATURE` event
(add an unsigned `data["kind"]` ONLY as a routing hint). Attestations are immutable signed
records; validity for a gate is computed on read via one
`compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind)` dispatcher.

## Kind identity + routing
- The SIGNED `manifest[0]` is AUTHORITATIVE for kind. Parse: the substring before the first
  ":" (e.g. "plan-review: PASS" -> "plan-review").
- `data["kind"]` is only a hint; `process_signature` uses it ONLY if it equals the
  manifest-derived kind, else ignores it (never trust the unsigned envelope over the signed
  manifest). A SIGNATURE event whose manifest is empty/unkindable (e.g. a legacy retired
  blank event) is ignored for routing (cannot key it) — its staleness is now handled by
  validity-on-read, not by clobbering.

## Compiled-state shape + the legacy mirror
- `process_signature` writes BOTH `state["attestations"][kind] = record` AND keeps
  `state["signature"]` as a LIVE mirror of the MOST-RECENT attestation (last SIGNATURE
  event wins the mirror), in live compiled state — not only in SNAPSHOT output.
- Why the live mirror: four existing consumers read `state.get("signature")` directly
  (`signing.verify_signature`, the close-gate path, `fsck`/validate, and the
  soon-removed `retire_attested_pin`); the live mirror means they keep working unchanged
  while `attestations` is wired up, AND it makes the SNAPSHOT/rollback mirror automatic
  (compiled_state already carries `signature`). The record shape is unchanged
  (manifest, algorithm, signature, key_id, head_sha, signed_at, author).
- 114f owns the canonical per-record shape; 2c2d/cc1b/ed26 reference it.

## Migration (expand only; contract deferred) + rollback
- Events keep the UNCHANGED `SIGNATURE` shape (plus the optional hint `data["kind"]`),
  so old code replays them fine.
- Read fold-in: an OLD SNAPSHOT whose `compiled_state` carries only `signature` (no
  `attestations`) is folded into `attestations` on restore (kind from its manifest).
  Raw post-snapshot SIGNATURE events replay normally into the map.
- New SNAPSHOTs carry BOTH `attestations` and the `signature` mirror automatically (the
  reducer maintains the mirror), so a rolled-back / not-yet-upgraded clone still reads one
  attestation. ROLLBACK is therefore safe at any time.
- The "contract" phase (dropping the mirror) is OUT OF SCOPE here; tracked by follow-up
  352b-5097-9971-4dc1, gated on an all-clones-upgraded readiness check.

## Verification surface
- `verify_signature(ticket_id, kind="plan-review")` ALWAYS returns a SINGLE verdict dict;
  `verify_signature_result.schema.json` is UNCHANGED. `kind` defaults to `"plan-review"`,
  so every existing library/MCP/CLI caller that omits a kind keeps single-verdict
  back-compat. Internally it reads `state["attestations"][kind]`, falling back to the
  legacy `state["signature"]` when the map is absent.
- The verify-ALL map is a SEPARATE entry-point `verify_attestations(ticket_id) ->
  {kind: verdict}` (NOT a new return shape of `verify_signature`).
- CLI: `verify-signature <t> --kind K` = single-gate exit code (0 iff certified);
  `verify-signature <t>` (no `--kind`) = human-readable listing via `verify_attestations`
  (exit 0 iff all certified). MCP `verify_signature` stays single (kind defaults to
  plan-review); the full per-kind set is surfaced via `show`/ticket-state (ed26) — no MCP
  schema break.
- Callers migrated to pass an explicit kind: `claim_gate_check`, `drift_refresh_candidate`,
  `refresh_attestation` (attest.py) -> `plan-review`; `_child_closure_findings`
  (completion.py) -> `completion-verifier`.

## Validity model (computed on read; records never mutated)
- `compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind)` centralizes per-kind rules; the
  MATERIAL-CHANGE field set reuses the EXISTING plan-review material fingerprint
  (`orchestrator.material_fingerprint`: description/AC/file_impact/children) as the single
  source — no new separately-owned constant. "Computed on read" means NOT stored/mutated;
  it MAY do I/O (plan-review still performs its filesystem code-drift re-hash).
- DATA SOURCE for "not reopened since": the reducer records `state["last_reopened_at"]` =
  the timestamp of the most recent `closed -> open` transition (detected in `process_status`
  from the pre-update status == "closed" and target == "open"); null if never reopened.
  `compute_validity` checks `signed_at >= last_reopened_at` (or null) purely from compiled
  state.
- completion-verifier valid iff: certified AND status=="closed" AND not-reopened-since AND
  material-fingerprint-unchanged-since `signed_at` (the completion signer records the
  material fingerprint in its manifest, symmetric with plan-review).
- plan-review valid iff: existing freshness (material + filesystem code drift) AND
  not-reopened-since.
- `retire_attested_pin` (the blank-`SIGNATURE`-on-reopen mutation, signing.py) is REMOVED
  with its reopen call (transition.py); reopen invalidation is validity-on-read for both
  kinds. No lifecycle transition mutates any attestation record.

## Presentation (no secret leak)
- `public_state()` (`_present.py`) gets a DEDICATED `attestations` branch (the flat
  `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` cannot express a wildcard kind level): if `out["attestations"]` is a
  dict, replace with `{kind: {k:v for k,v in rec.items() if k != "signature"} for kind, rec
  in out["attestations"].items() if isinstance(rec, dict)}`. The legacy single-`signature`
  one-level hex strip is retained. This is the canonical strip path for all present/future
  kinds.

## Success Criteria
- A ticket simultaneously holds plan-review AND completion attestations; signing one never
  clobbers the other (in `attestations`); re-running a kind replaces only that kind.
- Compaction/SNAPSHOT round-trips all kinds; legacy single-signature tickets and old-clone
  snapshots still read/verify; a new snapshot is readable by old code via the mirror.
- A reopened ticket stops reading as completion-valid AND plan-review-valid (via
  validity-on-read) with no record mutation; re-closing/re-reviewing re-signs the kind.
- No HMAC hex appears in `show`/MCP output for any attestation kind.

## Validation
- Manual dogfood smoke-test: create a ticket, sign a plan-review PASS, sign a
  completion-verifier PASS, `rebar show` shows BOTH kinds (neither overwriting the other,
  no hex); reopen and confirm both read not-valid; `rebar verify-signature --kind K` per
  kind.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `state["attestations"]` (kind-keyed, per-key LWW) added; `process_signature` writes
      `attestations[kind]` AND maintains the live `state["signature"]` most-recent mirror;
      routing uses authoritative `manifest[0]` (data["kind"] only as a validated hint).
- [ ] Signed canonical payload unchanged; every pre-existing signature still certifies.
- [ ] Old-snapshot read fold-in (`signature` -> `attestations`); new SNAPSHOTs carry both
      `attestations` and the mirror; contract deferred to 352b.
- [ ] Reducer records `state["last_reopened_at"]` (most recent closed->open; null if never).
- [ ] `compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind)` centralizes per-kind validity,
      reusing the existing material fingerprint; completion = certified AND closed AND
      not-reopened-since AND material-unchanged-since; plan-review = existing freshness AND
      not-reopened-since.
- [ ] `retire_attested_pin` removed; no transition mutates any attestation record.
- [ ] `verify_signature(ticket_id, kind="plan-review")` returns a single verdict (schema
      unchanged, back-compat default); `verify_attestations(ticket_id)` returns
      `{kind: verdict}`; CLI no-`--kind` lists all (exit 0 iff all certified); claim gate
      reads `plan-review`, close + child-closure read `completion-verifier`.
- [ ] `public_state()` dedicated `attestations` branch strips the `signature` subkey from
      every per-kind record (no hex in show/MCP for any kind); legacy strip retained.
- [ ] `show`/MCP render the `attestations` map (omitted when empty).
- [ ] Tests: coexistence; within-kind replace; compaction round-trip; legacy + new-snapshot
      cross-read; reopen invalidates completion + plan-review validity without mutation;
      material-change invalidates completion; concurrent different-kind signs both survive;
      no HMAC hex in public output.
## compute_validity contract (pinned)
`compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None) -> {"valid": bool,
"reason": str}`. It ASSUMES the attestation record is already HMAC-certified (the caller
runs `verify_signature` first); compute_validity adds ONLY the lifecycle/freshness layer on
top (per-kind: completion = closed AND not-reopened-since AND material-unchanged-since;
plan-review = material + filesystem code-drift AND not-reopened-since). `reason` is a
human-readable explanation when `valid` is False (empty/"certified ..." when True).
Consumers: `claim_gate_check` (plan-review), the close gate + `_child_closure_findings`
(completion-verifier) call it and branch on `.valid`.
## Acceptance Criteria (compute_validity)
- [ ] `compute_validity(...)` returns `{"valid": bool, "reason": str}`; callers HMAC-verify first then branch on `.valid`.
finding: AC line 8 introduces a 'no kind → `{kind: verdict}`' map return from `verify_signature()`, but the existing `verify_signature_cli`, MCP `verify_signature` tool, and `verify_signature_result.schema.json` all expect a single flat verdict dict. The plan says all enumerated callers are migrated to pass explicit `kind=`, but does not address: (a) what the CLI/MCP contract is when `--kind` is omitted (a map is not schema-valid), (b) whether `verify_signature_result.schema.json` needs a new variant schema, or (c) how callers outside the listed six that call `rebar.verify_signature()` without a kind are handled. This is a hidden API surface breakage for the no-kind call path.
location: AC line 8: '`verify_signature(ticket_id, kind=...)` certifies one kind; no kind returns `{kind: verdict}`'; src/rebar/signing.py:443-469; src/rebar/mcp_server.py:505-512

title: T5c security-overlay prompt hygiene: in-plan-asset scope + same-round dedup
plan:
Harden the T5c security-overlay rubric prompt against out-of-scope and duplicate advisory findings, without changing its posture (advisory @ 0.95) or its recall on genuinely in-scope security gaps.

Evidence: T5c produced 288 advisory findings in the last 7 days with zero blocks ever; observed failure shapes include (a) findings about credentials/assets the plan does not touch (e.g. demanding rotation of a Gerrit HTTP password on a plan that only destroys an SSM copy), and (b) same-round near-duplicates (ticket 9e76: two findings both demanding temp-key-file deletion). ADR 0018's trust-boundary scope gate already bounds WHEN T5c fires; this task bounds WHAT it may claim once fired.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_T5c.md gains an explicit in-plan-asset scope rule: a finding must name the plan section/step that creates, modifies, or transmits the asset at issue; assets merely adjacent to the plan (existing credentials, services, or stores the plan does not touch) are out of scope and must not be findings. Oracle: `grep -ni "does not touch\|in-plan" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_T5c.md` returns at least one hit.
- [ ] The prompt gains a same-round dedup rule: one finding per (surface, defect) pair; additional criteria angles on the same pair are folded into that finding's text, not emitted separately. Oracle: `grep -ni "one finding per" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_T5c.md` returns a hit.
- [ ] The derived prompt index is regenerated if the build requires it and the CI prompt-drift gate passes. Oracle: `make lint && make test` green locally; the prompt-index drift check (part of the test suite) passes.
- [ ] No routing change: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json is byte-identical for the T5c entry. Oracle: `git diff --stat -- src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json` is empty.

Files: src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_T5c.md (+ regenerated prompt index if applicable).
finding: AC3 asserts 'the derived prompt index is regenerated if the build requires it' and references a 'prompt-index drift check' as the relevant CI gate. However, plan_review_T5c.md has front-matter category: plan-review-criterion, and the derived prompt index (src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json) is built exclusively from files with category: review (confirmed in prompts.py and prompt_library.py). Editing only the body of T5c.md will never require or affect index.json regeneration. The true relevant CI gate for this change is the criteria_routing.json parity gate (validate_packaged_routing), which checks routing consistency — not the prompt-index drift gate. The plan's regeneration premise is either vacuously true (a no-op) or points at the wrong artifact, which could mislead the implementer.
location: AC3: 'The derived prompt index is regenerated if the build requires it and the CI prompt-drift gate passes.'
suggested_fix: Replace AC3's oracle text with: 'Oracle: `make lint && make test` green locally; the `criteria_routing.json` parity gate (`python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry validate-routing`) passes. Note: `plan_review_T5c.md` is a `plan-review-criterion` file and does NOT appear in the reviewer `index.json`; no prompt-index regeneration is required for a body-only edit.'

title: Adopt the DCO: Gerrit server-side enforcement, contributor docs, agent sign-off policy
plan:
# Adopt the DCO: declarative Gerrit enforcement, contributor docs, agent sign-off policy

## Why

rebar has no inbound-IP mechanism (no DCO, no CLA). Both external reports and peer convergence recommend DCO over CLA: Qt's CLA is the stated reason it cannot accept PRs, and OpenStack replaced its CLA with the DCO for all contributions (TC resolution 2025-05-20, https://governance.openstack.org/tc/resolutions/20250520-replace-the-cla-with-dco-for-all-contributions.html) explicitly to lower the barrier, directing enforcement into Gerrit itself — the server rejects an unsigned push at `refs/for/*` time, zero new infrastructure. Gerrit documents `receive.requireSignedOffBy` as a per-project `project.config` key (https://gerrit-review.googlesource.com/Documentation/config-project-config.html#receive.requireSignedOffBy — inheritable, NOT site-only); live verification below guards against placement surprises anyway.
