finding: The plan states no positive rationale for choosing a new deterministic scorer (`enumerates_all_unmet_criteria`) as the behavioral proof instrument rather than extending the existing llm-judge scorer (`finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded`) with a new multi-gap fixture case.
location: What section, eval case / scorer design paragraph; AC item 2

title: Code-review impact model redesign: two-lane tier-tagged MAX with re-anchored scale
plan:
# Code-review impact model redesign: two-lane tier-tagged MAX with re-anchored scale

## Why
Production-severity axes + the mean structurally mis-measure code-review's maintainability/latent-regression
findings: a feature silently dead in prod (untested wiring) scores 0.415 because prod_impact is 'none' and
reversibility 'easy' drag it down. Landmines and nits overlap so no scalar threshold separates them.

## What
A code-review-specific, verifier-embedded impact model (`decide.impact_code`) dispatched per-gate off the
shared `decide.pass3_decide` via the `impact_fn` param (that param ALREADY EXISTS — it landed with the
plan-review impact redesign child fishable-apivorous-redhead; code-review now passes `impact_fn=impact_code`).
- TIER VALUES: each consequence binary carries the severity it implies if true — minor=0.3, moderate=0.6,
  serious=0.9. Lane-severity = MAX of the tiers of the TRUE binaries (no dilution; a minor/moderate binary
  cannot alone reach the block zone). No cross-binary compounding (conservative default).
- TWO LANES: `prod_lane = MAX(correctness tier-tagged binaries) * trigger_likelihood_mult`; `maint_lane =
  MAX(maintainability tier-tagged binaries) * freq_mult`. Each binary is assigned to EXACTLY one lane + one
  tier by an explicit table (below).
- TRIGGER-LIKELIHOOD (LLM-emitted enum on the NEW field `trigger_likelihood`, deliberately NOT named
  `likelihood` — the base SeverityAttrs already declares `likelihood`=low|medium|high mapped by _LIKE01, so a
  distinct name avoids a field-override collision): `common=1.0 / sometimes=0.6 / rare=0.25`; absent =>
  `common` (1.0) so a serious correctness binary is not silently dampened by missing metadata.
- CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER: `freq_mult = 0.5 + 0.5 * min(churn_90d, 30) / 30` where churn_90d = commits
  touching the finding's file in the last 90 days (`git log`, DET). Linear, saturates at 30. Applies to
  maint_lane only. FALLBACK: git unavailable / shallow clone / no file attributable => churn_90d = 0 =>
  freq_mult = 0.5 (the minimum). Best-effort; never fails the review.
- REVERSIBILITY FLOOR (det/hybrid), GATED: `reversibility_floor = 0.6` iff BOTH (a) `impact_base > 0` (the
  finding identifies at least one true consequence) AND (b) the finding's file is a HARD-TO-REVERSE SURFACE by
  these DET path rules (else 0): migration = a path segment `migrations/` or a segment starting `migration`;
  released-artifact/packaging = basename in {pyproject.toml, setup.py, setup.cfg} or path contains `CHANGELOG`;
  serialization/schema = extension in {.proto, .sql} or basename matching `schema*.json`/`*.schema.json`;
  deletion = the file is removed in the diff (only `-` lines / git status D). 'exported public API' is
  best-effort (undetectable from paths alone -> treated as NOT hard-to-reverse; conservative floor 0).
  The `impact_base > 0` gate is deliberate: it lifts a GENUINE defect on a one-way-door surface to >=0.6 but
  does NOT manufacture impact for a clean/no-consequence finding on the same file (fixes the "fires
  unconditionally, over-inflates nits" hole).
- DETECTION AMPLIFIER: `amp = 1.0 if (silent_failure or escapes_automation) else 0.8`. Both are LLM-emitted
  booleans in `code_review_verification_model` (a code-review detection judgment), defaulting to false (=>
  amp 0.8) when absent.
- EXPLICIT COMPOSE ORDER (single expression, no ambiguity):
  `impact_base = MAX(prod_lane, maint_lane); impact = max(min(1.0, impact_base * amp), reversibility_floor)`.
- RE-ANCHOR: drop the 0.33 attribute floor; co-calibrate tier values + block threshold on the labeled set
  (below) so a maintainability landmine reaches ~0.8+ and a nit ~0.15-0.2.
- CARVED SUB-QUESTION SET (audit-approved) with EXPLICIT lane + tier (ADD to, do NOT replace, the existing
  severity_attributes — CodeSeverityAttrs extends the base five):
    prod lane:  data_loss_without_recovery=0.9, security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere=0.9,
                silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision=0.9, capability_degraded=0.6
    maint lane: unversioned_published_contract_break=0.9, safety_net_removal_without_replacement=0.9,
                contract_drift=0.6, hidden_invariant=0.6, implicit_coupling=0.3, dead_code=0.3
  Each serious binary carries its charitable carve-out in its verify-prompt definition.

## Implementation mechanics + calibration data
- SUB-ANSWER SCHEMA: emit the tier-tagged binaries (bool) + `trigger_likelihood` (enum: common|sometimes|rare) + `silent_failure` /
  `escapes_automation` (bool) as NEW fields on a `code_review_verification_model()` whose severity_attributes
  is `CodeSeverityAttrs` EXTENDING the shared 5 attrs — a SINGLE aggregate Pass-2 verify call, no added LLM
  calls. Wired in THREE named places: (i) register a `code_review_verification` contract in
  `code_review/contracts.py` -> code_review_verification_model; (ii) change the verify step's `output_schema`
  in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/code-review.yaml` (~line 147) from `verification` to
  `code_review_verification`; (iii) update the `outputs:` front-matter key in
  `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-verify.md` from `verification` to `code_review_verification`. The
  kernel `verification` contract (plan-review + default) is untouched. Each new binary defaults false / trigger_likelihood 'common' /
  detection false, so an older/absent verifier ABSTAINS (no true binary => lane 0 => impact 0).
- VERIFY-PROMPT INSTRUCTION TEXT (required so the LLM knows how to populate the new fields): `code-review-verify.md`
  gains an ADDITIVE 'CODE-REVIEW CONSEQUENCE BINARIES' block (kept alongside the existing severity-attribute
  instructions) that names each of the 10 tier-tagged binaries under its lane with its tier (serious/moderate/
  minor) and a one-line 'set TRUE only when the change genuinely exhibits this' rule, plus a 'TRIGGER LIKELIHOOD +
  DETECTION' block defining `trigger_likelihood` (common|sometimes|rare, default common) and the `silent_failure` /
  `escapes_automation` booleans. It instructs the verifier to leave a binary FALSE unless earned (a false binary
  contributes nothing; a minor/moderate binary alone cannot reach the block zone), mirroring the additive axes
  block plan-review's `plan_review_verifier.md` gained.
- DET ENRICHMENT (merge target = the VERIFICATION dict, NOT the finding dict): `pass3_decide` reads its
  attrs from the *verification* dict (`verification.get("severity_attributes")`), NOT from the finding
  dict (in `pass3_over_findings`, the finding `f` is used only for `threshold_for(f.get("criteria"))`;
  the attrs `impact_code` receives come from `verifs.get(i)`). So `code_review_decide` computes `churn90`
  (90-day git commit count for the finding's file) + `hard_to_reverse_surface` (from the changed-file
  path / deletion) per finding and injects them IN PLACE into `reshape.verifications[i]["severity_attributes"]`
  (the exact dict `impact_code` receives) BEFORE calling `pass3_over_findings`. Writing to the finding's
  own `severity_attributes` would silently no-op (impact_code would read only defaults). Best-effort: any
  git/path failure leaves the signal at its safe default (churn 0 => freq_mult 0.5; surface False => no floor).
- PER-GATE DISPATCH: `code_review_decide` passes `impact_fn=impact_code`. Plan-review is byte-unchanged
  (it threads impact_plan; nothing here touches it) — asserted by test.
- CALIBRATION/LABELED SET: a curated fixture at a NEW `tests/unit/fixtures/` directory (created by this ticket, with a provenance README) — `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` — of
  code-review findings each tagged HIGH (block-worthy) or NIT, provenance = the dogfooded code-review corpus +
  this program's held-out adjudication (initial ~31 labeled findings; grows via the data-capture child).
- ADR: this novel two-lane model is an architectural decision — record it under `docs/adr/`.

## Scope
- `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py` (`impact_code`), `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py`
  (`code_review_verification_model` + CodeSeverityAttrs), `src/rebar/llm/code_review/` (verify-step contract
  wiring, the DET enrichment in the decide op, the verify prompt), the labeled fixture + re-anchoring
  calibration test, and the ADR. Plan-review impact unchanged.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `impact_code = max(min(1.0, MAX(prod_lane, maint_lane) * amp), reversibility_floor)` per the explicit
      compose above, with tier-tagged-binary MAX within each lane using the stated tier values and the stated
      trigger_likelihood_mult / freq_mult / reversibility_floor (impact_base>0 gated) / amp formulas. Unit-tested for
      each multiplier + the gate. Proving command: `.venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_impact_code.py -v`.
- [ ] Carved serious/moderate/minor sub-question set implemented with charitable carve-outs, ADDED to (not
      replacing) severity_attributes via CodeSeverityAttrs; emitted in ONE aggregate Pass-2 call (no added
      LLM calls); reversibility/reach/change-frequency are DET/hybrid (diff + git) with a churn=0/freq=0.5
      fallback when git is unavailable. Proving command: `.venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_impact_code.py -v`.
- [ ] Per-gate dispatch via `impact_fn=impact_code`; a test asserts plan-review's decide path is unchanged
      (impact_plan still dispatched; impact_code never touches it); an ADR under docs/adr/ records the model.
      Proving command: `.venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_impact_code.py tests/unit/test_impact_plan.py -v`.
- [ ] `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` exists (each row HIGH/NIT, provenance in a header
      comment or sibling README). A test asserts impact_code SEPARATES the classes on the fixture with a
      CONCRETE margin (NO "or explained" escape): `median(impact_code[HIGH]) - median(impact_code[NIT]) >=
      0.30`, AND that this median gap is strictly GREATER than the old mean `impact`'s gap on the same
      fixture (the redesign beats the old scalar). Validated diff-grounded before any threshold-down tuning
      (the tuning itself is the calibration child raptorial-galloping-dragon).
finding: The plan's stated rationale for naming the new field `trigger_likelihood` (rather than `likelihood`) — 'the base SeverityAttrs already declares likelihood=low|medium|high mapped by _LIKE01, so a distinct name avoids a field-override collision' — is based on a false premise. Reading verify.py shows that SeverityAttrs is a locally-scoped class defined inside the verification_model() factory function, and PlanSeverityAttrs is likewise a locally-scoped class inside plan_review_verification_model() that RE-DECLARES all five base fields rather than subclassing SeverityAttrs. There is no Python inheritance hierarchy from which a field-override collision could occur. The new CodeSeverityAttrs will follow the same re-declaration pattern, so the 'avoid field-override collision' rationale is unsupported by the codebase structure.
location: What section — TRIGGER-LIKELIHOOD paragraph: 'deliberately NOT named `likelihood` — the base SeverityAttrs already declares `likelihood`=low|medium|high mapped by _LIKE01, so a distinct name avoids a field-override collision'
suggested_fix: Replace 'the base SeverityAttrs already declares `likelihood`=low|medium|high mapped by _LIKE01, so a distinct name avoids a field-override collision' with 'a distinct name avoids ambiguity in the LLM prompt between the existing ordinal `likelihood` severity attribute (low/medium/high) and this new trigger-frequency enum (common/sometimes/rare); the code_review_verification_model factory will follow the same re-declaration pattern as plan_review_verification_model (see verify.py lines 242-255), not Python subclassing'.

title: Add README badges (PyPI version, Python versions, license, CI status)
plan:
## Why

Audit OSS finding #6 (MEDIUM). README.md has no badges — no PyPI version, supported-Python, license, or CI status indicator. For a package published to PyPI (nava-rebar) these are the fastest trust signals an evaluator scans for, and their absence makes an otherwise rigorous project look less maintained than it is. The repo already has everything the badges would advertise (PyPI releases, a 3.11/3.12 matrix, Apache-2.0, a green test workflow).

## What / files

Add a badge row at the top of README.md: PyPI version (shields.io pypi/v/nava-rebar), Python versions (pypi/pyversions/nava-rebar), license (Apache-2.0), and the test.yml workflow status badge from the GitHub mirror. Keep it to one line; no coverage or misc vanity badges unless already measured in CI. Note the GitHub Actions badge reflects the mirror's branch CI, which is accurate (Gerrit replicates main there and branch CI runs on push).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] README.md renders a single badge row: PyPI version, Python versions, license, CI status
- [ ] All badge URLs resolve (spot-check rendered README on the mirror)
- [ ] Badge targets link to PyPI project page, workflow runs, and LICENSE respectively
- [ ] No README content reflow beyond the added row
finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing shields.io as the badge provider (vs. PyPI's own native badge endpoint or GitHub's built-in badge styling). The 'Why' section motivates the badges themselves but not the shields.io mechanism.
location: What / files section — 'PyPI version (shields.io pypi/v/nava-rebar), Python versions (pypi/pyversions/nava-rebar), license (Apache-2.0)'

title: region_gated_floor: content-addressed novelty convergence for code review
plan:
## Why
This is the convergence mechanism itself: reuse plan-review's novelty machinery to stop code review
re-nagging carryover findings and to drop novel low-impact findings on UNCHANGED code, while ALWAYS
raising novel findings on newly-changed code (the per-citation region gate is the deliberate
divergence from plan-review's whole-artifact floor).

## What
- **Consume the reader.** Call `latest_code_review_result(key)` (revenued-thickset-dassie) for the
  current review's typed key to obtain prior SURFACED findings + the prior `deps` map. Key selection:
  `session:<id>` for local reviews (set on the verdict by paradoxal-balsamic-bubblefish),
  `change:<id>` for Gerrit.
- **REUSE unchanged kernel primitives (no fork):** the novelty SCORING CONTRACT
  (`review_kernel.verify.novelty_model` / `NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS`), the reshaper
  `review_kernel.verify.reshape_novelties`, the per-finding novelty math
  `review_kernel.decide.novelty`, and the drop predicate
  `review_kernel.decide.rising_floor_drop(priority, novelty, *, t_novel, floor)` — all verbatim. A
  thin `score_code_novelty` (mirroring plan-review's `_score_floor_novelty` runner wiring) drives
  the sub-call and, from `reshape_novelties`, surfaces BOTH the novelty float (for the drop) AND the
  `matched_prior_id` (for `carried_from`). NOTE: it does NOT reuse the kernel `score_novelty`
  wrapper verbatim — that returns only `{index: float}` and DISCARDS `matched_prior_id`, which the
  carryover-labeling AC needs; `score_code_novelty` reuses the same underlying primitives
  (reshape + decide.novelty) so the scoring is identical, just also returning the matched id.
- **NEW code-review novelty PROMPT + reviewer id.** Add `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code_review_novelty.md`
  (`outputs: code_review_novelty`, `execution_mode: single_turn`, `category: code-review-pass`) and a
  `code-novelty` reviewer id used in the sub-call's `RunRequest(reviewers=["code-novelty"])`. Wiring
  mirrors plan-review's `_score_floor_novelty` runner: the diff is the domain context and the prior
  SURFACED findings are appended to the instructions as a context block (NOT a `{{plan}}`/template
  var). Register the reused contract at `code_review/contracts.py::register_contracts()`
  (`contracts.register_contract("code_review_novelty", novelty_model)`, importing `novelty_model`
  from `review_kernel.verify`) — mirroring `plan_review/passes.py`; without it a
  `RunRequest(output_schema="code_review_novelty")` fails at runtime.
  - **cites_prior_location REDEFINED for code (critical).** `NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS` shares the field
    name `cites_prior_location`, whose kernel/plan meaning is "same PLAN location/section/AC line".
    The code prompt MUST firmly redefine it so the model does not anchor on the plan meaning:
    `cites_prior_location — does this finding point at the SAME source file AND approximately the
    same line region (within ~10 lines) as the prior finding? (not merely the same file — the same
    localized area)`. (This closes the epic-review advisory T1.)
- **NEW `region_gate.py`** (`src/rebar/llm/code_review/region_gate.py`) defining the region-state
  vocabulary + detector:
  - Constants: `REGION_UNCHANGED = "unchanged"`, `REGION_CHANGED = "changed"`, `REGION_UNKNOWN = "unknown"`.
  - **Citation basis:** a finding's cited region is derived from its `location` field (a `path` or
    `path:line`). The detector takes the finding's cited FILE path, hashes the current file
    (`attest._hash_file`), and compares to `prior_deps[path]`: equal → `UNCHANGED`; differ → `CHANGED`;
    path absent from `prior_deps`, OR the finding is multi-location / absence-evidence (no concrete
    file) / moved / renamed / any error → `UNKNOWN`. File-level in v1 (line-level deferred).
- **NEW `region_gated_floor`** (in `code_review/workflow_ops.py`, applied over the decided advisory
  set in the produce path before the sidecar emit — the code-review analogue of plan-review's
  `_maybe_apply_rising_floor`, which sits after the verdict and before emit):
  - A finding is DROPPED iff `rising_floor_drop(priority, novelty, t_novel, floor)` is true AND its
    region is `REGION_UNCHANGED`. `REGION_CHANGED` and `REGION_UNKNOWN` NEVER drop (both raise).
  - **Thresholds/activation (decided):** reuse the EXISTING config — `verify.novelty_drop_threshold`
    (t_novel), `verify.novelty_priority_floor` (floor), AND `verify.novelty_drop_active` as the
    activation/evidence gate. This introduces NO NEW config key (the epic's "no eval-gated
    activation flag" = no NEW flag): the code-review floor reuses plan-review's SAME three keys, so
    it is OFF by default and an operator opts in with evidence exactly as plan-review requires
    (preserving the opt-in-with-evidence discipline the reviewer flagged). On top of the flag, the
    floor also SELF-GATES: inert whenever there is no prior memory for the current key (first review,
    or a reader error → empty prior set). Both gates must be open to drop anything.
  - Dropped findings carry `drop_reason = "novelty-region"`.
  - **Carryover labeling:** a finding that matches a prior surfaced finding (low novelty) but is NOT
    dropped is stamped `carried_from` = the `matched_prior_id` returned by `score_code_novelty` for
    that finding (the prior finding's id; empty ⇒ not stamped). Data model: by the time the floor runs (post-decide, in the produce path), findings
    are plain dicts in the verdict's `advisory`/`blocking` lists — `carried_from` is added as a KEY
    on that dict (no Pydantic model change needed; the coach already ran in-workflow, so the floor
    strips coaching notes whose `finding_refs` point at a `carried_from`-stamped finding from the
    verdict's `coaching` list). The finding remains surfaced.
  - **FAIL-SAFE (full mirror of plan-review):** the whole floor is wrapped in try/except; any
    reader/hash/compare/novelty error returns the FULL unfiltered finding set (no drops). A finding
    with no novelty entry defaults to novelty 0.0 (carryover = kept).
- **Neutrality (ADR 0008 Invariant 1):** the Pass-1 finder NEVER receives prior findings; only the
  post-find novelty seam does.

## Scope
- `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py` (region_gated_floor + novelty sub-call wiring),
  `src/rebar/llm/code_review/region_gate.py` (NEW: vocab + detector),
  `src/rebar/llm/code_review/contracts.py` (register `code_review_novelty`),
  `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code_review_novelty.md` (NEW prompt),
  `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` (invoke the floor before emit).
- Depends on the reader/payload (revenued-thickset-dassie) and, for end-to-end LOCAL integration, on
  local persistence (paradoxal-balsamic-bubblefish). This story is reviewed/implemented AFTER c639
  (+3bff) are committed on the stack, so the reader (`latest_code_review_result`) + `deps` map exist
  in the tree; region_gated_floor UNIT tests run against a mocked reader.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A `code-novelty` reviewer prompt (`code_review_novelty.md`) + reviewer id exist, binding the
      reused novelty contract, with diff context + prior findings in the instructions block and
      `cites_prior_location` redefined for source-file/line regions.
- [ ] The `code_review_novelty` contract is registered in `contracts.register_contracts()`;
      `rising_floor_drop`, `reshape_novelties`, and `decide.novelty` are reused UNCHANGED (the thin
      `score_code_novelty` surfaces `matched_prior_id` for carryover; it does not reuse the
      `score_novelty` wrapper, which discards it).
- [ ] `region_gate.py` defines `REGION_UNCHANGED`/`REGION_CHANGED`/`REGION_UNKNOWN` used by the
      detector, which compares a cited file's current sha256 to the prior `deps` map.
- [ ] `region_gated_floor` drops a finding ONLY when novel AND low-priority AND `REGION_UNCHANGED`;
      `REGION_CHANGED` and `REGION_UNKNOWN` both raise; dropped findings carry
      `drop_reason = "novelty-region"`.
- [ ] Thresholds/activation reuse the EXISTING `verify.novelty_drop_threshold`,
      `verify.novelty_priority_floor`, and `verify.novelty_drop_active` (no NEW config key); the
      floor is OFF by default and additionally self-gates inert with no prior memory.
- [ ] Carryover findings are stamped `carried_from` = the matched prior finding's id (string, added
      as a dict key), have their coaching notes stripped from the verdict, but remain surfaced.
- [ ] Any reader/hash/compare/novelty error returns the full unfiltered finding set (no drops).
- [ ] The Pass-1 finder receives no prior findings; only the novelty seam does — asserted by a test
      that the finder/verifier pass inputs contain no prior-findings context (ADR 0008 Invariant 1).
- [ ] Tests (mocked reader): unchanged-region low-priority novel → dropped; changed-region novel →
      raised; absence/multi-location → UNKNOWN → raised; carryover → `carried_from` = matched id +
      coaching stripped; forced reader error → no drops; and a neutrality test that the Pass-1
      finder step receives no prior findings.
finding: `score_code_novelty` is specified to bypass `score_novelty` (which includes token-budget chunking) to gain access to `matched_prior_id`, but the plan only says it 'mirrors' `_score_floor_novelty` wiring without explicitly specifying whether it re-implements the chunking from `verify_request_chunks`. If chunking is omitted, `score_code_novelty` will silently send the entire advisory set in one over-budget sub-call when advisory findings are numerous, causing the novelty sub-call to fail or produce garbage — and the fail-safe (empty map → novelty 0.0 → carryover → no drops) would then suppress all drops rather than correctly scoring them.
location: What section: 'REUSE unchanged kernel primitives' / 'NOTE: it does NOT reuse the kernel score_novelty wrapper verbatim'
suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'score_code_novelty wraps the full chunked sub-call loop (mirroring score_novelty's verify_request_chunks + per-chunk run loop), but collects both the novelty float AND matched_prior_id from reshape_novelties in the same loop — the only divergence from score_novelty is the richer return type {index: (float, str)}; the chunking, fail-safe, and window budget are identical.'

title: S6 Docs (CLAUDE.md/CONTRIBUTING.md landing-contract rewrite) + ADR-0042
plan:
## Why
The agent landing contract only works if agents adopt it: the old "watch the 2 votes" model is now broken (two green votes no longer means "it will land" — a conflict blocks it while votes stay green). Docs must teach the new contract or the conflict case silently reintroduces babysitting.

## What
Rewrite this repo's landing section in `CLAUDE.md` + `CONTRIBUTING.md` to the new contract, and record the design in `docs/adr/0042-*.md`.

## Dependencies
Depends on **S1** (`sterilized-papery-horse`, 843b-801f-9ef9-442d) — the `Autosubmit` label — and **S4** (`brightish-growable-dodo`, 73f5-4df7-4fac-4738) — the `land` command: the docs must not merge to `main` until the label and command they instruct agents to use are actually live — otherwise the guide points at tooling that does not yet exist.

## Scope
Docs + ADR only.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `CLAUDE.md` + `CONTRIBUTING.md` landing section rewritten: "set `Autosubmit` / run `land`; act on the single typed outcome; do NOT watch votes or treat two green votes as 'it will land'."
- [ ] **Grep the whole repo** for the deprecated "two green votes" / "watch votes" pattern (e.g. in `docs/your-first-change.md`) and update **ALL** occurrences to the new contract — not only `CLAUDE.md`/`CONTRIBUTING.md`.
- [ ] `docs/adr/0042-*.md` records the auto-lander design AND the rationale behind the key decisions, so future idea-epics inherit the "why", not just the "what". It MUST capture: the agent contract + outcome enum; the R5 safety invariant (tested tree = merged tree) and how each mechanism preserves it; the **eight key design decisions with their prior-art grounding** (Zuul / LUCI CV-Chromium CQ / Gerrit primitives / Prow-Tide) — the `Autosubmit`↔`CQ+2` label, the two-channel status surface, the CQ-reset hand-back, the raw time-to-land timer, the autoheal hang-recovery, the stdlib-not-pygerrit2 client choice, `rebase_on_behalf_of_uploader`, and the sentinel+drain stop; the separation-of-concerns boundary (project infra, not `rebar` core); the reliability posture + accepted single-instance SPOF tradeoff; and the **deferrals with their promotion triggers** — the provider-seam (idea `1701-8bc1-e3b7-4984`, promote on a 2nd landing platform), bounded-speculation batching (idea `c3a9-5f4c-bdc6-4e27`, promote only on MEASURED serial-treadmill pain), and HA/external-state (promote if single-instance availability is insufficient). **Confirm both idea ids resolve in the store** and anchor ADR-0042 to them. (A full drafted record is attached as a comment on this ticket — land that content.)
- [ ] Docs cross-reference ADR-0040 (FFO) + ADR-0041 (LLM-Review `TRIVIAL_REBASE` carry) as the gate substrate the lander sits on.
finding: AC2 requires the ADR to record 'the staged serial→batch plan', but the plan body (Why/What/Scope) never describes or explains what this plan is. The term originates in the parent epic f1fa's description but is not restated or summarized here. An ADR author working only from this plan has no specification to write the required ADR section.
location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'docs/adr/0042-*.md records … the staged serial→batch plan'
suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'The ADR should record the two-phase delivery: Phase 1 — a serial auto-lander (one change/chain at a time; no gate-semantics change); Phase 2 — cross-stack batching (assemble N non-conflicting changes into one CI run, land atomically).'

title: Committed store compatibility record with a fail-closed capability gate
plan:
# Committed store compatibility record with a fail-closed capability gate

Introduce a committed store-level compatibility record (`format_version` +
`required_capabilities`) that a v1.0 binary reads before any mutating or
externally-publishing operation, failing closed on an unknown version or required
capability while keeping reads and diagnostics available. (Readiness Finding 2 —
verified VALID against `src/rebar/_store/ensures.py`, `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py`,
`src/rebar/_commands/{compact,fsck,init}.py`, `docs/migrations.md`.)

## Why

rebar's migration mechanism is an idempotent desired-state ensure-registry, not an
ordered ledger: `run_ensures` runs every unit under the write lock, converts
exceptions to `failed`, and **returns normally** (`src/rebar/_store/ensures.py`
docstring "Never raises"; init/boot never abort). Event compatibility is
preserve-and-ignore: unknown event types are skipped in replay
(`reducer/_replay.py:195-196`) and omitted from the compaction fold but kept on
disk (`_commands/compact.py:179-195`), and `fsck` emits an informational WARN that
does not increment `issue_count` or stop operation (`_commands/fsck.py:229-252`,
which itself notes "a reconcile host on an old binary may push stale state").

This forward-compat is correct for *optional additive* data — it prevents an old
compactor from deleting new bytes. But there is **no committed store-format-version
or required-capability record** (confirmed absent; `SCHEMA_VERSION` is a stamp,
never compared to fail closed). So an old binary computes state that omits the
unknown event and can then emit a new event, compact, evaluate close/verify policy,
or push a stale field to Jira from that incomplete state. `docs/migrations.md`
explicitly files the "A-tier ledger + committed store-format version that fails
closed" under future work. **The reader must ship in v1.0**: an already-installed
v1.0 binary cannot be taught later to inspect a record whose existence and
semantics were not defined in v1.0 — so to ever mark a store as requiring a
post-v1.0 capability, v1.0 must already know how to detect and refuse unknown
required capabilities.

## What

Adopt git's repository-format-version pattern
(`core.repositoryformatversion` + `extensions.*`: an implementation that does not
understand the advertised format or a required extension must not operate, because
continuing can produce incorrect results or lose data), and git's staged rollout
(teach readers first, make writing opt-in, default later):

- **The record.** Add a committed store-level record, minimally
  `{"format_version": 1, "required_capabilities": []}`, at a fixed store path (e.g.
  `.store-compat.json`). It is **committed to the `tickets` branch** (not
  git-ignored) — it is the shared fact the gate reads. Write it with the store's
  standard write-to-tempfile-then-atomic-rename pattern so a partial/interrupted
  write never leaves a truncated file.
- **Recognised-capability vocabulary.** The new module exports
  `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES: frozenset[str] = frozenset()` — the set of capability strings
  **this** binary understands. The gate blocks when any entry in a record's
  `required_capabilities` is not in `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES`. Future capabilities are
  added to this set only when the feature that consumes them ships (git
  `extensions.*` semantics).
- **The gate error.** The module exports `StoreIncompatibleError(Exception)`, raised
  by the gate; entry-point callers convert it to the existing `CommandError` /
  non-zero exit path (as other gate errors do), with a message naming the
  unsupported version/capability.
- **Four record states (explicit).** (1) **Absent** → implicit legacy format
  version 0, compatible / pass-through, NOT blocked. (2) **Present + compatible** →
  pass. (3) **Present + incompatible** (unknown `format_version` or a
  `required_capabilities` entry ∉ `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES`) → **fail-closed**. (4)
  **Present + unreadable/corrupt** (JSON parse error, truncation, permission denied)
  → **fail-closed** with a diagnostic message naming the parse error and the record
  path (a corrupt record is NOT treated as absent — that would silently bypass the
  gate).
- **Expand phase (writing the record into pre-v1.0 stores).** Every store created by
  a pre-v1.0 build is state (1). An idempotent ensure unit (School B) writes the
  record at the current version when absent, at init / fsck-repair / MCP-boot.
  **Because `run_ensures` is fail-silent** (a `LockTimeout` silently skips the whole
  sweep; exceptions become `failed` and it returns normally), the covered-write path
  must not *depend* on the ensure sweep having run: the gate treats absent as
  pass-through regardless, and the record is written opportunistically. So a missed
  sweep never blocks writes — it only defers stamping the record to a later covered
  operation. **absent = legacy/needs-migration, not blocked; present-incompatible or
  corrupt = blocked.**
- **Gate at the true structural choke point, NOT by enumerating call-sites.** "No
  mutation without the gate" must not rest on a hand-maintained list a future path can
  bypass. The single convergence point for **all lock-based store mutations** is
  **`lock.acquire()` in `src/rebar/_store/lock.py`** — both the `write_lock()` context
  manager AND the direct callers that bypass it (`txn.py`'s `_acquire_write_lock` for
  claim/transition, `compact.py:167`/`:415`, `fsck.py:419`) call `acquire()`. Insert
  the `check_store_compat()` gate **inside `lock.acquire()`**, after `canonical_tracker()`
  resolves the path and before the fcntl/mkdir legs — so every lock-holding path
  (context-manager and raw-handle) is covered by one insertion point, with no
  enumeration. **Gate ALL `lock.acquire()` unconditionally — no `read_only` parameter
  needed.** The diagnostic exemption is already **structural**: `fsck`'s diagnostic
  path reads ticket data **without acquiring the lock at all**, so it is inherently
  ungated and stays available; only `fsck`'s **repair** path (which *does* call
  `lock.acquire()` at `fsck.py:419`), and `compact.py`/`txn.py`, hold the lock and are
  therefore gated. This is simpler and less error-prone than a read/write-intent flag.
- **`run_ensures` must NOT swallow the gate (fail-closed integrity).** `run_ensures`
  (`src/rebar/_store/ensures.py`) wraps its whole `with write_lock(...)` body in a broad
  `except Exception` that logs-and-returns (honouring its "Never raises" sweep contract).
  Because the gate raises `StoreIncompatibleError` **inside `lock.acquire()`** — which
  `write_lock()` calls — that broad handler would **catch and swallow it**, turning a
  fail-closed incompatibility into a silent sweep no-op for every caller that self-heals
  through `run_ensures` (init, MCP-boot's ensure sweep, `fsck --repair`). Remediate by
  adding an explicit `except StoreIncompatibleError: raise` **before** the broad
  `except Exception` in `run_ensures`, so the incompatibility propagates to the caller;
  the init / MCP-boot / fsck-repair entry points then surface it as a **non-zero exit /
  refused startup** (converting `StoreIncompatibleError` to their existing
  `CommandError` / error path). This keeps the gate un-swallowable on the ensure-sweep
  path. (Note: `StoreIncompatibleError` subclasses `Exception`, so this explicit re-raise
  is required — it is not skipped automatically the way a `BaseException` would be.)
- **Two paths do NOT go through `lock.acquire()` and each gets its own explicit gate:**
  1. **`fsck_recover.py`** performs raw `git cherry-pick`/`rebase --continue`/`merge
     --abort` via its `_git()` helper with **no locking at all** — so it is genuinely
     un-gated by the lock choke point. Add an explicit `check_store_compat(tracker_dir)`
     at the top of `fsck_recover_cli` before any mutation. (Wrapping the mutation block
     in `write_lock` too would additionally close a real pre-existing concurrency gap,
     but at minimum the explicit gate is required.)
  2. **Outbound Jira publish** — the push/reconcile side effect is a **remote API
     call**, not a lock-held write. Jira writes are scattered
     (`create_issue`/`update_issue`/`delete_issue`/`add_comment` across
     `apply_outbound.py` + `dispatch_one.py`), but they all funnel once per pass
     through **`reconcile._apply_mutations()`** (`src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py`,
     which calls `applier.apply(...)`). **Exact insertion point (no top-level `persist`
     guard exists in this function):** `_apply_mutations()` binds `persist = ctx.persist`
     near the top and threads it down into `applier.apply(...)`; it has **no**
     `if persist:` / `if not persist: return` branch of its own. So add the gate
     explicitly: immediately after `persist = ctx.persist` is bound (before the
     filter-narrowing / preflight block), insert
     `if persist: check_store_compat(repo_root / ".tickets-tracker")`. Because dry-run /
     reconcile-check / cap-0 passes run with `persist=False`, the `if persist:` guard
     excludes them; live (persisting) passes are gated before any `applier.apply(...)`
     Jira write. This mirrors the existing fail-closed cross-project guard precedent at
     `applier.py:637-652` ("refuse BEFORE issuing any Jira write … abort the whole pass,
     no partial writes"). **Use the reconciler's own tracker path**
     (`repo_root / ".tickets-tracker"`, hardcoded at `reconcile.py:133/670`), NOT
     `config.tracker_dir()`, since the reconciler resolves the store directly.
- **Completeness is CI-enforced.** A coverage test/lint greps the tree for **both**
  `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(` (and asserts each routes through the gate) plus
  asserts `fsck_recover_cli` calls `check_store_compat` — so a new un-gated write path,
  in any of the acquisition forms, fails CI rather than relying on this prose list.
- **Read/diagnostic allowance + the structured report shape (defined).** Under an
  incompatible/corrupt record, the read-only commands — `fsck` (diagnostic mode),
  `show`, `list`, `search` — stay available (exit 0). The incompatibility is surfaced in
  a **defined, machine-checkable shape**: `fsck`'s diagnostic JSON gains a top-level
  `compat_error` object
  `{"compat_error": {"kind": "unknown_format_version" | "unknown_capability" | "corrupt_record",
  "detail": "<human string naming the offending version / capability / parse-error + the
  record path>"}}` (and the same message on a stderr `WARNING` line). The `compat` module
  exports a **non-raising** `describe_store_compat(tracker) -> dict | None` that returns
  exactly this `{"kind", "detail"}` payload (or `None` when compatible/absent); the fsck
  diagnostic path calls it and, when non-`None`, attaches it as `compat_error` and emits
  the WARNING — without changing the exit code. AC6 asserts `.compat_error.kind` via
  `jq -e` so the check is deterministic. `fsck`'s **repair** mode (write-intent) is gated
  like any other mutation (non-zero exit).
- **Rollback safety (decided, then proven).** Post-rollback the committed
  `.store-compat.json` remains on the `tickets` branch unchanged, and a pre-v1.0 binary
  **ignores it** (ignored-unknown → rollback-safe): `.store-compat.json` is a *new*
  file at a path no pre-v1.0 code reads — pre-v1.0 replay/compaction/fsck open only
  event files and the known metadata files (`.env-id`, `.gitignore`, etc.), never this
  name — so there is no parse-and-error path. This is the committed design position
  (not a deferral), and the **hard rollback test below is its empirical proof** (git-
  stash the gate code, run `rebar fsck` against a store containing `.store-compat.json`,
  assert operable + file byte-unchanged). The delete-`.store-compat.json` recovery is
  documented as belt-and-suspenders only.
- **Scope boundary.** Keep the ensure-registry for convergent check-then-act repairs.
  Do **not** build the ordered ledger here — only the record + reader gate. Clarify
  the relationship to `SCHEMA_VERSION` in `reducer/_version.py` (it stays the
  event-schema discriminator; `format_version` is the new store-format gate — state
  whether they move together or independently).

## Scope

- New module under `src/rebar/_store/` for the compatibility record + gate, exporting
  `StoreIncompatibleError`, `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES`, and `check_store_compat()`.
- `src/rebar/_store/lock.py` — insert the gate inside `lock.acquire()` (the single
  convergence point for `write_lock()` + all direct `lock.acquire()` callers:
  `txn.py`, `compact.py:167`/`:415`, `fsck.py:419`), gating every acquisition
  unconditionally (fsck diagnostic holds no lock → structurally exempt).
- `src/rebar/_commands/fsck_recover.py` — explicit `check_store_compat()` before its
  raw-git mutation (it holds no lock, so the `lock.acquire()` gate does not cover it).
- `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py` — `if persist: check_store_compat(repo_root/".tickets-tracker")`
  inserted in `_apply_mutations()` immediately after `persist = ctx.persist` is bound
  (the function has no top-level `persist` guard; the `if persist:` wrapper is the single
  per-pass chokepoint for both write paths and excludes dry-run/cap-0 passes).
- `src/rebar/_store/ensures.py` — add `except StoreIncompatibleError: raise` before
  `run_ensures`'s broad `except Exception` so the gate is not swallowed on the ensure
  sweep; add a `store-compat` ensure unit id.
- A coverage test/lint grepping `lock.acquire(` + `write_lock(` + `fsck_recover`'s check.
- `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` — relate `format_version` to the existing
  preserve-and-ignore `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` machinery and to `SCHEMA_VERSION`
  (optional-additive events stay preserve-and-ignore; a required capability is the
  new fail-closed path).
- `src/rebar/_commands/init.py` — the ensure unit that writes the record when absent.
- `docs/migrations.md` — document the record, the four states, the fail-closed
  semantics, the absent-record upgrade path, and the concrete rollback state.
- Interface tests extending `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py`.
- Out of scope: the ordered migration ledger; changing any existing event-replay
  behaviour for currently-known types.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A committed store compatibility record (`format_version` +
  `required_capabilities`) is created at init at a fixed path, written atomically
  (tempfile + rename), and read by a gate function that raises
  `StoreIncompatibleError`; the module exports `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES: frozenset[str]`.
- [ ] An **absent** record (pre-v1.0 store) is treated as legacy format version 0 /
  compatible (pass-through, not blocked), and the ensure unit idempotently writes the
  record on the next init / fsck-repair / MCP-boot — verified by a test that opens a
  record-less store with a v1.0 build, confirms it operates, and confirms the record
  is then present (upgrade-in-place, no manual step); the test also confirms a missed
  ensure sweep does not block writes — by monkeypatching `run_ensures` to raise
  `LockTimeout` (or return a `failed` outcome) and asserting a subsequent write still
  succeeds (the gate treats the still-absent record as pass-through).
- [ ] The gate is inserted inside `lock.acquire()` (`src/rebar/_store/lock.py`, after
  path resolution, before fcntl/mkdir) and gates **every** acquisition unconditionally
  — `write_lock()` context-manager callers AND direct `lock.acquire()` callers
  (`txn.py`, `compact.py`, `fsck.py:419` repair) — by one insertion point; `fsck`
  diagnostic holds no lock so it is structurally exempt. Plus an explicit
  `check_store_compat()` at the top of `fsck_recover_cli` (raw-git, no-lock mutation)
  and in `reconcile._apply_mutations()` as `if persist: check_store_compat(repo_root/".tickets-tracker")`
  inserted immediately after `persist = ctx.persist` is bound (there is no top-level
  `persist` guard in that function; the `if persist:` wrapper is what excludes
  dry-run/reconcile-check/cap-0 passes), before `applier.apply()`, for the outbound
  Jira push. It raises `StoreIncompatibleError` (→ non-zero exit) before any side
  effect when the record is **present but incompatible**. Verified by (a) a test in
  `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py` writing a record
  with a `required_capabilities` entry ∉ `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES` (e.g. `["unknown-v99"]`)
  and driving a lock-held mutation (store files byte-unchanged), an `fsck_recover`
  invocation, and a reconcile pass (mock Jira adapter records zero calls); and (b) a
  coverage test/lint that greps the tree for **both** `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(`
  and for `fsck_recover_cli`'s + `_apply_mutations`'s `check_store_compat` call,
  asserting each routes through the
  gate — so a new un-gated write path in any acquisition form fails CI.
- [ ] A **corrupt/unreadable** record (truncated JSON / parse error) is treated as
  fail-closed (NOT as absent) with a diagnostic naming the parse error and path —
  verified by a test writing a truncated record and asserting a mutating entry point
  is blocked.
- [ ] A rollback **test** (hard requirement) simulates a pre-v1.0 binary by invoking
  the store layer with the gate patched out (monkeypatch `check_store_compat` to a
  no-op, emulating code that predates it) against a store carrying the committed
  `.store-compat.json`, and asserts **operable** = `rebar list`/`fsck` exit 0 AND a
  normal write (e.g. `comment`) succeeds, and the `.store-compat.json` file is
  byte-unchanged afterward (proving upgrade-in-place won't brick a pre-v1.0 clone). The
  ignored-unknown guarantee and the delete-`.store-compat.json` compensating path are
  also documented in `docs/migrations.md`.
- [ ] Under an incompatible/corrupt record, the read-only commands `fsck` (diagnostic
  mode), `show`, `list`, and `search` exit with the read-allowed code (0) and `fsck`'s
  diagnostic JSON carries a defined `compat_error` object
  `{"compat_error": {"kind": "unknown_format_version"|"unknown_capability"|"corrupt_record",
  "detail": "<...>"}}` (produced by the non-raising `describe_store_compat()`), with the
  same message on a stderr `WARNING` line — while `fsck` **repair** mode (write-intent)
  exits non-zero — verified by a test asserting `.compat_error.kind` via `jq -e` (a
  deterministic field check, not a substring grep) and the repair-mode non-zero exit.
- [ ] The gate is **un-swallowable on the ensure-sweep path**: `run_ensures`'s broad
  `except Exception` does not silently absorb `StoreIncompatibleError` (it has an explicit
  `except StoreIncompatibleError: raise` before it), so init / MCP-boot / `fsck --repair`
  against a present-but-incompatible store fail closed (non-zero exit / refused startup)
  rather than logging a sweep no-op — verified by a test that stamps an incompatible
  record and asserts `run_ensures` (or the init entry point that calls it) surfaces the
  error rather than returning a benign `failed` outcome.
- [ ] Optional additive (unknown-but-not-*required*) events retain today's
  preserve-and-ignore behaviour — the existing forward-compat contract tests still
  pass, and a new test proves a *required* capability fails closed while an *optional*
  additive event does not.
- [ ] `docs/migrations.md` documents the record, the four states, the fail-closed
  gate, the deployment-order rationale (why the reader ships in v1.0), and the
  concrete rollback state.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass, and the change lands via the
  Gerrit two-vote gate.
finding: The plan claims '`reconcile.py:133/670`' hardcodes `.tickets-tracker` as justification for using `repo_root / ".tickets-tracker"` as the tracker path in the reconciler gate. Line 670 is verified correct (`tracker_dir = repo_root / ".tickets-tracker"`). However, line 133 is inside the `_commit_binding_store_snapshot()` function docstring context — it is NOT a `.tickets-tracker` hardcoding. The plan uses two line numbers to justify a stable path, but one of the cited lines does not support the claim.
location: What section / Outbound Jira publish gate: 'Use the reconciler's own tracker path (`repo_root / ".tickets-tracker"`, hardcoded at `reconcile.py:133/670`)'
suggested_fix: Change 'hardcoded at `reconcile.py:133/670`' to 'hardcoded at `reconcile.py:670`; the canonical constant is `git_adapter.TRACKER_DIR = ".tickets-tracker"`'.

title: op-cert: AWS IaC + deploy of the trusted gate service (zero fixed-cost: existing VM + API Gateway SigV4) + live authenticated integrity test
plan:
# op-cert: AWS IaC + deploy of the trusted gate service (zero fixed-cost: existing VM + API Gateway SigV4) + live authenticated integrity test

## Why
The trusted gate service (the sibling service story) is only a trust anchor once it runs in the
controlled environment with SigV4-authenticated access and a key nobody else can read. This
story declares that as code, **actually deploys it** (an authenticated AWS CLI is provided — the
deploy is in scope), and proves the deployed endpoint end-to-end. Deployment decisions are
settled in the epic's "Producer-signing & compute-runtime design resolution", **cost-verified
against the live account**: rebar's entire AWS footprint is the one t4g.large `rebar-gerrit`
box (+ Route 53 + free SSM parameters), so the binding constraint is **zero increase in fixed
monthly cost** — the service rides the existing box (the compose slot freed by the autolander's
retirement, ADR 0047) behind an API Gateway HTTP API (pay-per-request, no fixed fee), with the
key in free SSM Parameter Store. A dedicated Fargate/NLB/Secrets-Manager/KMS-CMK stack was
rejected (~$50+/mo new spend for isolation ADR 0046 L4 does not require).

**Hard dependency (not yet on `main`):** the sibling service story provides
`src/rebar/opcert_service/` and `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` — neither exists on `main`
yet; this story starts only after that story lands (enforced by the `depends_on` link).

## What
- **Terraform — extend the existing `infra/terraform/`** (the module that already declares the
  Gerrit EC2, its IAM instance profile, and the data volume):
  - an **API Gateway HTTP API** (v2) whose routes set `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` (SigV4
    — a ROUTE attribute; HTTP API v2 has NO separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource for
    IAM), over TLS. The integration URI is **`https://` to the box's HOST-nginx TLS origin**
    (nginx + certbot run as HOST services on the box — `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` header —
    with TLS on 443 at the Elastic-IP-bound hostname; NOT `http://…:80`, which the existing
    config 301-redirects and would break the integration) for the `/opcert/` routes, injecting a shared origin-guard header. Caller restriction is
    identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy): `AWS_IAM` auth requires SigV4,
    and the IaC CREATES the admin role it restricts to — `aws_iam_role` **`rebar-opcert-admin`**
    (no such role pre-exists in `infra/terraform/`), trust policy limited to the principal ARNs
    supplied via a new variable `opcert_admin_principal_arns` (set at deploy to the operator's
    IAM principals), with an inline policy granting `execute-api:Invoke` scoped to exactly this
    API's execution ARN — and no other principal/policy in the IaC grants Invoke;
  - an **SSM Parameter Store SecureString** `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` (declared with a
    placeholder `value` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`, so the out-of-band-seeded
    key is never reverted or clobbered by a later `terraform apply`) holding the
    **passphrase-free Ed25519 private key**. NO new IAM
    grant: the parameter lives under `/rebar/prod/`, which the instance profile's existing
    `rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read` policy (`infra/terraform/iam.tf`) already covers — and
    nothing broader is added;
  - the **origin-guard header provisioning path**: Terraform generates the guard value with
    `random_password` — the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to
    `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers` (today it pins only `hashicorp/aws
    ~> 5.0`; `terraform init` refreshes `.terraform.lock.hcl`) — stores it as SSM SecureString
    `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` (also under the existing grant), and maps it as a static
    request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration. Unlike the KEY
    parameter, the guard parameter is fully Terraform-managed and carries NO
    `lifecycle ignore_changes` (its value IS `random_password.result`; rotation =
    `terraform apply -replace=` on the random resource, which updates the SSM value and the
    integration header together) — the `ignore_changes = [value]` guard applies ONLY to the
    operator-seeded Ed25519 key parameter;
  - NO new fixed-monthly-cost resources: no Fargate/ECS service, no load balancer/VPC link, no
    Secrets Manager secret, no customer KMS CMK, and no `kms:Sign` anywhere (SSHSIG signing is
    performed by the service itself; AWS KMS has no Ed25519 signing).
- **Compose — extend `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`**: an `opcert` service on
  `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (sibling story) with env `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` and the SSM
  parameter names; an nginx route `/opcert/` → the service that rejects requests whose
  `X-Opcert-Guard` header does not match. On the box the guard value is materialized from its
  SSM parameter by `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` into a HOST-nginx map entry
  (nginx runs as a host service, not a container) with NAMED files and NO install-step
  ownership problem: `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` — which today has NO `/opcert/`
  location — gains, IN THE TEMPLATE ITSELF, the guard map with a fail-closed default and a
  GLOB include (`map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; include
  /etc/nginx/opcert-guard*.conf; }` — nginx accepts a zero-match glob include, unlike a
  missing literal include) plus the `location /opcert/ { if ($opcert_guard_ok = 0) { return
  403; } proxy_pass … }` block routing to the compose service. The DENY-ALL default is
  therefore STRUCTURAL in the template: a fresh boot with no materialized file serves 403 on
  /opcert/ and nginx starts cleanly — no separate default-file install step exists to own.
  `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` writes only the one-line map entry
  `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.map.conf` (`"<value>" 1;`) from the SSM parameter and reloads
  nginx; the guard value also lands in the service env. It is WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator
  `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` alongside the existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls,
  running BEFORE `docker compose up` (the precedent ordering), and it finishes with
  `nginx -s reload` so a guard rotation re-run takes effect (at first boot the reload is a
  no-op-safe refresh). Rotation hazard closed explicitly; (b) ROTATION — the documented
  runbook is two steps in order: `terraform apply -replace=random_password.opcert_guard`
  (updates the SSM value + the API Gateway static header), then re-run
  `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (rewrites the nginx map + `nginx -s reload`); between the two
  steps /opcert/ serves 403 — fail-closed, a brief and acceptable rotation window following the existing
  precedent (`infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh` / `materialize-g2p-config.sh` — SSM →
  file). It is WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` alongside the
  existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls, running BEFORE `docker compose up` (the
  precedent ordering), and it finishes with `nginx -s reload` so a guard rotation re-run takes
  effect on the host nginx (at first boot the reload is a no-op-safe refresh) — the same value Terraform maps onto the API Gateway integration, closing
  the loop.
- **Deploy + prove** (authenticated AWS CLI): `terraform apply`; generate the key locally
  (`ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N ""`) and seed the SecureString (the key exists nowhere else).
  **Key-loss recovery runbook (documented in the story's deploy notes):** if the SSM parameter
  is deleted or state is lost, the trust anchor is NOT recoverable and is not meant to be —
  recovery = mint a NEW keypair, re-seed the parameter, and pin the new public key as a NEW
  era entry in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (`added_at_log_position` = current tickets
  tip) while the lost key's entry is revoked with a grandfather pin at the loss point — certs
  already stored under the lost key stay valid (the era-anchor model), and only new certs
  require the new key. No historical re-certification is needed;
  deploy the compose service on the box; pin the environment's public key + `env_id` into
  `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` per the epic's "Authoritative era anchor" resolution
  (`added_at_log_position`, operator-stamped at the current tickets tip); then the live checks — a
  SigV4-signed job round-trip (submit → poll → terminal status), an unsigned request rejected
  (401/403 at API Gateway), and a direct-to-origin request without the guard header rejected.
## Scope
- In: the Terraform extension, compose/nginx wiring, key generation + SSM seeding procedure,
  the real deploy, the live round-trip evidence, the trusted-env pinning.
- Out: the service code and Dockerfile (sibling story); any change to rebar library code; the
  merge-gate consumer (shipped, 4214); resizing or re-homing the existing box.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `infra/terraform/` declares: the API Gateway HTTP API (v2) with
      `authorization_type = "AWS_IAM"` on its routes over TLS,
      `execute-api:Invoke` granted only to the IaC-created `rebar-opcert-admin` role (trust policy
      from `opcert_admin_principal_arns`; the assertion method is STRUCTURED and SELF-FAILING —
      the literal command the test runs exits non-zero on any violation:
      `terraform plan -out=tf.plan && terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e
      '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type == "aws_iam_role_policy" or
      .type == "aws_iam_policy") | {label: .name, s: (.values.policy | fromjson | .Statement[])}
      | select([.s.Action] | flatten | any(. == "execute-api:Invoke"))] | length == 1 and
      .[0].label == "opcert_admin_invoke"'` — where `.name` in `terraform show -json` is the
      Terraform RESOURCE LABEL, so the invoke policy is declared with the pinned label
      `aws_iam_role_policy.opcert_admin_invoke` on the `rebar-opcert-admin` role; exactly one
      statement grants Invoke and it is that resource, else the command exits non-zero), the `hashicorp/random` provider in `required_providers`,
      the box-origin integration with the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping; and
      the two SecureString parameters (the key parameter guarded by
      `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`)
      (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard`) under the existing
      `rebar-gerrit-ssm-params-read` path grant with NO new/broader IAM grant. EVERY AC1 sub-claim is asserted with its own literal `jq -e` query against the one
      `terraform show -json tf.plan` output, each exiting non-zero on violation:
      (a) https URI — `.planned_values.root_module.resources[] |
      select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") | .values.integration_uri |
      startswith("https://")`;
      (b) guard header — `... select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") |
      .values.request_parameters | has("append:header.X-Opcert-Guard")`;
      (c+d) parameters — `[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] |
      select(.type=="aws_ssm_parameter") | {n:.values.name, t:.values.type}] | (map(.n) |
      contains(["/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key","/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard"])) and
      all(.t=="SecureString")`;
      (e) route auth — `[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] |
      select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_route") | .values.authorization_type] | length > 0 and
      all(. == "AWS_IAM")` (aggregated with `all()` — a per-route stream would let `jq -e`
      pass on the last route alone);
      (f) key-param `lifecycle.ignore_changes` — `.configuration.root_module.resources[] |
      select(.address=="aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_ed25519_key") |
      .lifecycle_meta_arguments.ignore_changes? // empty | length > 0` (in `terraform show
      -json`'s `configuration` section lifecycle meta-arguments nest under
      `.lifecycle_meta_arguments`, not `.lifecycle`, and are absent from `planned_values`);
      (g) provider — `.configuration.provider_config | has("random")`.
- [ ] `terraform fmt -check` and `terraform validate` pass (asserted by a test or CI step);
      `terraform plan` against the provided account succeeds.
- [ ] `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` + nginx config gain the `opcert` service and the
      guarded `/opcert/` route; a test asserts the service entry, the env wiring
      (`REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`, parameter names), that the nginx route requires the
      `X-Opcert-Guard` header, and that the materialize script (per the `infra/gerrit/materialize-*.sh`
      precedent) renders the guard value from its SSM parameter — tested hermetically with a
      stub `aws` executable prepended to `PATH` that returns a fixture value (no live SSM or
      account access in unit tests). The test also asserts `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` calls
      the materialize script before `docker compose up`, that the script ends with
      `nginx -s reload` (host nginx), that the TEMPLATE itself contains the fail-closed guard
      map (`default 0;` + the zero-match-safe glob include) and the guarded `/opcert/`
      location, and that the Terraform integration URI uses the `https://` scheme.
- [ ] The deployed environment's public key and `env_id` are pinned in
      `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (with `added_at_log_position` per the epic's SUPERSEDING "Authoritative era anchor" resolution — the tickets-branch log-position era fields, not the superseded `added_at_commit` main-SHA form); a
      test asserts the entry parses and the `env_id` matches the compose service's
      `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`.
- [ ] [operator-attested] The service is deployed from `infra/` via the authenticated AWS CLI
      and the live checks pass: a SigV4-signed job round-trip succeeds; an unsigned request is
      rejected at API Gateway; a direct-to-origin request without the guard header is rejected —
      evidence: a comment recording the `terraform apply` outputs (API endpoint id/URL) and the
      captured round-trip + both rejections.
finding: The `hashicorp/random` provider is introduced (a new provider dependency) solely to generate the origin-guard value via `random_password`. This stores the guard value in Terraform state (readable by anyone with state access via `terraform state show`). The codebase's consistent pattern for all 12 secret values is operator-seeded out-of-band with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`, never stored in Terraform state. No positive rationale is given for why `hashicorp/random` is preferable to the established operator-seeded pattern for this secret value.
location: What section — Terraform, origin-guard provisioning path; 'the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED'

title: WS5: tests — idempotency, hot-path regression, concurrency, no_mutate, absent-marker
plan:
## Why
The design has sharp edges (hot-path cost, concurrency on the shared branch, rate-limit,
no_mutate, absent marker). Only tests lock the invariants so future ensures can be added
safely.

## What
The code under test is provided by this ticket's DEPENDENCIES (WS1 `ec29`, WS2 `891c`, WS3 `424c`),
all in the same feature stack: `rebar._store.ensures` exports `run_ensures(tracker)`,
`REGISTRY_IDS`/`registry_ids()`, `applied_ids(tracker)`, `maybe_emit_pending_hint(tracker)`,
`APPLIED_MARKER` (`.ensure-applied`). Add behavioral tests covering: git-level idempotency (`run_ensures` twice on a converged store
→ zero new commits); hot-path regression: a common mutation never invokes `rebar._store.ensures.run_ensures` and reads `.ensure-applied`
at most once per process; AND the write-path nudge `rebar._store.ensures.maybe_emit_pending_hint` itself spawns NO
subprocess at all (it only reads a marker file — unlike the old init-time git-config migration),
asserted by spying subprocess around a direct call to it; concurrency (two real OS processes running the sweep on the same checkout both complete —
serialized by the store write lock — and the store converges to a VALID, COMPLETE `.ensure-applied`
(parseable JSON containing exactly the registry ids), demonstrating the atomic temp+rename write
leaves no partial/invalid marker); no_mutate (library read-only fsck mutates nothing);
absent/garbage `.ensure-applied` (→ empty set → converges, no re-commit storm). The fsck byte-parity/help-golden tests are owned by WS3, and the pending-hint unit tests by WS2 — NOT here.

## Scope
In: the behavioral test suite for WS1-2 invariants.
Out: fsck byte-parity/help goldens (WS3), the feature code itself (WS1-3), docs (WS4).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Idempotency test: `run_ensures()` twice on a converged store makes zero new commits/pushes (git-log assertion)
- [ ] Hot-path regression test: (a) a ticket mutation (create/comment) never calls `run_ensures`; (b) it reads `.ensure-applied` at most once per process (the cached pending set); (c) the write-path nudge `rebar._store.ensures.maybe_emit_pending_hint` spawns NO subprocess — verified by spying `subprocess` (run/Popen/check_call/check_output/call) around a direct call to `rebar._store.ensures.maybe_emit_pending_hint` — so the hot path never shells out (contrast the old init-time git-config migration)
- [ ] Concurrency test: two real OS processes sweeping the same checkout both exit 0 (serialized by the store write lock, `_store/lock.py`) and the store converges to a VALID, COMPLETE `.ensure-applied` (parses as JSON == `set(rebar._store.ensures.REGISTRY_IDS)`) — the observable outcome of the atomic temp+rename write; the test asserts this positive convergence, not the (structurally-impossible-to-observe) absence of a torn write
- [ ] no_mutate test: a library read-only fsck surface (no_mutate=True) mutates nothing and never runs the sweep
- [ ] absent/garbage-marker test: a missing or garbage `.ensure-applied` degrades to empty-set (pending=all) without crashing
finding: The concurrency test (AC3) mandates 'two real OS processes' but provides no mechanism constraint enforcing subprocess-based implementation. Because `_store/lock.py` uses `fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB)`, which is per-PID (threads within the same process share flock state and a re-entrant acquisition always succeeds without blocking), a ThreadPoolExecutor-based implementation — consistent with the pattern used in `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_alert_store_concurrency.py` and `tests/unit/test_repo_snapshot.py` — would pass trivially without ever exercising real cross-process lock contention. The invariant under test would not be locked.
location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (Concurrency test)
suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The test MUST use real OS subprocesses (e.g., `subprocess.Popen` or a worker script invoked via `subprocess.run`) to drive both sweepers — a `ThreadPoolExecutor` with in-process calls would share the same PID and pass trivially because `fcntl.flock` does not block within one process. Follow the `tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py` pattern.'

title: Two-scope gate wiring, verdict plumbing, never-sign guard, exit 11, docs
plan:
Wire the two-scope gate posture across the three gates, plumb resolution-class + sanitized diagnostic through the two verdict-returning gates, define the exit-11 contract, own the shared-file (runner.py) module-size reconciliation, enforce never-certify structurally, wrap the MCP failure surface, and document the model. Lands LAST (wave 3); INTEGRATION OWNER for the seams the other children touch.

## Why
The three gates must share a systemic *block-but-retryable* posture while PRESERVING per-criterion fail-open, carry `resolution_class` + `diagnostic` so routing and troubleshooting both work, expose a distinct-but-grounded exit code, never sign a degraded result, and tell the human exactly what to do.

## What

### Two-scope posture
A **systemic** failure → block-but-retryable (exit 11, unsigned) across the gates; a **per-criterion** failure → drop-and-continue fail-open (one flaky finder among N must NOT block a claim — PRESERVED, regression-tested). Make code-review match plan-review's existing systemic block; turn completion's fail-closed-as-FAIL into a distinct retryable exit (NOT a verification FAIL, which means "your work is wrong").

### The single source of retryability: `LLMOutcome`
mamba's `classify_llm_failure` returns an `LLMOutcome` with `resolution_class` (the closed 8-value enum) AND a pre-computed `retryable: bool`. Everything downstream reuses BOTH — the write-sites persist both into `coverage`; the CLIs read the persisted `retryable` bool directly and never re-derive retryability by string-comparing the class name.

### Two data-flow shapes
- **Shape A — plan-review + code-review return a DEGRADED VERDICT DICT.**
  - **Write-site:** `gate_dispatch._degraded_plan_review_verdict` / `_degraded_code_review_verdict` read `outcome = getattr(error, 'outcome', None)` off the caught `error` and, WHEN PRESENT, set `coverage['resolution_class'] = outcome.resolution_class.value`, `coverage['diagnostic'] = outcome.diagnostic`, and `coverage['retryable'] = outcome.retryable`.
  - **Reachability (verified):** `produce_plan_review_verdict` calls the helper three ways: `preflight` failure (`error=exc`), mid-run `LLMUnavailableError` (`error=exc`), and the finders-failed tail (`error=res.error`, a STRING). TODAY only mamba's run-seam attaches `.outcome`; `PydanticAIRunner.preflight` raises WITHOUT it. So this story ALSO attaches `.outcome` in `preflight` — `raise LLMUnavailableError(...)` becomes `exc.outcome = classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(...)); raise exc` — so the common pre-call outage carries disposition too. The write-site reads `getattr(error,'outcome',None)`: present on the two exception paths (preflight now included), absent on the string finders-failed tail (→ plain INDETERMINATE, exit 2, correct — a finders-produced-nothing failure is not classified transient).
  - **code-review translation:** `rebar.llm.review_code` returns a `review_result` TRANSLATED from the gate `code_review_verdict` by `shim._verdict_to_review_result`. That translator is updated to COPY the gate verdict's `coverage` (with `resolution_class`/`retryable`/`diagnostic`) to the TOP-LEVEL `review_result['coverage']`, so `_review_code()` reads `result.get('coverage',{}).get('retryable')` directly (mirroring `_review_plan`).
- **Shape B — completion-verify FAILS by RAISING `LLMUnavailableError`** (no verdict dict). `transition_close._completion_precheck` (line ~174) has a broad `except Exception as exc: raise CommandError(...) from None` that TODAY discards the exception + its `.outcome`. Fix (SINGLE mechanism — no subclass, no new attribute): in `_completion_precheck`, before building the message, compute `_o = getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` and `rc = 11 if (_o and _o.retryable) else 1`, then `raise CommandError(<class-specific message>, returncode=rc) from None`. `CommandError` already carries `returncode`, and `transition.py` already returns `exc.returncode` to the process — so exit 11 propagates with no plumbing change. There are TWO shape-B CLI surfaces with DISTINCT mechanisms: (a) the `rebar close`/transition path (`close_ticket`→`_completion_precheck`→`CommandError(returncode=11)`, above); (b) the standalone `rebar verify-completion` (`_verify_completion()`), whose OWN `except llm.LLMError as exc: return 1` arm (`_llm_commands.py` ~line 305) catches `LLMUnavailableError` directly — so THAT arm is updated to `_o=getattr(exc,'outcome',None); return 11 if (_o and _o.retryable) else 1`. Both shape-B arms also write the sanitized diagnostic to the session log (see below).

### Exit-code-11 contract
Retryable (`_o.retryable`, i.e. `WAIT_AND_RETRY`/`RETRY_NOW`) → exit **11** ("transient — retry", NEW). Any other non-null disposition → the gate's EXISTING INDETERMINATE exit. Null disposition → the existing PASS/BLOCK exit.
- **Exit 2 is NOT redefined.** `review-plan` ALREADY exits 2 for INDETERMINATE (pinned by arawana); UNCHANGED. Only the retryable subset is peeled to 11.
- **CLI sites (`_llm_commands.py`):** `_review_plan()` (shape A; tail currently `0/1/2` — extended to add 11) and `_review_code()` (shape A; TODAY always returns 0 on any verdict — updated to distinguish `0/1/2/11` off the preserved `coverage`). Shape B surfaces through TWO CLI entrypoints: `rebar verify-completion` (`_verify_completion()`) AND the `rebar close` / `rebar transition … closed` path (which reaches `close_ticket` → `_completion_precheck`; `transition.py` returns the `CommandError.returncode`, so exit 11 flows there too). `_review()` (the per-ticket `review_ticket` advisory reviewer) is NOT a site.
- **Message delivery:** the class-specific message (table below) is printed to STDERR as the line `llm-degrade: <resolution_class> — <message>` — shape B embeds it in the `CommandError` message; shape A renders it in `_review_plan`/`_review_code` from `coverage['resolution_class']` via a `_RESOLUTION_MESSAGES` lookup dict (the table below, keyed by class name).
- **Worked example:** a 429 degraded plan-review carries `coverage.retryable=True, resolution_class='WAIT_AND_RETRY'` → `_review_plan` exits 11; a 429 during `rebar close` raises `LLMUnavailableError`, `_completion_precheck` reads `.outcome.retryable` → `CommandError(returncode=11)` → the close exits 11 (fail-closed, unsigned).

### Per-class actionable messages (ResolutionClass → stderr text)
| ResolutionClass | Message |
|---|---|
| `WAIT_AND_RETRY` | Transient provider overload/rate-limit — wait for the backoff window, then retry. |
| `RETRY_NOW` | Transient connection blip — retry immediately. |
| `INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS` | Provider usage/quota ceiling hit — raise the provider limit or wait for the reset. |
| `CHANGE_SETTINGS` | The request exceeded a configured bound (tokens/steps/timeout) — adjust it and retry. |
| `CHANGE_INPUT` | The input was rejected (too large / malformed) — reduce or fix it and retry. |
| `CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL` | The model/provider is unavailable or refused — switch model/provider. |
| `FIX_AGENT_DESIGN` | An agent-construction bug (no tools / bad output contract) — fix the op wiring. |
| `NEEDS_INVESTIGATION` | Unclassified failure — inspect the sanitized diagnostic. |

### Verdict-schema field (the two verdict-returning gates only)
Add OPTIONAL `coverage.resolution_class` (enum, 8 values), `coverage.retryable` (bool), `coverage.diagnostic` (sanitized object) to `plan_review_verdict.schema.json` and `code_review_verdict.schema.json` (their `coverage` is already `additionalProperties:true`, so this is documentary; readers tolerate unknown keys — forward/rollback-safe). **`completion_verdict.schema.json` is NOT changed** (shape B surfaces via the raised `.outcome`, no verdict dict — a field there would be dead). Sole writer of the schema edits.

### Session-log diagnostic write (calling convention, both shapes)
At each degrade site (shape A helpers AND the shape-B `_completion_precheck` arm), call `rebar.append_session_log(entry, summary=…, relates_to=<ticket_id>)` where `entry = json.dumps(sanitized_diagnostic, sort_keys=True)` (the sanitized dict, never the raw exception), `summary = f"llm-degrade {resolution_class} on <gate>"`, `relates_to` = the reviewed ticket id when available. Wrapped best-effort (`try/except Exception`): a session-log failure must NEVER fail the gate (regression-tested).

### Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck (at the CALLER, not generic `sign_manifest`)
`signing.sign_manifest(ticket_id, manifest)` is a generic multi-caller primitive with no verdict in scope, so the guards live at the SIGN-DECISION callers:
- **Never-sign guard:** at the plan-review sign path (`review_plan`) and the completion sign path (`close_ticket`), refuse to CALL `sign_manifest` when `verdict.get("verdict") != "PASS"` OR `verdict.get("coverage",{}).get("resolution_class")` is set. This makes the existing "degraded ⇒ unsigned" invariant STRUCTURAL (regression-tested). It introduces no new unsigned state (INDETERMINATE is already unsigned).
- **Pre-sign fingerprint recheck:** "material fingerprint" = the committed head SHA the verdict was verified against, which is ALREADY carried in the returned manifest as its `verified-at-sha:<sha>` step (`_verdict_manifest`). In `close_ticket`, INSIDE the write lock, immediately before calling `sign_manifest`, extract that sha from the manifest and compare it to a fresh `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` read; on mismatch (code drifted during the retry/timeout-widened verify→sign window) SKIP signing. The ticket STILL closes (the transition already committed) — this is the same closed-without-signature outcome as `--force-close`/`local`-source, so the close command EXITS 0 with a stderr warning "closed without a completion signature (code drifted between verify and sign)". Verify itself stays OUTSIDE the write lock (`transition_close` ordering preserved; asserted).

### MCP structured failure return (`_mcp_llm.py`)
The write-gated MCP tools (`review_plan`, `review_code`, `verify_completion`) in `src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py` today `return rebar.llm.X(...)` and let a raised `LLMError`/`LLMUnavailableError` propagate as a FastMCP tool ERROR (opaque to the agent). Wrap each body: on a raised `LLMError`, RETURN a structured result dict `{"error": str(exc), "resolution_class": <cls or None>, "retryable": <bool>, "diagnostic": <sanitized>}` (disposition read from `getattr(exc,'outcome',None)`), so the driving agent can branch on `retryable` instead of parsing an error string. (Shape-A tools already return a degraded verdict dict; this closes the shape-B/hard-error raise.)

### Integration ownership (shared files)
runner.py's module-size allowlisting (allowlist + ceilings 850 + `docs/architecture.md` remedy row) ALREADY LANDED with the anole commit in this stack (visible at `--ref HEAD`), so it is NOT a deliverable here. This story owns the final runner.py reconciliation (the `preflight` `.outcome` attachment above) and the taxonomy/retry/timeout docs, and lands LAST.

## Scope
- `gate_dispatch.py` degrade write-sites; `code_review/shim.py` (`_verdict_to_review_result` coverage preservation); `transition_close.py` (`_completion_precheck` returncode-11 + shape-B session-log; `close_ticket` never-sign guard + in-lock fingerprint recheck) preserving verify-outside-lock ordering; `_llm_commands.py` exit-11 mapping (`_review_plan`/`_review_code`/`_verify_completion`); `_mcp_llm.py` structured-failure wrap; `signing.py` (only if a caller-side helper is factored — the guard lives at the callers); the two verdict JSON schemas; `docs/exit-codes.md`; `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_exit_codes.py`; `docs/adr/`.
- NOT in scope: removing/weakening per-criterion fail-open; changing `completion_verdict.schema.json`; changing `sign_manifest`'s signature.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `_review_plan()` exits 11 when the verdict's `coverage.retryable` is true.
- [ ] `_review_plan()` exits 2 (existing INDETERMINATE) when INDETERMINATE and `coverage.retryable` is false/absent.
- [ ] `_review_code()` distinguishes `0/1/2/11` off the preserved `coverage` (it currently always returns 0).
- [ ] `shim._verdict_to_review_result` preserves `coverage.resolution_class`/`retryable`/`diagnostic` onto the returned `review_result`.
- [ ] `_completion_precheck` raises `CommandError(returncode=11)` when the caught exception's `.outcome.retryable` is true, else `returncode=1`.
- [ ] `PydanticAIRunner.preflight` attaches `.outcome` (via `classify_llm_failure`) to its raised `LLMUnavailableError`, so preflight outages carry disposition.
