- [ ] `_verify_completion()`'s `except llm.LLMError` arm returns 11 when `.outcome.retryable` is true, else 1.
- [ ] The `rebar close` / transition CLI path surfaces that returncode (exit 11) unchanged via `transition.py`.
- [ ] A degraded / INDETERMINATE verdict is emitted unsigned (never-sign guard at the caller refuses to call `sign_manifest`; regression test).
- [ ] The pre-sign head-SHA fingerprint (from the manifest's `verified-at-sha:` step) is re-checked inside the write lock immediately before signing; a mismatch closes unsigned and the close command exits 0 with a stderr warning (test for the mismatch path).
- [ ] Verify remains OUTSIDE the write lock (`transition_close` ordering asserted by a test).
- [ ] A degraded verdict/close prints the class-specific message (from the table) to stderr.
- [ ] The sanitized diagnostic is written to the session log at BOTH the shape-A and shape-B degrade sites via `append_session_log(json.dumps(diagnostic, sort_keys=True), summary=…, relates_to=…)`, best-effort (a session-log failure does not fail the gate; regression test).
- [ ] `coverage.resolution_class` (enum), `coverage.retryable` (bool), `coverage.diagnostic` (object) are optional properties on `plan_review_verdict.schema.json` and `code_review_verdict.schema.json`; `completion_verdict.schema.json` is unchanged.
- [ ] The shape-A write-site copies `resolution_class`/`retryable`/`diagnostic` from the caught error's `.outcome` when present (outage paths); the string-error finders-failed path yields a plain INDETERMINATE.
- [ ] Per-criterion fail-open preserved: a single flaky finder among N does not block (regression test).
- [ ] `_mcp_llm.py`'s `review_plan`/`review_code`/`verify_completion` return a structured failure dict `{error,resolution_class,retryable,diagnostic}` on a raised `LLMError` instead of propagating a FastMCP tool error (test for a degraded sub-path).
- [ ] `docs/exit-codes.md` gains a recorded-decision entry defining exit 11; `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_exit_codes.py` pins 11.
- [ ] The exit-11 ADR (sections: Context, Decision — 11=block-but-retryable distinct from 2, Consumers+additive-safety, Rollback) enumerates consumers: the driving agent/human (docs/exit-codes.md), CI (`.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml`, `test.yml` — any-non-zero-is-failure, never invokes the gate CLIs so 11 is invisible to Verified), the review-bot (`src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` — votes off the verdict dict, tolerates open coverage keys); each additive-safe; rollback = retryable falls back to the existing INDETERMINATE exit (no data migration).
- [ ] The diagnostic-sanitization ADR (sections: Context, Allowlist + redaction rules, Threat model — keys/PII/hex/base64/email must never leak, Test coverage) is authored.
- [ ] `make lint`, `make typecheck`, `make test` are all green.

## Verified structural facts (experiment log — repo snapshot, offline)
```
Shape-B: transition_close.py::_completion_precheck (line ~174 broad except → CommandError from None discards .outcome; fix sets returncode from .outcome.retryable). CommandError (_commands/_seam.py) already carries returncode; transition.py returns exc.returncode (lines ~359/467).
Shape-A: gate_dispatch.py::_degraded_plan_review_verdict/_degraded_code_review_verdict. Called 3x: preflight (error=exc), mid-run (error=exc) → .outcome present; finders-failed (error=res.error STRING) → no .outcome → plain INDETERMINATE.
code-review CLI: rebar.llm.review_code → shim._verdict_to_review_result (translates verdict→review_result; must preserve coverage). _review_code currently returns 0 for any verdict.
CLI arms: _llm_commands.py::_review_plan (tail 0/1/2), _review_code, _verify_completion. rebar close/transition → close_ticket → _completion_precheck.
Schemas (coverage additionalProperties:true): plan_review_verdict.schema.json, code_review_verdict.schema.json (edited); completion_verdict.schema.json unchanged.
Signing: signing.py::sign_manifest(ticket_id, manifest) generic multi-caller; guard + head_sha recheck live at callers (review_plan sign path, close_ticket) — verify OUTSIDE write lock.
MCP: _mcp_llm.py review_plan/review_code/verify_completion return rebar.llm.X(...) (raise propagates as FastMCP error today).
Consumers: CI .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml + test.yml (make targets, not gate CLIs); review-bot src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py (verdict dict, not exit codes).
Exit contract: docs/exit-codes.md (0/1/2/10 frozen 2026-06-09) pinned by tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_exit_codes.py; 11 additive.
```
Additive/rollback-safe: schema fields optional; exit 11 additive. Records the two NEW ADRs (exit-11, diagnostic-sanitization); transport-retry (0037) + async-watchdog (0038) already exist. Owns runner.py module-size reconciliation. Lands LAST.
finding: The plan asserts 'rebar review (code-review) read[s] shape (A)' and lists `rebar review` as a site to update with exit-code mapping in `_llm_commands.py`. But `_review()` (the `rebar review` handler) calls `llm.review_ticket()` — it is a TICKET reviewer, not the code-review gate. The code-review gate CLI is `_review_code()` / `rebar review-code`. The plan appears to conflate the two, risking that implementers apply exit-11 mapping to the wrong command.
location: What section: 'Sites to update, each with a CLI test: rebar review-plan (_llm_commands.py, currently 0/1/2) and rebar review (code-review) read shape (A)'

title: Ticket audit-trail UI (optional, disabled by default)
plan:
## Context

Auditors need to review, for one work ticket, what the automated LLM review gates decided: the
latest plan review, the completion-verification result, and the latest associated code review,
alongside the ticket's current details — with the ability to navigate the history of plan and code
reviews and to see each review's full four-pass breakdown (pass-1 findings, pass-2 verification,
pass-3 determination with thresholds, pass-4 coaching). The audit data is made lossless and readable
by the data-enrichment epic (a hard dependency; see the linked `depends_on` epic).

This epic delivers that as an OPTIONAL, disabled-by-default, read-only local web UI.

## Data contract (the named interface Epic 1 must expose)

This UI is a pure function of the enriched fields the data-enrichment epic (Epic 1) persists per
finding and per criterion. The two epics share THIS named interface — Epic 1 delivers it (via story
A's plan-review v2 `_slim`, story B's code-review v2 payload, and story C's completion `criteria[]`),
Epic 2 consumes it. The minimal enriched field set is:

| Consumer (UI region)          | Source (Epic 1)                        | Required fields                                                                                                                              |
|-------------------------------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Finding-provenance (per finding) | plan-review v2 (story A) / code-review v2 (story B) | `finding`, `criteria`, `location`, `evidence`, `scenarios`, `impact`, `suggested_fix`, `verification`, `validity`, `priority`, `decision`, `reason`, `block_threshold`, `blocking_enabled` |
| Round history + convergence   | plan-/code-review sidecar history      | full retained per-round sidecars (newest-first) + each round's `coaching[]` (`{move_id, finding_refs}`)                                        |
| Threshold meter (per finding) | plan-/code-review v2                    | the finding's resolved (per-finding) `block_threshold` + `blocking_enabled` + `priority` + `decision`                                          |
| Completion panel (per criterion) | completion `criteria[]` (story C)     | `criteria[]` of `{criterion, met, citation, kind}` where `kind ∈ {codebase-verifiable, operator-attested}`                                     |

`block_threshold` is the PER-FINDING resolved value each finding was judged against (persisted by
stories A/B), NOT a round-level scalar. The completion panel's per-criterion rows have NO source in
the pre-enrichment completion verdict (findings are failures-only); they are PRODUCED by story C's
`criteria[]`. This epic MUST NOT proceed to render a region until its Epic 1 field source has landed.

## Approach rationale

Server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2, REUSING the `reviewbot` extra's already-shipped web stack rather
than adding a second web framework, bound to loopback only, with no client build step. This fits a
read-only, low-traffic, single-user audit view and reuses proven in-repo machinery (the reviewbot
FastAPI app is the established "thin, lazily-imported, never-required-by-core" precedent). The
alternative of a static HTML export was weighed and rejected for v1: the audit data is naturally
navigable (history rounds, drill-in) and replay is cheap, which a point-in-time static dump serves
poorly.

## Success Criteria

- A `rebar audit serve` subcommand starts a read-only web server, gated OFF by default, that renders
  a ticket's audit trail (ticket details + plan/completion/code panels), navigable across the
  retained plan- and code-review history, with each review's four passes shown including the
  per-finding resolved thresholds and the completion `criteria[]` rows.
- With the feature disabled (default) and its extra not installed, rebar behaves exactly as today
  (no new import cost; `import rebar` unaffected).

## Scope

- IN: the optional web-server surface (subcommand, config flag, `[ui]` extra, lazy import, loopback
  binding) and the per-ticket audit page rendering.
- OUT: any data-model/sidecar change (owned by the dependency epic — the Data-contract fields above
  are DELIVERED by Epic 1, not by this epic); network exposure / multi-user auth; write/edit actions
  (the UI is strictly read-only).

## Compatibility & rollback

The whole feature is default-off (`[ui] enabled = false`) and its web deps live in a new
`nava-rebar[ui]` extra, lazy-imported — so it is inert when disabled and when the extra is absent.
Rollback is turning the flag off / not installing the extra; no data or schema change.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Both child stories are closed.
- [ ] The page is navigable across the retained review history for BOTH plan review AND code review:
      an OLDER round (not just the latest) of EACH can be selected and its distinct content renders.
      Oracle: a live end-to-end test seeding ≥2 plan-review rounds AND ≥2 code-review rounds on one
      ticket, selecting the older round in each gate, and asserting each renders its (distinct from
      latest) content.
- [ ] With the feature enabled, `rebar audit serve` serves a ticket audit page showing the ticket
      details and the plan/completion/code panels with pass-1..4 detail, per-finding resolved
      thresholds, and the completion `criteria[]` rows. Oracle: a live end-to-end test that starts
      the server and asserts on the rendered response.
- [ ] With the feature disabled (default), no web dependency is imported and all existing commands
      behave unchanged. Oracle: a test asserting the server is not started and `import rebar` needs
      no web dep.
finding: The plan states it 'REUSES the `reviewbot` extra's already-shipped web stack' as part of its approach rationale, but the `reviewbot` extra does not include Jinja2 (`reviewbot = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]`). Jinja2 is an optional FastAPI dependency. The plan correctly names a new `nava-rebar[ui]` extra, but the rationale conflates 'reusing reviewbot's framework choice' with 'reusing reviewbot's declared extra' — the [ui] extra will still need an explicit `jinja2` (or `fastapi[all]`) dependency declaration that has no precedent in the repo.
location: Approach rationale section: 'REUSING the `reviewbot` extra's already-shipped web stack'
suggested_fix: Add `"jinja2>=3"` (or `"fastapi[all]"`) to the planned `[ui]` extra definition and update the rationale sentence to: 'REUSING the `reviewbot` extra's framework choice (FastAPI + uvicorn) rather than adding a second web framework; Jinja2 is added to the new [ui] extra as its server-rendering layer.'

title: Cupid enrichment op: LLM ticket-digest extractor + digest schema
plan:
Cupid enrichment op: LLM ticket-digest extractor + digest schema.

## Why
Semantic overlap detection needs a compact, normalized per-ticket representation that
catches paraphrase ("login broken" vs "users can't authenticate") WITHOUT embeddings,
keeping rebar on its Anthropic-only, no-heavy-ML grain. The Cupid pattern
(arXiv:2308.10022) uses an LLM as a one-time feature extractor. DERISKED: the recall
experiment (see epic experiment comment) showed digest-BM25F retrieves 8/8 heavy
paraphrases at rank 1 (recall@20 = 1.0); the digest is the load-bearing artifact for the
whole feature.

## What
A new LLM operation `rebar.llm.enrich(ticket_id)` returning a validated dedup-digest
wrapper, plus its output schema and prompt. It is a **structured single-turn** op:
assemble the ticket's canonical text -> load the prompt ->
`RunRequest(mode="structured", output_schema="ticket_digest", execution_mode="single_turn")`
-> `get_runner(cfg, override=runner).run(req)` (the runner validates the structured
response against the `ticket_digest` schema via `finalize_outcome`/`validate_structured`)
-> the op post-processes the proposition count and returns the wrapper.

Modeled on the structured single-turn pattern in
`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py::pass1_chunk` (which builds
`RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn")`) — **NOT** on
`src/rebar/llm/completion.py` (that is a *gate* op that resolves a read-root and delegates
verdict production to the workflow engine; it contains no `RunRequest`), and **NOT** on
`operations.py::review_ticket` (which uses `mode="findings"`/`execution_mode="agentic"`,
the opposite of what we want). Because `enrich` is single-turn with no file/repo tools it
does **NOT** need `gate_source.resolve_gate_handle` / `gate_read_root` or snapshot
materialization; it builds cfg via `LLMConfig.from_env(repo_root=...)` (or an explicit
`config=`), assembles the ticket text, and runs.

The digest schema (`ticket_digest`):
`{problem_keywords: string[], component_or_area: string, key_entities: string[], propositions: string[]}`;
`propositions` = 2-6 atomic problem/repro statements; logs/stack-traces/code are discarded
by the prompt. The payload is deterministic (no timestamps/uuids) so the stored record is
stable modulo LLM wording.

### Op signature (with a body-injection escape hatch for offline tests)
`enrich(ticket_id=None, *, text=None, repo_root=None, config=None, runner=None)`. Exactly
one of `ticket_id` / `text` must be given (else `ValueError`):
- `ticket_id` given -> the op reads the ticket via the established `rebar.llm` facade
  `from rebar import _reads; state = _reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=repo_root)` (the
  same layering-correct pattern used by `completion.py:294`, `operations.py:82`,
  `plan_review/orchestrator.py:168`), then assembles the source text with a TRIVIAL INLINE
  join of `state["title"]` + `state["description"]` + each `state["comments"][*]["body"]`
  ("\n"-joined, skipping empties) directly in `enrich.py`. It does NOT import
  `rebar._engine_support.gates._ticket_text` — no `rebar.llm.*` module imports
  `_engine_support.gates`, and doing so would be the first layering violation in the package;
  the join is a couple of lines and needs no private helper. (S1/`2d0f`, which computes a
  content-hash, owns its OWN local normalizer for hashing; enrich's inline join only feeds the
  LLM prompt and need not be byte-identical to it.)
- `text` given -> the op uses that string directly as the source, bypassing the store read
  entirely. This is the injection seam the offline `test_enrich_quality` and other unit
  tests use to feed a fixture `body` without a live ticket store. (It is a real API on the
  op, not a test-only monkeypatch.)

### System prompt + instructions assembly (query context)
- **system_prompt** is resolved from the packaged prompt FILE `reviewers/ticket_digest.md`
  (file stem `ticket_digest` -> prompt id `ticket-digest`, underscores normalized to dashes
  exactly as `ticket_quality.md` -> `ticket-quality`) via the da27 prompt machinery —
  `prompts.get_prompt("ticket-digest", repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` then
  `prompts.resolve_prompt(prompt, {...}, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` — mirroring
  `plan_review/passes.py::_resolve_system`. No inline prompt string constants; a project
  `.rebar/prompts/ticket-digest.md` override wins.
- **instructions** are the source text resolved above (from `ticket_id` or the injected
  `text`), rendered as plain text. No graph, no file contents. The PROMPT (not the op)
  instructs the model to discard any logs/stack-traces/code that appear in the body — so log
  exclusion is a prompt-encoded guarantee, verified statically by `test_prompt_excludes_logs`.

## Return shape & processing order (grounded in `findings.finalize_outcome`)
`get_runner(cfg, override=runner).run(req)` returns, for `mode="structured"`, the dict
`{**payload, "runner": <str>, "model": <str|None>, "trace_id": <str|None>}` where `payload`
is the model's structured output already validated against the `ticket_digest` schema
(`findings.finalize_outcome` -> `validate_structured`, `findings.py:321`). So the run result
carries the four digest fields PLUS three provenance keys — it is NOT itself a bare digest.
The op therefore:
1. Selects the digest by field name:
   `digest = {k: run_result[k] for k in ("problem_keywords", "component_or_area", "key_entities", "propositions")}`
   (dropping the `runner`/`model`/`trace_id` provenance keys).
2. Post-processes the `propositions` list ONLY: if `len > overlap_propositions_max`, truncate
   to max; if `len < overlap_propositions_min`, keep them all and set
   `low_proposition_count=True`; it never raises on count.
3. Returns the wrapper
   `{"digest": <ticket_digest dict, exactly the four fields>, "low_proposition_count": bool}`.
   `low_proposition_count` lives on the op's RETURN object only — never a fifth field of the
   persisted `ticket_digest`. The `digest` value validates against `ticket_digest`.

## Error contract (resolves AC "clean error, no partial write")
- A shape-invalid structured payload (valid JSON, wrong shape — e.g. `propositions` missing
  or non-string) raises `FindingsError` from `validate_structured` (`findings.py`) INSIDE
  the runner's `run()`, before the op sees any result; the op never catch-and-partial-writes,
  so no record is persisted. This is the path `test_bad_shape` exercises with a FakeRunner
  seeded with a schema-invalid `structured=` dict.
- An absent structured response at runtime (the live model emits no parseable structured
  object) is raised as `rebar.llm.errors.StructuredOutputError` by `finalize_outcome`
  (`findings.py:305`, `structured is None` branch) — a framework guarantee on the live path
  (FakeRunner cannot reproduce it: `FakeRunner(structured=None)` falls through to the
  findings branch, `runner.py:120-137`), so it is asserted at the `finalize_outcome` unit
  level, not fabricated through FakeRunner.
- Absent LLM (no `[agents]` extra / no API key) raises `LLMUnavailableError`/`LLMConfigError`
  (subclasses of `LLMError`) cleanly via the runner preflight/config path; `test_absent_llm`.
- The op defines NO new exception type — it reuses the framework's (`FindingsError`,
  `StructuredOutputError`, `LLMUnavailableError`).

## Config-bound threading (resolves the zero-arg-builder gap)
The proposition bound is NOT enforced through the `register_contract` schema builder (the
zero-arg `Callable[[], type]` cannot see config). Instead: (a) the prompt instructs the
model to emit MIN..MAX propositions, and (b) the op post-validates the parsed count
(truncate to max if more; keep + flag `low_proposition_count` if fewer; never raise). The
MIN/MAX bounds are LLM-feature tunables on `LLMConfig` (`[tool.rebar.llm]`, env
`REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MIN` / `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MAX`, read in
`LLMConfig.from_env` via the `_llm_int` helper) — `_config_schema.py` reserves the `llm.*`
layer, so these must NOT go on `VerifyConfig`.

## Scope (exact edit targets)
- `src/rebar/llm/config.py`: add module constants `DEFAULT_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MIN = 2` /
  `DEFAULT_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MAX = 6`, dataclass fields
  `LLMConfig.overlap_propositions_min` / `overlap_propositions_max`, and two `_llm_int(...)`
  lines in `from_env` (env `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX`, file keys
  `overlap_propositions_min/max`).
- New op module `src/rebar/llm/enrich.py` defining
  `enrich(ticket_id=None, *, text=None, repo_root=None, config=None, runner=None)` (exactly
  one of `ticket_id`/`text`; `text` bypasses the store read — see Op signature above).
- Export: add `from rebar.llm.enrich import enrich` to `src/rebar/llm/__init__.py` and add
  `"enrich"` to the `# operations` group of its `__all__` (NOT `src/rebar/__init__.py`; the
  core package must not import `rebar.llm`).
- Prompt `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ticket_digest.md` (stem `ticket_digest` -> id
  `ticket-digest`), front-matter `schema_version: 1`, `execution_mode: single_turn`,
  `outputs: ticket_digest`, `category: enrich` (NOT `review`, so it is not indexed/selected
  as a reviewer — `reviewers/index.json` lists only `category=="review"`), and a body that
  explicitly instructs the model to discard logs/stack-traces/code and emit MIN..MAX atomic
  propositions. The valid `execution_mode` enum is `single_turn|agentic`
  (`prompting/prompts.py:74`); `1-TURN` is invalid. If a new packaged prompt must be
  enumerated in a `_packaged_prompt_files()`/catalog manifest, add `ticket_digest.md` there.
- Output schema `src/rebar/schemas/ticket_digest.schema.json` (all four fields required;
  `problem_keywords`/`key_entities`/`propositions` = arrays of non-empty strings;
  `component_or_area` a non-empty string; `propositions` `minItems: 1`). WIRE IT: in
  `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py` add `TICKET_DIGEST = "ticket_digest"`, register
  `OUTPUT_SCHEMAS["enrich"] = TICKET_DIGEST`, and add `TICKET_DIGEST` to `__all__` — this
  satisfies `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_coverage.py::test_every_schema_file_is_wired`
  (every schema file must appear in `OUTPUT_SCHEMAS.values() | exempt`). The synthetic
  `"enrich"` key has no CLI `--output` arm (like `"verify_completion"`), so the CLI-coverage
  invariants do not drive it.
- Contract registration + bootstrap: define the builder `ticket_digest_response_model()`
  **inside `src/rebar/llm/contracts.py`** (co-located with `completion_verdict_response_model`,
  pydantic imported inside the body), and register it at the BOTTOM of `contracts.py` with
  `register_contract("ticket_digest", ticket_digest_response_model)` next to the two built-ins
  (`review_result`, `completion_verdict`). Registration is guaranteed-before-use by
  **co-location**: `response_model_for` is defined in `contracts.py`, so any code path that
  calls `response_model_for("ticket_digest")` has necessarily imported `contracts.py`, which
  executes the module body — including the bottom `register_contract(...)` line — first. (This
  is the correct mechanism; `rebar.llm.__init__` does NOT import `contracts` at module top —
  the runner imports it lazily — so the guarantee is co-location, not startup import.) Defining
  the builder in `contracts.py` (not `enrich.py`) also avoids a `contracts -> enrich` import
  cycle. `enrich.py` merely references the schema by the string name `"ticket_digest"`.

## Dependencies
None (leaf). S1/S2/S7 consume the op + the `ticket_digest` schema as sibling stories.

## Testing
- Unit `tests/unit/test_enrich.py` (all use `FakeRunner(structured=...)` — NO live LLM):
  - `test_schema_valid` — `enrich()` with a FakeRunner seeded with a valid digest returns
    `{digest, low_proposition_count}` and `digest` validates against `ticket_digest`.
  - `test_no_nondeterministic_fields` — the returned `digest` has exactly the four schema
    fields (no timestamps/uuids/provenance keys leaked from the run result).
  - `test_propositions_bounded` — FakeRunner returning >max propositions => truncated to max;
    <min => kept with `low_proposition_count=True`, no raise.
  - `test_bad_shape` — FakeRunner seeded with a schema-invalid `structured=` dict (e.g.
    `propositions` omitted) => `FindingsError` raised inside the run, and nothing is returned/
    written (no partial write).
  - `test_absent_llm` — with the real (pydantic_ai) runner and no `[agents]`/key, preflight/run
    raises `LLMUnavailableError`/`LLMConfigError` cleanly.
  - `test_enrich_exported` — `from rebar.llm import enrich` succeeds AND
    `rebar.llm.contracts.response_model_for("ticket_digest")` returns the registered
    ticket_digest model (not the default findings model) — covers the export + contract-
    registration Scope items.
  - `test_prompt_excludes_logs` — STATIC assertion that the packaged
    `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` prompt text contains the explicit "discard
    logs/stack-traces/code" directive (the honest place the exclusion guarantee lives — a
    FakeRunner cannot prove model behaviour).
  - `test_prompt_frontmatter` — loads the prompt via `prompts.get_prompt("ticket-digest")`
    and asserts `execution_mode == "single_turn"`, `outputs == "ticket_digest"`, and that it
    is NOT a reviewer (`category != "review"`, absent from `reviewers/index.json`) — so the
    enrich prompt is never selected as a plan/ticket reviewer.
  - `test_finalize_absent_structured` — a `finalize_outcome`-level unit asserting that a
    `structured_response=None` outcome raises `StructuredOutputError` (the live-path guarantee
    FakeRunner cannot reproduce).
- Quality fixture test `test_enrich_quality` — **offline + deterministic, exercises the real
  `enrich()` op path** (assembly -> run -> select -> post-process -> wrap). Fixtures live at
  `tests/fixtures/enrich_quality/*.json`, >=6 files COMMITTED as deliverables of this story,
  each shaped
  `{"ticket_id": str, "body": str (one is a ~4000-token body), "digest": {<pre-generated ticket_digest, four fields, captured once from a real enrich run>}, "gold": [<expected entity/keyword strings>]}`.
  The test FIRST asserts cardinality — `assert len(fixtures) >= 6` (so an empty/missing
  fixture dir fails loudly rather than passing vacuously). Then for each fixture it builds a
  `FakeRunner(structured=fixture["digest"])` and calls `enrich(text=fixture["body"], runner=<fake>)`
  (the `text=` injection seam — no store read), and asserts deterministic string-set
  membership on the RESULT: >=1 `gold` string appears (case-insensitive) in
  `result["digest"]["key_entities"]` union `result["digest"]["problem_keywords"]`. This both
  exercises `enrich()` and stays deterministic (the canned digests are real captured model
  outputs; regenerate only when the prompt changes).
- Gated live regenerator `test_enrich_quality_live` — `@pytest.mark.skipif` unless
  `REBAR_RUN_LLM_EVAL=1`; calls the real model over the fixture bodies and re-asserts gold
  membership (the path that regenerates the canned `digest` blocks). NEVER runs in CI.
- Proving command: `python -m pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py -q` (self-contained;
  fixtures committed, live test skipped, so it never needs a generation step first).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `rebar.llm.enrich(ticket_id)` returns `{digest, low_proposition_count}`, and the
  `digest` field (exactly the four schema fields) validates against `ticket_digest`;
  `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_schema_valid` and `::test_no_nondeterministic_fields`.
- [ ] `from rebar.llm import enrich` succeeds and `contracts.response_model_for("ticket_digest")`
  returns the registered model (not the default findings model);
  `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_enrich_exported`.
- [ ] The packaged `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` prompt encodes the logs/stack-traces/code exclusion
  directive; `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_prompt_excludes_logs`.
- [ ] The `propositions` count is enforced in the op (>max truncate to max; <min keep + flag,
  no raise); `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_propositions_bounded`.
- [ ] A shape-invalid structured payload raises `FindingsError` with no partial write
  (`::test_bad_shape`); an absent structured response raises `StructuredOutputError`
  (`::test_finalize_absent_structured`); absent-LLM raises `LLMUnavailableError`
  (`::test_absent_llm`).
- [ ] The quality fixture test asserts `len(fixtures) >= 6` (fails loudly if the committed
  fixture dir is empty/missing) and, for each, calls `enrich(text=fixture["body"], runner=<fake>)`
  with a FakeRunner seeded with that fixture's captured digest, asserting >=1 gold
  entity/keyword appears in `key_entities` union `problem_keywords` (deterministic; exercises
  the op path via the `text=` injection seam); `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_enrich_quality`.
- [ ] The packaged prompt loads as `ticket-digest` with `execution_mode: single_turn`,
  `outputs: ticket_digest`, and is NOT a reviewer (`category != review`);
  `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_prompt_frontmatter`.
finding: The plan specifies the ticket read as `reads.show_state(ticket_id, ...)` + `gates._ticket_text(state)`, but no code in `rebar.llm.*` ever imports from `rebar._engine_support.gates` (where `_ticket_text` lives), and `rebar._engine_support.reads.show_state` requires a `tracker: str` argument, not `repo_root=`. The established, layering-correct pattern across all other `rebar.llm` ops is `from rebar import _reads` then `_reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=repo_root)` (the public facade that also applies the read-freshness policy). Importing `_engine_support.gates._ticket_text` from a new `rebar.llm.enrich` module would be a first-ever layering violation in this package; the text assembly (`title + description + comments`) is trivially inline-able without the private helper.
location: Op signature section / "ticket_id given -> the op reads the ticket via `reads.show_state(ticket_id, ...)` and derives the source text with `gates._ticket_text(state)`"
suggested_fix: In the Op signature section, replace: "the op reads the ticket via `reads.show_state(ticket_id, ...)` and derives the source text with `gates._ticket_text(state)` (title+description+comments)" with: "the op reads the ticket via `from rebar import _reads; state = _reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=<resolved>)` and assembles the source text inline as `'\n'.join(filter(None, [state.get('title'), state.get('description'), *(c.get('body','') for c in state.get('comments',[]) or [])]))` — matching `_ticket_text`'s semantics without importing the private engine-support helper."

title: S1 — Optional Streamable-HTTP transport for rebar-mcp + transport hardening
plan:
# S1 — Optional Streamable-HTTP transport for rebar-mcp + transport hardening

## Why
Authentication in MCP is an HTTP-transport concern; the spec says stdio servers must not
authenticate. rebar-mcp is stdio-only today (`src/rebar/mcp_server.py`, `build_server().run()` with
no transport arg). Before any verifier can exist there must be an HTTP transport to authenticate
against. This story adds that transport and its network hardening — the foundation every auth story
builds on — with stdio unchanged as the default. It also owns the SDK version pin, because it is
the first story to construct the SDK's HTTP transport / `TransportSecuritySettings`.

## What
Add an **optional Streamable-HTTP transport** to rebar-mcp, selected by config, using the official
`mcp` SDK's native support:
- **Pin the SDK here:** set `mcp>=1.28.1,<2` in `pyproject.toml` — in BOTH places it appears: the
  `[project.optional-dependencies] mcp` extra AND the `[dev]` extra (both currently `mcp>=1.9`). The
  v2-alpha renames the surface; 1.28.1 is at/above the DNS-rebinding fix GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f. All
  later stories build on this pin.
- Extend the existing **flat** `McpConfig` dataclass and the flat `_SECTIONS['mcp']` key→coercer
  dict in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (the one-level `coerce_sparse` architecture — NO nested
  sub-tables) with flat scalar keys: `transport` (`stdio` default | `http`), `http_host` (default
  `127.0.0.1`), `http_port` (default `8000`), `http_path` (default `/mcp`), `http_allowed_hosts`
  (comma-separated), `http_allowed_origins` (comma-separated), and `allow_unauthenticated_http`
  (bool, default `false`). Each is one dataclass field + one flat coercer entry, auto-deriving its
  `REBAR_MCP_*` env var. This story registers these keys in `MCP_ENV_VARS` + `server.json` and
  regenerates `docs/mcp-reference.md`/`docs/env-vars.md` for its slice (the CI drift gate
  `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` must pass). NOTE — both doc generators currently miss
  dynamically-derived MCP env vars and must be adjusted (all later key-adding stories rely on these
  fixes): (a) `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` **hardcodes** the gate-env-var table (line ~208, the
  static tuple `("REBAR_MCP_READONLY","REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM","REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC")`) instead of
  iterating `MCP_ENV_VARS` → change it to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`; (b) `scripts/gen_env_registry.py`
  (which generates `docs/env-vars.md`) AST-scans for literal `os.environ`/`os.getenv` calls, so it
  will NOT see the `REBAR_MCP_*` names the flat coercer derives dynamically → teach it to also ingest
  `MCP_ENV_VARS`. `MCP_ENV_VARS` + `server.json` (drift-gated by `scripts/check_server_manifest.py`)
  remain the authoritative registration; the two generators are made to reflect it.
- In `build_server`/`main`, when `transport=http` construct `FastMCP("rebar", host=…, port=…,
  transport_security=TransportSecuritySettings(...))` and run via `run(transport="streamable-http")`;
  the stdio path is unchanged.
- **Explicit transport-security defaults:** DNS-rebinding/Origin protection **on**. For a loopback
  bind, set explicit loopback allowlists (`127.0.0.1:<port>`, `localhost:<port>`, `[::1]:<port>` and
  the matching `http://…` origins) rather than empty lists (empty + protection-on either 421s
  localhost or degrades protection). Construct `host`/`port`/`transport_security` at `FastMCP(...)`
  time, NOT via late attribute assignment (the documented "421" late-host bug).
- **Non-loopback bind fail-closed gates:** a non-loopback `http_host` requires BOTH
  `http_allowed_hosts` AND `http_allowed_origins` to be non-empty, AND an explicit TLS-at-edge
  acknowledgment `REBAR_MCP_HTTP_TLS_AT_EDGE` (a flat `http_tls_at_edge` bool key) — because bearer
  tokens and proxy secrets must never cross the network in cleartext; without it the server refuses
  to start.
- **Unauthenticated-HTTP guard:** `allow_unauthenticated_http` (env `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP`)
  read through the flat coercer (a resolved config field, NOT a bespoke `os.environ` read). Starting
  `transport=http` with auth disabled refuses to start unless `allow_unauthenticated_http` is true.
  (Auth config lands in S2; until then "auth enabled" is treated as false, so HTTP requires the ack.)

## Approach & rationale
Use the SDK's built-in Streamable-HTTP transport and `TransportSecuritySettings` rather than
hand-rolling an ASGI server — the SDK provides both, keeping this a thin wiring change on a blessed
seam, consistent with peer servers (GitHub/Grafana/Neon). Keep every config key flat to fit rebar's
existing `coerce_sparse` architecture (adding scalars alongside `readonly`/`allow_llm`/
`allow_jira_sync`).

## Scope
The transport + transport-security + the two fail-closed startup gates (non-loopback and
unauthenticated-HTTP) + the SDK pin only. It ships **no** token verifiers and validates **no** token
(that is S2+). A subset of the epic.

## Security
Crosses a trust boundary (opens a network listener). Mitigated here by: DNS-rebinding/Origin
allowlisting on by default with explicit loopback defaults; loopback-only bind by default; a
non-loopback bind requiring both allowlists AND the explicit TLS-at-edge ack; and the fail-closed
unauthenticated-HTTP refusal. TLS is terminated at an edge proxy (documented in S7); rebar binds
HTTP behind it and never advertises or serves bearer auth in cleartext.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `pyproject.toml` pins `mcp>=1.28.1,<2`; `make typecheck` resolves the SDK imports used here.
- [ ] `McpConfig` exposes the flat `transport`/`http_host`/`http_port`/`http_path`/
      `http_allowed_hosts`/`http_allowed_origins`/`http_tls_at_edge`/`allow_unauthenticated_http`
      keys as fields + `_SECTIONS['mcp']` coercer entries; unset → stdio, byte-identical to today
      (observable: existing `tests/interfaces/facades/test_mcp.py` stdio suite passes).
- [ ] Integration test (tier: integration — an in-process ASGI HTTP client, e.g. httpx
      `ASGITransport`, driving the SDK's Streamable-HTTP ASGI entrypoint within its lifespan/session
      context; concrete entrypoint resolved against the pinned `mcp>=1.28.1,<2` at implementation
      time; a unit test cannot cross the Starlette transport/middleware seam) boots with
      `transport=http` (and `allow_unauthenticated_http=true` set for this auth-off boot) and asserts
      a baseline MCP request succeeds (observable oracle: HTTP 200 + valid `initialize` response).
- [ ] Integration test asserts a disallowed `Host` → **421** and a disallowed `Origin` → **403**,
      while an allowed loopback request passes (negative control in the same test), confirming the
      explicit loopback defaults work with protection ON.
- [ ] Unit/integration test asserts a non-loopback `http_host` refuses to start when either
      allowlist is empty OR `http_tls_at_edge` is unset, and starts when both allowlists AND the ack
      are supplied (observable oracle: process exits non-zero / raises a clear message vs boots).
- [ ] Unit/integration test asserts `transport=http` with auth off and no
      `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP` refuses to start, and starts when the ack is set.
- [ ] `MCP_ENV_VARS` + `server.json` include the new keys; `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` iterates
      `MCP_ENV_VARS` (not the hardcoded tuple) and `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` ingests `MCP_ENV_VARS`,
      so the regenerated `docs/mcp-reference.md` AND `docs/env-vars.md` include every new `REBAR_MCP_*`
      key; and `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` passes (observable oracle: both regenerated docs
      contain the new key names and the drift tests are green).
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.

## Compatibility
Additive: stdio remains default; all new keys default to today's behavior when unset. The only
cross-cutting change is the `mcp` pin, backward-compatible with existing installs. No change to the
existing 48 tools or the stdio path.
finding: `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` line 208 hardcodes a static tuple of the three current gate-var names (`REBAR_MCP_READONLY`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`) rather than iterating `MCP_ENV_VARS`. The plan says to 'regenerate docs/mcp-reference.md for its slice,' but running the existing script will not include the new transport env-vars in the Gate-environment-variables table without also patching the generator. This is an unenumerated edit target whose omission makes the stated regeneration outcome unachievable.
location: What section — 'regenerates docs/mcp-reference.md/docs/env-vars.md for its slice'; scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py line 208
suggested_fix: Replace line 208's `for var in ("REBAR_MCP_READONLY", ...)` with `from rebar.mcp_server import MCP_ENV_VARS` and `for entry in MCP_ENV_VARS: var = entry['name']` so the table auto-extends when MCP_ENV_VARS grows.

title: Adopt snap shared resources into rebar terraform + live import/apply/retag
plan:
## Why
rebar depends on the GitHub OIDC provider that snap's dead terraform owns, and the domain-wide wildcard cert + `auth_host` SSO stack are to be kept alive under rebar (user decision). All must move from snap's `snap-demo-tfstate` shared state into rebar's `rebar-tfstate` state, owned by rebar's `infra/terraform`. Physical names are preserved (rename = destroy/recreate); only ownership + tags move.

## What
**Provider declarations** (`infra/terraform/versions.tf`): add `hashicorp/random` (~> 3.6) and `hashicorp/archive` (~> 2.4) to `required_providers` alongside the existing `hashicorp/aws` — `random_password` and `archive_file` below require them, or `terraform init`/`validate` fail.

**`infra/terraform/oidc.tf`**: `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github` (url `https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com`, client_id `sts.amazonaws.com`, thumbprint `6938fd4d98bab03faadb97b34396831e3780aea1`), `tags = { Project = "rebar" }`. Rewire `iam.tf`'s `gha_terraform_plan_assume` federated identifier to `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github.arn`.

**`infra/terraform/acm.tf`**: `aws_acm_certificate.wildcard` (`*.solutions.navateam.com`, DNS), `aws_route53_record.cert_validation` (for_each), `aws_acm_certificate_validation.wildcard`. Cert gets `tags = { Project = "rebar" }` and `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` (shared prod-critical). Validation records use rebar's existing `var.dns_zone_id`.

**`infra/terraform/auth_sso.tf`**: SSO `locals` (auth_host, cookie_domain, google/cookie secret names, google_client_id, TTL). Manage `aws_ssm_parameter.cookie_signing_secret` at its EXISTING path `/auth-solutions/COOKIE_SIGNING_SECRET` with `value = "CHANGEME"` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` (rebar's ssm.tf idiom) — the live value is preserved on import and the Lambda reads it at runtime, so terraform never needs the value. DROP snap's `random_password.cookie_signing_secret` and the `data.aws_ssm_parameter.cookie_signing_secret` (only the edge-gate baking needed it; we do not adopt the sso-gate). Keep `data.aws_ssm_parameter.google_client_secret` (metadata only).

**`infra/terraform/auth_host.tf`**: `random_password.auth_origin_secret` (KEPT — its value must be identical in the Lambda `ORIGIN_SECRET` env and the CloudFront custom origin header; imported with its LIVE value so both sides stay matched and nothing rotates), `data.archive_file.auth_host`, `data.aws_kms_alias.ssm`, `aws_iam_role.auth_host` + `aws_iam_role_policy.auth_host` (least-privilege, verbatim from snap: own log group + `ssm:GetParameters` on the two `/auth-solutions/*` params + `kms:Decrypt` via `ssm.<region>`), `aws_lambda_function.auth_host`, `aws_apigatewayv2_api/_integration/_route/_stage`, `aws_lambda_permission.auth_host_apigw`, cache/origin-request policy data sources, `aws_cloudfront_distribution.auth_host` (`lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }`, viewer cert = the adopted wildcard), `aws_route53_record.auth_host_a` + `_aaaa`. Tag the taggable resources (Lambda, IAM role, API, CloudFront) `Project = "rebar"`.

**Lambda source**: copy the live-deployed auth-host source from the snap checkout `~/snap-oakhart-manual/snap-oakhart-manual/infra/shared/auth/` — specifically `auth-host/index.js` (306 LOC) and `lib/cookie.js` (114 LOC), both verified present — into `infra/terraform/auth/auth-host/index.js` and `infra/terraform/auth/lib/cookie.js` (consumed by `archive_file.auth_host`). These are the exact files the live `snap-demo-auth-host` Lambda runs.

**Name preservation**: `locals { legacy_name_prefix = "snap-demo" }` with a prominent comment that changing it destroys+recreates 18 live resources. Physical names stay `snap-demo-*`.

## Live migration procedure (operator-attested)
Live IDs (captured): OIDC `arn:…:oidc-provider/token.actions.githubusercontent.com`; ACM `…certificate/6597ee19-a770-4524-a0bd-fb86d608dd99`; Lambda `snap-demo-auth-host`; API `q8mv4bhkmh`; CloudFront `E15OTAFJILDOX6`.
1. `terraform import` each live object into rebar state. Imports are idempotent and non-destructive; on partial failure, re-run the remaining imports (or `state rm` a bad one and retry) — nothing is destroyed until `apply`.
2. Import `random_password.auth_origin_secret` with its live value read at import time from the live Lambda `ORIGIN_SECRET` env into a shell variable (never committed / not in HCL).
3. `terraform plan` and iterate the HCL until the ONLY diff is the retag (drop snap default_tags `project/managed-by/root`, add `Project=rebar`) plus the incidental pre-existing `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` create. NO destroy/replace of adopted resources, NO secret-value change. Record the plan as a ticket comment.
4. `terraform apply` (retag only). Then `terraform state rm` the 18 adopted resources from snap's `infra/shared` state, and remove/neutralize their HCL in snap's dead repo so its config cannot recreate them (snap is decommissioned; its drift CI is off).
5. Post-migration health check: confirm `https://auth.solutions.navateam.com/authorize?return_to=https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com` still issues a 302 to Google and the CloudFront distro + Lambda are healthy (the stack must survive the ownership move).

## Scope
Terraform + Lambda source + live import/apply/state-rm only. Docs are child 6725. Does NOT adopt snap's `github_deploy` role, audit-dashboard sso-gate, ECS/ECR/VPC/RDS.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `versions.tf` declares `hashicorp/random` + `hashicorp/archive`; `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github` is in `infra/terraform` tagged `Project=rebar` and the drift role references its `.arn`.
- [ ] Wildcard cert + auth_sso + auth_host resources exist in `infra/terraform` with physical names preserved, `Project=rebar` tags where supported, and `prevent_destroy` on the cert + CloudFront.
- [ ] Cookie-signing SSM param is managed at `/auth-solutions/COOKIE_SIGNING_SECRET` with `ignore_changes=[value]`; the only remaining `random_password` is `auth_origin_secret`.
- [ ] `terraform fmt -check`, `terraform validate`, and `make lint` pass on the new files; `infra/terraform/auth/auth-host/index.js` + `auth/lib/cookie.js` exist.
- [ ] [operator-attested] All 18 resources imported into rebar state; `terraform plan` shows no destroy/replace and no secret-value change (evidence: plan output as a comment).
- [ ] [operator-attested] `terraform apply` completed (retag); resources removed from snap's shared state; `https://auth.solutions.navateam.com/authorize` still returns a 302 to Google post-migration (evidence: outputs as a comment).
finding: The plan asserts 'copy `auth/auth-host/index.js` + `auth/lib/cookie.js` into `infra/terraform/auth/`' as if the source location is known, but the `auth/` directory does not exist anywhere in the rebar repo, and the plan never names the snap repository, branch, or path where these files currently live. With snap described as 'dead' and decommissioned, the source for these files is an unverified implicit dependency.
location: What section — 'Lambda source' paragraph; AC line: 'infra/terraform/auth/auth-host/index.js + auth/lib/cookie.js exist'
suggested_fix: Add to the 'Lambda source' paragraph: 'Source: `github.com/navapbc/snap-demo` (or equivalent), path `auth/auth-host/index.js` and `auth/lib/cookie.js`, at commit `<sha>`. Pin the commit so the copy is reproducible.' Include these files in the PR diff so their content is reviewed alongside the HCL.

title: Alias generation: adjective-adjective-animal for new tickets (legacy unchanged)
plan:
## What
Switch ticket alias generation for NEW tickets from adjective-noun-noun to
adjective-adjective-animal, using a vendored OSS wordlist (gfycat lists from
a-type/adjective-adjective-animal, MIT). Existing tickets are untouched and keep
resolving by their legacy aliases.

## Scope / Design
- New generator `compute_genesis_alias` in `src/rebar/_alias.py`, backed by a new
  bundled wordlist `src/rebar/_engine/resources/ticket-wordlist-v2.txt`
  (adjectives, `# ANIMALS` marker, animals). The two adjectives are guaranteed
  distinct; deterministic off the hex id.
- Wired into the create path only: `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py` `_compute_alias`
  now calls `compute_genesis_alias`. New tickets persist the alias onto the CREATE
  event, locking in the format.
- Legacy `compute_alias` (adjective-noun-noun) + old wordlist are UNCHANGED, so the
  read-time backfill for pre-existing tickets is byte-identical (no existing alias
  changes).
- License vendored to `docs/licenses/adjective-adjective-animal-LICENSE.txt`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] New tickets get a 3-word `adjective-adjective-animal` alias whose two
      adjectives differ, deterministic from the ticket id.
- [ ] Legacy `compute_alias` output is unchanged (existing tickets resolve to the
      same aliases; pinned literals `real-soil-anger` / `brawny-gill-inlay` still hold).
- [ ] Generator is total + deterministic; too-short (<12 hex) id → None; missing
      wordlist falls back to a hex alias with a one-shot WARN.
- [ ] Wordlist sourced from OSS with license vendored; not hand-curated.
- [ ] Tests cover the new format, path independence, and the missing-wordlist fallback.
finding: The plan asserts the two adjectives will be 'guaranteed distinct' but provides no mechanism description. With a simple hex-nibble modulo approach (as used in the legacy generator), any ticket ID where int(hex[0:4],16) % len(adjs) == int(hex[4:8],16) % len(adjs) produces two identical adjectives, silently violating the guarantee stated in AC-1.
location: Scope / Design — 'The two adjectives are guaranteed distinct; deterministic off the hex id' / AC-1
suggested_fix: When deriving adj2, if `int(hex_id[4:8], 16) % len(adjs) == adj1_idx`, use `(adj1_idx + 1) % len(adjs)` as adj2. Document this rule in the plan so tests can pin it.

title: Project-supplied review criteria + project-invariant compliance (unified cross-gate registry)
plan:
# Project-supplied review criteria + project-invariant compliance (unified cross-gate registry)

## Context

rebar's review gates ship a fixed, built-in set of criteria. Client projects have their own
standards and invariants — naming, layering, banned APIs, architectural rules — the built-in
set can't express. This epic lets a **project supply its own review criteria** and declare
**architectural invariants** the gates check, **beyond** the built-in set.

**Cross-cutting:** it must serve **plan-review, code-review (`b744`), and any future
criteria-based gate** through **one shared registry**. (Spun out of `5fd2` as follow-on F8;
config-UX sibling `55de`/F5.)

**Posture (from `5fd2`, binding):** coach-not-block, advisory-by-default, **fail-open
(ironclad)**, polyglot / broad client base, convergent-by-construction, **reuse existing
machinery — no plugin system, no new DSL**.

## Design decisions (settled in brainstorm; see rationale comment)

1. **Unified criterion data model + shared registration/discovery/schema/eval plumbing;
   per-gate application** (gate-scoped routing inside the shared record; postures do NOT
   converge).
2. **Two authoring forms, no project code in the gate:** DET "invariants" = declarative
   pattern rules via the existing grounding scan facade; LLM criteria = prompt files.
3. **Any criterion (DET or LLM) may block** — `--force`-escapable, folds into the signed
   (kind-keyed) attestation coverage, can never rebind a built-in.
4. **Blocking is project-owned policy.** rebar surfaces the same eval/calibration instruments
   it uses on its built-ins; the maintainer decides blocking + threshold. Trust is theirs.
5. **Reuse existing formats** (prompt front-matter, the `criteria_routing.json` schema,
   Inspect-AI fixtures, the scan facade). No new formats.
6. **Explicit activation; presence != active.** Routing/threshold in a `.rebar/` overlay
   (§ Overlay schema); namespaced + additive; the overlay may re-tune or **disable** a
   built-in (recorded in coverage).
7. **Eval tooling in scope** = fixture/discrimination harness + calibration/agreement view.
   **Out** = REVIEW_RESULT FP-replay (F1; gate-agnostic, inherited later).
8. **An "invariant" is just a blocking criterion** (usually DET). One mechanism.
9. **Visual editor extended** to author project criteria (reuses `/library/create`).

## Overlay schema, activation & caching (clears E2/G6/T8)

- **File:** `.rebar/criteria_routing.json`, reusing each gate's packaged `criteria_routing.json`
  schema, keyed by gate:
  `{"plan_review": {"<id>": {…routing…}}, "code_review": {"<id>": {…}}, "activate": ["project.<name>", …]}`.
- **Per-criterion routing** reuses existing keys: plan-review `{exec, applies_at, block_threshold,
  default_posture, facet, checklist}`; code-review `{exec, applies_to (globs), block_threshold,
  blocking_enabled}`. (Note the deliberate posture divergence — plan-review derives blocking
  from `default_posture`; code-review uses an explicit `blocking_enabled`; the shared
  `threshold_for` must honor BOTH — do not "fix" the divergence.) **DET criteria also carry
  `fail_mode: open|closed`** (default `open` for project invariants; `closed` for the built-in
  security class) — see § DET-invariant scan scoping.
- **`exec` vocabulary:** `1-TURN` / `2-STEP` / `AGENT` (LLM tiers) or **`DET`** (pattern-rule
  invariant; code-review already uses `DET` inline). A **DET criterion has no prompt** — descriptor
  assembly must treat prompt resolution as OPTIONAL for `exec: DET` (today `_descriptor_from_prompt`
  unconditionally calls `get_prompt(...)` and raises if absent). A DET criterion's "rubric" is its
  detector rule id (`.rebar/detectors/`), not a `.md`; only its routing entry is required.
  **DET fallback descriptor shape** (a `_descriptor_from_prompt` DET branch that skips
  `get_prompt`): `name` = routing `name` or id; `scenario` = the detector rule's `message`/
  description from its `.rebar/detectors/<id>` envelope; `facet` = routing `facet` (default = id);
  `checklist` = empty. This is an expand-contract change (add the DET branch; existing prompt-backed
  callers unaffected).
- **Namespace / collision (load-time, located error):** a net-new project criterion id MUST be
  `project.<name>`-prefixed. An overlay entry keyed by an **un-prefixed built-in id** is a
  **re-tune/disable** of that built-in (allowed, §Design 6). A `project.`-prefixed id equal to a
  built-in id, or a duplicate project id, is **rejected at load** (a project id can never rebind
  a built-in).
- **Activation:** a criterion runs only if listed in `activate` (presence in the file ≠ active).
- **Discovery — `effective_criteria(repo_root)`** = `CANONICAL_LLM`/`OVERLAY_IDS` ∪ activated
  project ids (each resolved via `get_prompt(id, repo_root)` for its rubric + the overlay routing
  entry). Route it through **every** vocabulary site: plan-review `load_criteria`,
  `check_registry_coverage`, and the **workflow Pass-1 assemble op** (`workflow_ops` emits a
  sanitized `include_project_<name>` boolean — `.`→`_` — per activated project id as the
  coverage/routing record). **LANDED-DESIGN NOTE (ef7e):** the v3 `batch` schema is IMMUTABLE and
  the gate self-validates, so project criteria do NOT fan out via a new workflow field; instead the
  rebar-specific `ProductionBatchRunner` appends the `project.` subset of `route_criteria` to the
  finder set (built-ins keep their static YAML `criteria:` slots, unchanged). Code-review sites
  (`OVERLAY_IDS`, `routing_index`, `threshold_for`) are unified later, after b744 (story 5065).
- **Caching (clears G6 cache bug) — LANDED (ef7e):** `_routing_index()` stays `@lru_cache`d for the
  **packaged** index (immutable per binary); `effective_routing(repo_root)` / `load_criteria` are
  `@lru_cache(maxsize=128)` keyed by `(repo_root, sha256(overlay-bytes))` — a CONTENT signature
  (not mtime), so an overlay edit self-invalidates and cross-repo leakage is impossible;
  `prompt_library._invalidate_caches()` clears them. A RED cross-repo-leak test proves it.

## Attestation & invalidation (clears G6/COH/E1; reuses kind-keyed attest infra)

- **`registry_version(repo_root=None)`** additionally hashes the `.rebar/` overlay when
  `repo_root` is given; thread `repo_root` through **every** caller — `claim_gate_check →
  compute_validity`, and also `drift_refresh_candidate` and `remediation_mode_candidate` (which
  call `registry_version()` today without it) — so activating/editing/disabling a criterion
  **invalidates a prior attestation at claim time** (today it hashes packaged routing only — the
  change does not invalidate).
- **Disable recorded in coverage:** a disabled built-in is listed in a `disabled_builtins: [ids]`
  field in the verdict `coverage` dict (and folded into the signed manifest) so the cert reflects
  reduced coverage rather than silent omission; the field is additive/expand-contract (absent on
  old certs).
- **Cross-gate:** applies to **any gate attestation backed by the shared registry** (plan-review
  now; code-review once it signs — see Migration; code-review signs nothing today).
- **Expand-contract / back-compat:** the new regver basis is additive; an existing attestation
  lacking it is treated as **stale → re-review**, never a crash. Overlay absent ⇒ regver == today
  (no invalidation churn).

## DET-invariant scan scoping & fail-open (clears COH)

- A DET invariant runs as a **whole-repo content scan** via the shared `grounding.engine_b.scan`.
  The **oracle always fails open** (engine binary absent / parse-error / timeout → `abstain`). The
  **consumer** then applies the criterion's **`fail_mode`**: `open` (default for project invariants)
  turns an abstain into a skip = PASS; `closed` (the built-in security class, via the existing
  `apply_failclosed`) turns an abstain into a BLOCK. So generalizing `code_review/detectors.py` =
  making its currently-hardcoded fail-closed behavior a per-criterion `fail_mode` that **defaults
  open** — the two behaviors are one code path parameterized by `fail_mode`, not a contradiction.
  Matches are **gate-scoped**: code-review filters matches to the **diff** (as
  `code_review/detectors.py` already does); plan-review has no diff, so a DET invariant checks the
  plan's declared intent and repo context — an invariant that inherently needs a diff is **N/A at
  plan time (fail-open)** and belongs to code-review.
- Because matches are gate-scoped (diff / plan), pre-existing whole-repo violations are **not**
  surfaced as new work — this is why **no baseline/grandfathering** is needed (reconciles the
  Out-of-scope note).
- **LLM fail-open:** an LLM criterion configured to block whose call errors/times out **fails
  open** (skipped, does not block) — same rule as DET.

- **`apply_failclosed` → `fail_mode` expand-contract (clears T4; story `7f0d`).** The generalization
  is backward-compatible, no flag-day: `apply_failclosed(...)` is KEPT as a thin wrapper that calls
  the new `apply_fail_mode(..., fail_mode="closed")` — so its **default stays `closed`** and every
  existing caller (`code_review/detectors.py`, the built-in security class) is byte-identical until
  explicitly migrated. `detectors.py` is then migrated to call the new function with the
  criterion's routing `fail_mode` (project invariants default `open`, security class `closed`); the
  old wrapper is deprecated and removed only after all callers pass an explicit `fail_mode`.
  **Rollback** = revert callers to the `apply_failclosed` wrapper (still `closed`). No caller's
  behavior changes silently.

## What exists vs. what we build (grounded to file:line, 2026-06-30; incl. b744 worktree)

**Already built (reuse):**
- **Shared three-pass kernel** `review_kernel/` (verify/decide/coach) — **both** gates already
  consume it (code-review via `code_review/workflow_ops.py`); the divergence-risky core is
  already unified.
- **A DET pattern-rule → finding consumer already exists:** `code_review/detectors.py` calls the
  shared `grounding.engine_b.scan` + `load_registry` (which already unions `.rebar/detectors/`) +
  `apply_failclosed` (consumer-side fail-CLOSED; oracle stays fail-OPEN). Generalizable — the only
  hardcoded bits are the `rebar.builtin.security.` prefix + 2 criterion mappings.
- **Per-criterion blocking is DONE:** `code_review/registry.threshold_for` + kernel Pass-3 +
  detector fail-closed.
- **Project extension points:** `.rebar/prompts/` (rubric override), `.rebar/<gate>_moves.json`
  (additive coach moves), `.rebar/detectors/` (project pattern rules), `.rebar/evals/` (fixtures).
- **Kind-keyed attestation infra** (`plan_review/attest.py`); Inspect-AI eval seam; discrimination
  scorers + Cohen's κ + parity metrics.

**Net-new (build):**
- **Open the closed criterion vocabularies** (`CANONICAL_LLM`, `OVERLAY_IDS`) via
  `effective_criteria(repo_root)` — THE precondition; a project criterion is silently skipped
  today regardless of overlay.
- **`.rebar/` routing overlay + activation + cache isolation** (§ Overlay schema).
- **A shared descriptor abstraction:** code-review has only `routing_index()` (raw routing), no
  `load_criteria`-style merged descriptor and no `facet/checklist/…`; unification introduces/
  generalizes one and reconciles the `blocking_enabled` vs `default_posture` postures + exec-tier-
  polymorphic (prompt-less DET vs LLM) criteria.
- **Generalize the DET-scan consumer** beyond the security prefix (data-driven criterion↔detector
  map) so arbitrary project invariants route; make the fail-open/closed behavior a per-criterion
  `fail_mode` (today `apply_failclosed` is hardcoded closed; default project invariants to open);
  expose to plan-review too.
- **Per-criterion eval runner** — `eval_solver.run_case` is a closed 3-id dispatch (raises
  otherwise) + a validate-only CLI; add a criterion-aware runner + a live CLI + the
  **calibration/agreement view** (rendering is net-new; κ/parity metrics exist).
- **Attestation port to code-review** + `registry_version(repo_root)` + disable-coverage +
  claim-gate invalidation (§ Attestation).
- **Editor live must-fire/must-not-fire preview** — a new **synchronous** editor endpoint
  `POST /criterion/preview` `{criterion_id | inline(prompt+routing), fixture}` →
  `{verdict: "fire"|"no-fire", finding?: <findings.py finding shape>, rationale: str}`, reusing
  the per-criterion eval runner behind the `agents` extra + credentials, with a **configurable
  timeout (default 60s)** (no prompt-execution endpoint exists today — the editor's `POST` set is
  `/save`, `/prompt/save`, `/validate`, `/library/create`). **Spike gate:** if a single sync
  preview does not return within the timeout on the reference model, fall back to an async
  job+poll shape rather than blocking the editor's `ThreadingHTTPServer` handler.
- **Routing derivation/validation pipeline** (`criteria_routing.json` is hand-maintained; no
  generator/drift gate).

## Migration & compatibility

- **Callsites** to route through `effective_criteria`: plan-review `load_criteria`,
  `check_registry_coverage`, `attest.registry_version`, `workflow_ops` vocab; code-review
  `OVERLAY_IDS`, `routing_index`, `threshold_for`.
- **Preserve the `review_code` / `code_review/shim.py` `review_result` return shape** — it is a
  back-compat surface consumed by MCP `review_code`, CLI `rebar review-code`, and the Gerrit
  `review_bot/adapter.py`.
- **Rollback = fail-open to packaged:** overlay absent ⇒ both gates behave exactly as today. The
  unification is expand-contract; a misbehaving unified registry falls back per-gate. The
  `registry_version` code change is itself expand-contract (old certs → stale, never crash).
- **Docs deliverable:** an ADR under `docs/adr/` (LANDED: `docs/adr/0015-project-supplied-criteria.md` for the plan-review slice) + a `CLAUDE.md` "project-supplied criteria"
  section document the unified registry contract, the overlay merge/activation semantics, and the
  attestation-invalidation protocol, plus a how-to for authoring/activating/eval'ing/blocking on a
  criterion (the concrete target for the docs AC).

## Build order (risk-ordered MVP; clears G5 thin-slice)

Prove the full loop on the riskiest piece first, then fan out:
1. **MVP slice — ✅ DONE (story `ef7e`, PR #103).** `effective_criteria`/`effective_routing`
   (content-hash cache isolation) → activate ONE project LLM criterion from the `.rebar/` overlay →
   `ProductionBatchRunner` fans it into Pass-1 → surfaces its finding; CI parity gate + ADR 0015.
   (The `registry_version`-reflects-the-overlay + attestation-invalidation half is split into story
   `08af`, step 6 below — this slice proved author→activate→run→surface.)
2. Generalize the DET-invariant scan consumer (data-driven map).
3. Per-criterion eval runner + calibration view.
4. Editor live-preview + authoring extension.
5. **Unify** plan-review + code-review registries into `rebar.llm.criteria` (after `b744` lands).

## Dependency

**Blocked on `b744`** (code-review; WS1–WS5 landed on `worktree-code-review-capability`, WS6 =
Gerrit voter pending). The `review_kernel` is already shared; the remaining fork is the
registry/descriptor + posture + vocabulary layer, unified after b744 lands (temporary
duplication accepted).

## Success Criteria

- A project can add its own criteria (DET pattern-rules + LLM prompts) that run in plan-review,
  code-review, and future gates via **one shared registry**.
- A project can declare architectural invariants (blocking DET criteria) the gates check.
- The project **owns blocking/threshold policy** per criterion, informed by the same
  eval/calibration instruments rebar uses on its built-ins.
- **No project code executes in the gate**; unsupported stacks fail open.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `effective_criteria(repo_root)` opens the closed vocabularies (`CANONICAL_LLM` +
      `OVERLAY_IDS`) so an activated project criterion is loaded/run by the gate; routed through
      every vocabulary callsite (see Migration).
- [ ] `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` overlay per the § Overlay schema: gate-keyed, namespaced,
      explicit `activate` list, load-time collision/rebind rejection (a located error).
- [ ] Overlay merge is cache-isolated per repo (packaged index stays cached; overlay is not); a
      RED test proves no cross-repo routing leakage.
- [ ] The overlay may re-tune or disable a built-in; a disable is recorded in attestation coverage.
- [ ] Project DET pattern-rule invariants run through the generalized (data-driven)
      `engine_b.scan` consumer and surface as blocking-capable findings; fail-open on
      engine-binary-absent / parse-error / timeout per the criterion's `fail_mode` (default `open`
      → skip = PASS; security-class `closed` → block).
- [ ] Project LLM prompt criteria run through the shared registry with gate-scoped routing; an
      LLM-block criterion that errors fails open. … [truncated by reconciler]
finding: The plan states that a DET invariant at plan-review time 'checks the plan's declared intent and repo context' and that 'an invariant that inherently needs a diff is N/A at plan time (fail-open).' But `grounding.engine_b.scan` is a whole-repo content scan (it runs opengrep/ast-grep over the repository tree). Running a whole-repo DET scan for every activated project invariant at plan-review time has no specified scan target (the plan text is not the scan target; the whole repo is). The plan provides no rationale for WHY a whole-repo scan is the right approach at plan time (as opposed to, e.g., scanning only the declared `file_impact` files), no performance characterization, and no statement of what 'checking the plan's declared intent' actually means in terms of the scanner's input.
location: § DET-invariant scan scoping & fail-open — 'A DET invariant runs as a whole-repo content scan via the shared `grounding.engine_b.scan`'; 'plan-review has no diff, so a DET invariant checks the plan's declared intent and repo context'

title: Retire merge=ours .gitattributes + b859 lock-retry band-aid; migrate committed locks
plan:
## What

**Retire the band-aids** now that the ref-lock (C3) is proven, flip the default backend to `ref`,
and remove the dead file-lock machinery + `merge=ours` entry, with a migration for existing repos.

**Depends on: 2711-21bf-ab71-4136 (C3).** The ref backend must be wired and validated first;
removing the file backend / retry loop before C3 would leave the reconciler with no working lock.

- Flip `[reconciler] lock_backend` default to `ref` and remove the `file` backend path from
  `_advisory_lock.py` once the ref backend has baked.
- **Remove the `b859-8fa1` retry loop** in `acquire_pass_lock` AND its now-dead helpers
  (`_resolve_retry_budget`, `_LOCK_RETRY_BUDGET_DEFAULT`, `_compute_backoff_seconds`, and the
  b859-only backoff constants). Retire the config key `lock_max_retries` (`_config_schema.py`
  `ReconcilerConfig` field + coercion entry) and its env aliases
  (`REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES` + deprecated alias `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_RETRY_BUDGET` in
  `config.py`). Delete/adjust the tests that exercise them:
  `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_advisory_lock_retry_jitter.py`, and the retry-budget
  assertions in `tests/.../test_config_file_wiring.py` + `test_ev3c_env_renames.py`.
- **Deprecation path for the retired config key.** `lock_max_retries` removal is operator-visible;
  rather than a hard break, treat a still-present `lock_max_retries` as **deprecated** — emit a
  one-time deprecation warning (config-load path) and ignore it — and note the removal in
  `docs/config.md` + the changelog, so existing operator configs do not error on upgrade.
- **Remove `.reconciler-*` from `_GITATTRIBUTES`** in `src/rebar/_commands/init.py` (leave
  `.bridge_state/* merge=ours` untouched — that is WS-1 binding data, out of scope), and update
  `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` to assert `.reconciler-*` is absent and
  `.bridge_state/*` still present.
- **Migration for already-initialized trackers:** `_commit_gitattributes` is create-if-absent only,
  so editing the constant does not fix existing repos. Add an update-migration arm (mirroring the
  `_commit_gitignore` line-migration) that strips the `.reconciler-* merge=ours` line from an
  already-committed `.gitattributes`. Idempotent; on git failure, log-and-continue (do not abort).
- **One-time cleanup:** remove any pre-existing `.reconciler-pass-lock`/`.reconciler-phase-gate`
  committed on the tickets branch from the old scheme (a single named helper, idempotent, invoked
  from exactly one site — reconciler startup; state the site explicitly).
- Extend the ref-lock ADR to record that the b859 loop is superseded by the C3 self-healing lock.

## Design decisions (resolves plan-review ambiguities; binding on the implementation)

1. **Precise removal set (verified dead by grep, not by line number).** After the file backend is
   gone, delete every symbol left with zero consumers: the file dispatch branches in
   `acquire_pass_lock` / `release_pass_lock` / `check_phase_gate`; `_write_file_to_tickets_branch`,
   `_delete_file_from_tickets_branch`, `_commit_in_detached_tickets_worktree`,
   `_git_show_tickets_file`, the file-based `check_pass_lock`, the `_LOCK_FILE`/`_GATE_FILE`
   constants; the b859 outer-retry loop + `_resolve_retry_budget` / `_LOCK_RETRY_BUDGET_DEFAULT` /
   `_compute_backoff_seconds`; and `_cas_advance_with_retry` / `_cas_backoff_seconds` /
   `_jittered_backoff` / the backoff constants **iff** grep confirms no remaining consumer once the
   file backend is deleted. **KEEP** `_cas_once` and `_is_cas_mismatch` — they are imported by
   `_ref_lock.py` and are the shared CAS discriminator. Every deletion is justified by a
   zero-consumer grep across `src/` and `tests/`.
2. **`lock_backend` post-removal.** Keep the key (default now `ref`); the acquire/release/gate
   dispatch becomes unconditional ref. A still-configured `lock_backend=file` is
   **deprecated-and-ignored**: a one-time warning is logged and the ref backend is used anyway
   (rollback via `file` is intentionally gone — ref is proven). The `{file, ref}` choice validator
   stays so the value still parses.
3. **`lock_max_retries` deprecation mechanics.** Removing the `ReconcilerConfig.lock_max_retries`
   field means a config that still sets it hits `Config.from_mapping`'s existing **unknown-key
   path**, which WARNs-and-ignores under the default non-strict load — exactly the required
   "one-time warning, not a load error". Verify no reconciler config-load site passes `strict=True`
   (which would reject it); if one does, add `lock_max_retries` to an explicit deprecated-and-ignored
   set rather than letting it error. The env aliases are removed from `config.py`'s env map.
4. **Migrations — two distinct, idempotent, single-site helpers.**
   (a) *gitattributes line-strip* lives in `init.py` (an update arm on `_commit_gitattributes`,
   mirroring the `_commit_gitignore` line-migration): if the committed `.gitattributes` contains a
   `.reconciler-*` line, rewrite without it and commit; on any git failure log-and-continue (never
   abort init). (b) *committed-lock-file cleanup* is a single named helper (e.g.
   `_purge_committed_reconciler_locks`) invoked from exactly one site — the reconciler startup in
   `rebar_reconciler/__main__.py:main()`, before the pass acquires — that deletes any
   `.reconciler-pass-lock` / `.reconciler-phase-gate` still committed on the tickets branch (no-op if
   absent → idempotent; git failure log-and-continue).
5. **Regression test.** A test asserts no `.reconciler-*` path is ever committed to the tickets
   branch after a ref-backend pass (and that the cleanup helper removes a pre-seeded one).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `lock_backend` defaults to `ref`; the file-lock backend + the `b859` retry loop are deleted,
      along with `_resolve_retry_budget`, `_LOCK_RETRY_BUDGET_DEFAULT`, `_compute_backoff_seconds`,
      any then-dead backoff/CAS-retry helpers (grep-verified), the `lock_max_retries` config field +
      coercion + env aliases, and their tests. `_cas_once`/`_is_cas_mismatch` are kept (used by
      `_ref_lock`). A configured `lock_backend=file` is deprecated-and-ignored (one-time warning).
- [ ] `.reconciler-*` is removed from `_GITATTRIBUTES`; `.bridge_state/*` is left in place;
      `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` updated accordingly.
- [ ] A `.gitattributes` update-migration (mirroring `_commit_gitignore`) strips
      `.reconciler-* merge=ours` from already-committed trackers; idempotent; git failure logs and
      continues rather than aborting.
- [ ] A single, named, idempotent startup migration (invoked from `__main__.main()` before acquire)
      removes any pre-existing committed `.reconciler-*` files; a regression test asserts no
      `.reconciler-*` path is ever committed to the tickets branch.
- [ ] `lock_max_retries` is handled as deprecated-and-ignored (one-time warning, not a load error)
      and its removal is documented in `docs/config.md` + changelog.
- [ ] Full non-integration suite green.
finding: The plan directs updating `tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py`, but that path does not exist. The actual test file is `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py`. Following the plan literally would create a new file in the wrong location and leave the existing interface test (which still asserts `.reconciler-* merge=ours` IS present at line 51) unmodified — causing the full non-integration suite to fail on AC2.
location: AC line: '`tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` updated to assert `.reconciler-*` is absent'
suggested_fix: Replace `tests/unit/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` with `tests/interfaces/store/test_gitattributes_merge.py` everywhere in the plan. In that file, change line 51 from `assert ".reconciler-* merge=ours" in show.stdout` to `assert ".reconciler-*" not in show.stdout` (or equivalent), and update the module docstring at line 3-9 to remove the `.reconciler-*` reference.

title: Project DET invariants: generalize the scan consumer + per-criterion fail_mode
plan:
## Why

Project "invariants" = deterministic pattern-rule criteria (banned APIs, layering, naming). The
consumer already exists: `code_review/detectors.py` calls the shared `grounding.engine_b.scan` +
`load_registry` (which already unions `.rebar/detectors/`) + `apply_failclosed`. But it is
hardcoded to the `rebar.builtin.security.` prefix + 2 criterion mappings and is fail-CLOSED. This
story generalizes it into a data-driven, per-criterion-`fail_mode` consumer usable by any project
invariant, in both gates.

## What

- Generalize the detector→criterion mapping: a data-driven `criterion↔detector` map (drop the
  hardcoded `rebar.builtin.security.` prefix) so arbitrary `.rebar/detectors/` rules route to an
  activated DET criterion.
- Per-criterion `fail_mode: open|closed` on the routing record (default `open` for project
  invariants; `closed` for the built-in security class). The ORACLE stays fail-open (abstain on
  absent-binary/parse-error/timeout); the CONSUMER applies `fail_mode` — `open` → skip=PASS,
  `closed` → BLOCK (the existing `apply_failclosed` path). One code path parameterized by
  `fail_mode`, not two behaviors.
- DET fallback descriptor shape (a `_descriptor_from_prompt` DET branch that skips `get_prompt`):
  `name`=routing name/id, `scenario`=detector rule `message`, `facet`=routing facet (default id),
  `checklist`=empty. Expand-contract (prompt-backed callers unaffected).
- Expose the generalized consumer to **plan-review** (which calls no scan consumer today — only
  `refute_absence` in `det_floor.py`); matches are gate-scoped (code-review: diff; plan-review:
  plan/N-A).

## Scope

- Files: `src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py` (generalize), `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/`
  (new DET-invariant consumer wiring), `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py` (DET descriptor
  branch + `fail_mode`), the routing schema.
- Depends on story `#1` (registry/overlay/activation). Reuses the shared grounding facade
  (already unions `.rebar/detectors/`) — no new oracle work.
- Out: graph-level invariants (transitive layering/cycles) — a later follow-on, not a new DSL.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The scan consumer is data-driven (no hardcoded `rebar.builtin.security.` prefix); an
      activated project DET invariant routes its `.rebar/detectors/` rule and surfaces findings.
- [ ] Per-criterion `fail_mode` honored: project invariants default fail-open (abstain→skip=PASS);
      the security class stays fail-closed (abstain→BLOCK) via the existing `apply_failclosed`.
- [ ] DET criteria build a descriptor without a prompt (the DET `_descriptor_from_prompt` branch).
- [ ] The generalized consumer is wired into plan-review as well as code-review; matches are
      gate-scoped (diff for code-review; plan/N-A for plan-review).
- [ ] Fail-open is preserved on engine-binary-absent / parse-error / timeout for `fail_mode:open`.


## Plan-review consumer integration (remediation, run 1)

### Match-scoping without a diff (clears G6 blocker 1)
Plan-review has no diff, so a project DET invariant is scoped to the ticket's DECLARED intent +
its `file_impact` paths (from the ticket state), NOT a whole-repo scan surfaced as new work:
- If the ticket declares `file_impact` paths, the consumer scopes matches to THOSE paths (the
  files this plan intends to touch) — the plan-time analog of code-review's `changed_files`
  diff-scope. A match in a declared file surfaces as a finding.
- If there is no `file_impact`, the DET invariant is advisory-only over the repo context (it
  cannot pin the match to this plan's change), never a hard block on a pre-existing whole-repo
  violation — this is why NO baseline/grandfathering is needed (pre-existing violations outside
  the plan's declared files are not surfaced as new work).
- An invariant that INHERENTLY needs a diff (e.g. "no new call to X") is **N/A at plan time
