  (fail-open)** and belongs to code-review. So plan-time DET scoping ∈ {file_impact-scoped,
  advisory-repo-context, N-A-fail-open} — never an unscoped whole-repo block.

### Integration point (clears G6 blocker 2)
The generalized consumer runs as an ADDITIONAL DET check appended into `det_floor.run_det_floor`
(alongside P1–P9), returning `DetResult`-shaped results (`id=<project criterion id>`, `name=<detector
rule message>`, `status`, `blocking`, `finding`, `coverage`). Project-invariant results therefore
flow UNCHANGED through the existing partition in `workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck`
(`det_blocking_findings` / `det_advisory_findings` / `det_coverage`) → `orchestrator.partition_findings`.
Blocking is gated by the criterion's `fail_mode` + posture: a `fail_mode:open` invariant that abstains
(engine binary absent / parse-error / timeout) → skip=PASS (advisory at most); a `fail_mode:closed`
(security class) abstain → BLOCK (the existing `apply_failclosed` path, now parameterized). A `match`
in a declared file blocks per the criterion's `default_posture`/`blocking_enabled`.

### Data-driven map location (clears the hardcoded-prefix AC)
The detector→criterion association + `fail_mode` move INTO `criteria_routing.json` (both gates): a
DET criterion's routing entry carries a `detector` selector (id or id-prefix) + `fail_mode:
open|closed`. `run_security_detectors` (renamed to the general `run_detectors`, a thin back-compat
alias kept) reads the routing map instead of the hardcoded `SECURITY_DETECTOR_PREFIX` /
`_criterion_for`; the security class is just the built-in rows (`fail_mode: closed`) — same code
path, data-driven. `_validate_routing_entry` gains `fail_mode ∈ {open, closed}` validation.

## Acceptance Criteria (remediation additions)

- [ ] Plan-review DET-invariant matches are scoped to the ticket's `file_impact` (or advisory when
      absent) — never an unscoped whole-repo block; a test asserts the scoping.
- [ ] The consumer wires in as a `DetResult`-shaped check via `run_det_floor`, flowing through the
      existing det_blocking/det_advisory/det_coverage partition; `fail_mode` gates abstain→block.


## Static P1–P9 + dynamic project-check phase (remediation, run 2)

`DET_CHECKS` (the static `(p1_readiness_shape, …, p9_file_impact_coverage)` tuple) is UNCHANGED —
the built-in floor stays a fixed constant (expand-contract). `run_det_floor(ctx)` gains a SECOND,
additive phase AFTER the static P1–P9 loop: it discovers the activated `exec: DET` PROJECT criteria
at call time from `ctx.repo_root` (via `registry.effective_criteria(ctx.repo_root)` filtered to
`exec == "DET"` + `project.`-prefixed), and for each runs the generalized scan consumer
(`engine_b.scan` over the criterion's `detector` selector, matches scoped to the ticket's
`file_impact`) → one `DetResult` per criterion, appended to the SAME result list P1–P9 return. A
repo with no activated project DET criterion (the common case) adds zero checks ⇒ byte-identical to
today. The phase is fail-open (a raising project check → `abstain`, logged, per the existing
`run_det_floor` fail-open contract), so a misbehaving project rule never breaks the floor.

## Acceptance Criteria (remediation additions, run 2)

- [ ] `DET_CHECKS` stays static; `run_det_floor` adds a call-time second phase that discovers
      activated `exec: DET` project criteria from `ctx.repo_root` and appends their `DetResult`s.
      A repo with no project DET criterion is byte-identical to today (a test asserts zero added).


## DET-tier isolation, thin-slice sequencing & schema details (remediation, run 4)

### exec:DET criteria never enter the LLM batch (clears G6)
`effective_criteria()` returns activated project ids regardless of tier, so the LLM-routing sites
must FILTER OUT `exec == "DET"`:
- `orchestrator.route_criteria` skips any descriptor whose `exec == "DET"` (they are dispatched to
  the DET consumer, never fanned out as an LLM finder) — the same way `ISF` is already excluded.
- `workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria`'s `effective` vocabulary excludes `exec == "DET"`
  ids from the `include_<id>` batch fan-out (a DET criterion has no finder prompt to run).
- The exclusion filter reads `str(crit.get('exec','')).upper() == 'DET'` DIRECTLY from the
  descriptor dict — NOT via `registry.exec_tier()`, which has NO DET arm and silently remaps
  DET→1-TURN (so filtering via `exec_tier` would wrongly classify a DET criterion as a
  single-turn finder and admit it to the batch). The routing/descriptor path must never hand
  a DET descriptor to `pass1_chunk`. A DET descriptor is consumed ONLY by the
  `run_det_floor` project-check phase. A test asserts an activated DET project criterion appears in
  the DET-floor results and is ABSENT from the LLM `include_*`/route_criteria set.

### Thin-slice sequencing + coherence (clears G5)
This is ONE architectural seam — "the generalized DET-scan consumer, data-driven + exposed to both
gates" — not four independent features; the files it touches are the single call-graph of that one
consumer. Risk-ordered build (each independently testable, no child tickets needed):
1. **MVP:** promote the detector→criterion map + `fail_mode` into `criteria_routing.json` and make
   `code_review/detectors.py` read it data-drivenly (drop the hardcoded prefix), security class
   preserved fail-closed. Proven by the existing `test_code_review_ws5.py` fail-closed tests.
2. Add the `exec: DET` descriptor branch in `plan_review/registry._descriptor_from_prompt`
   (skip `get_prompt`; rubric = detector rule `message`).
3. Add the `run_det_floor` project-DET phase (file_impact-scoped) + DET-tier isolation from the LLM
   batch.

### Schema + migration details (clears T8/T4/E1/E5 advisories)
- **`detector` selector shape:** each DET routing entry carries `detector: {id?: str, id_prefix?:
  str}` — an exact detector id OR an id-prefix (the security class uses `id_prefix:
  "rebar.builtin.security."`, reproducing today's behaviour data-drivenly). Validated in
  `_validate_routing_entry`.
- **`fail_mode` default is SAFE:** the built-in security rows in the packaged
  `code_review/criteria_routing.json` are written with an EXPLICIT `fail_mode: "closed"` in this
  change, so they can never silently revert to open. A DET routing entry with NO `fail_mode`
  defaults to `"open"` (project-invariant default); the consumer resolves the value per-criterion.
  `_validate_routing_entry` rejects a `fail_mode` outside `{open, closed}`.
- **Rename:** `run_security_detectors` → `run_detectors`; `run_security_detectors` is KEPT as a thin
  deprecated alias (a test asserts the alias still returns the same result) so no external caller
  breaks.
- **Tests:** `fail_mode` branching (open abstain→skip=PASS, closed abstain→BLOCK) over a fixture;
  the three-way plan-review scoping (file_impact-present→finding, absent→advisory, diff-needed→N/A);
  the alias equivalence; DET-not-in-LLM-batch isolation.


## Docs deliverable (remediation, run 5)

An ADR (`docs/adr/0016-project-det-invariants.md`) — or an equivalent section extending ADR 0015 —
captures for future maintainers: (1) the **two-phase `run_det_floor`** contract (static P1–P9 tuple
+ a call-time dynamic project-DET phase discovered from `ctx.repo_root`, each check fail-open);
(2) the **`exec: DET` LLM-batch exclusion** rule (the filter sites: `orchestrator.route_criteria`
and `workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria` skip DET descriptors so a prompt-less DET criterion
is never handed to `pass1_chunk`); and (3) the **plan-time DET scoping** semantics
(file_impact-scoped → finding / no file_impact → advisory-repo-context / diff-inherent → N/A
fail-open). The routing-schema additions (`detector` selector + `fail_mode`) are documented there
and in the `criteria_routing.json` schema doc.

- [ ] An ADR (or ADR-0015 extension) documents the two-phase run_det_floor contract, the exec:DET
      LLM-batch exclusion, and the plan-time DET scoping semantics.


## Cross-story boundaries (remediation, run 7)

- **Parity gate unaffected:** project DET invariants live in the `.rebar/criteria_routing.json`
  OVERLAY, NOT the packaged plan-review `criteria_routing.json`. So `validate_packaged_routing()`
  (packaged-only, `CANONICAL_LLM` parity — from ef7e) gains NO new keys and is unaffected. The
  `detector` + `fail_mode` fields are ADDITIVE keys WITHIN existing/overlay routing entries, not new
  top-level criterion keys, so the orphan check does not fire. (The packaged code-review
  `criteria_routing.json`'s security DET rows already exist and are already outside plan-review's
  `CANONICAL_LLM` — that gate is plan-review-scoped and does not read code-review's file.)
- **Attestation invalidation is story `08af`, NOT this story:** `attest.registry_version()` today
  hashes the PACKAGED routing only. Making it hash the `.rebar/` overlay via
  `registry_version(repo_root)` — so that activating/editing a project DET criterion invalidates a
  prior attestation at claim time — is the explicit deliverable of story `08af`. This story (`7f0d`)
  does NOT modify `registry_version`; it only makes the DET criterion RUN. The two compose: 7f0d
  makes project DET invariants execute; 08af makes them attestation-invalidating.
finding: The plan says the DET `_descriptor_from_prompt` branch 'skips `get_prompt`' but does not specify that this check must occur BEFORE the `prompts.get_prompt(...)` call in the function body. The current `_descriptor_from_prompt` calls `get_prompt` unconditionally at line 344 before reading `routing`. A DET criterion has no `plan-review-<cid>.md` prompt file; if the branch guard is placed after the prompt call (e.g. as a post-load fallback), `get_prompt` will raise `RegistryError` or similar before the branch executes.
location: ## What — 'DET fallback descriptor shape (a `_descriptor_from_prompt` DET branch that skips `get_prompt`)'
suggested_fix: Add to AC or Scope: 'The DET branch in `_descriptor_from_prompt` reads the routing entry first; if `routing.get("exec") == "DET"`, it returns the descriptor without calling `get_prompt` (the prompt call is in the `else` branch or after the guard).'

title: Scheduled external contract canary that fails when all live tests skip
plan:
## Why
The live Jira/LLM external tier (`tests/external/`) runs only on `workflow_dispatch`
(`.github/workflows/external-integration.yml`), which sets `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1` and maps
`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`/`OPENAI_API_KEY` but **no `JIRA_*` secrets** and does **not install the
`acli` binary** — and `test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py::_live_jira_ready()` gates on BOTH
(`creds and shutil.which("acli")`). So the live Jira round-trip can only ever SKIP: Jira
API/SDK drift merges through normal CI with no real integration signal, and nothing fails a
run in which the whole external tier silently skips. VALIDATED.

Correction to the earlier framing: the live test is NOT read-only — it calls `create_issue`
twice + `set_relationship`, then in a `try/finally` deletes every artifact it created (links
then issues). It makes REAL, self-cleaning Jira mutations against whatever project the creds
point at, so it requires a DEDICATED disposable test project, not a shared one.

## What
1. **Workflow** (`external-integration.yml`): add an `on.schedule` trigger with the exact cron
   `0 6 * * 1` (Mondays 06:00 UTC, weekly) alongside `workflow_dispatch`, and MIRROR the
   established `reconcile-bridge.yml` convention
   EXACTLY (not an ad-hoc variant):
   - Jira env, sourced the SAME way reconcile-bridge does: `JIRA_URL: ${{ vars.JIRA_URL }}`,
     `JIRA_USER: ${{ vars.JIRA_USER }}` (repo VARIABLES, not secrets), and
     `JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}` (the only secret), and `JIRA_PROJECT: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT }}` (the live Jira tests read it for the target project).
   - the `acli` install, reusing reconcile-bridge's THREE companion steps verbatim in shape:
     the `cache-acli` cache step, the `Download + verify + install acli` step (pinned
     `vars.ACLI_VERSION` + `vars.ACLI_SHA256`, sha256-checked), AND the `Add acli to PATH and
     authenticate` step exactly as reconcile-bridge does it: `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli"
     /usr/local/bin/acli` (symlink the binary into the on-PATH `/usr/local/bin`, NOT
     `$GITHUB_PATH`), then `echo "$JIRA_API_TOKEN" | acli jira auth login --site "$JIRA_URL"
     --email "$JIRA_USER" --token`. Without this step `shutil.which("acli")` and the live auth
     both fail, so the Jira tests still skip.
2. **Fail-on-all-skip Jira canary** — deterministic, marker-based (NOT skip-string matching,
   which is fragile across the tier's many distinct skip reasons), and SELF-MAINTAINING (no
   hardcoded module list, so a future Jira module is auto-covered). All Jira live modules share
   one gate signature: a module-level `_live_jira_ready()` + `_skip = skipif(not
   _live_jira_ready(), …)` (currently `test_link_sync_live.py`, `test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py`,
   `test_reconcile_live.py`, `test_verified_fake_contract_live.py`, but the hook keys off the
   signature, not this list).
   - Register a `jira_live` marker (pyproject `[tool.pytest.ini_options].markers`).
   - In `tests/external/conftest.py`, a `pytest_collection_modifyitems` hook AUTO-APPLIES the
     `jira_live` marker to every collected item whose `item.module` defines `_live_jira_ready`
     (i.e. every Jira-gated module, present or future) — no per-file `@pytest.mark` edits, no
     enumeration to drift.
   - A `pytest_sessionfinish(session, exitstatus)` hook (which sets a nonzero `session.exitstatus` to fail the run) that, when
     `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL` is truthy, REPORTS the `jira_live` executed-vs-skipped counts and
     FAILS the session (nonzero exit) iff ≥1 `jira_live` test was COLLECTED but ZERO executed
     (passed or failed) — exactly the "Jira secrets/acli missing, no real signal" condition.
     Membership is the marker (deterministic), so unrelated always-on tests
     (`test_model_requests_enabled`, `test_deps_live`) neither mask nor trip it.
   - When `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL` is unset (the default suite, tier inert via the opt-in guard),
     the hook is a no-op.
3. **Tests**:
   - a behavioral test of the fail-on-all-skip hook over SIMULATED session reports: a session
     where all `jira_live` tests skipped (with `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1`) makes the hook signal
     failure; a session where ≥1 `jira_live` test executed passes; and an unset
     `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL` is a no-op. (Drive the hook function directly with constructed
     report objects — no live creds needed.)
   - a config-as-artifact test asserting `external-integration.yml` declares an `on.schedule`
     trigger, maps `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` into the external job env, AND
     installs `acli` / puts it on PATH (a drift guard mirroring the repo's YAML-artifact tests).

## Scope
- `.github/workflows/external-integration.yml` — schedule + JIRA_* env + acli install/PATH/auth.
- `pyproject.toml` — register the `jira_live` pytest marker.
- `tests/external/conftest.py` — register-free auto-marking (`pytest_collection_modifyitems`
  keyed on `_live_jira_ready`) + the marker-scoped fail-on-all-skip session hook + report.
- A test module (e.g. `tests/unit/test_external_canary.py`) — the hook behavior + workflow wiring.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The workflow declares `on.schedule` with cron `0 6 * * 1`; sources `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_PROJECT`
      from `vars.*` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*` (matching reconcile-bridge.yml); and
      installs `acli` + symlinks it onto PATH + authenticates (the cache / download-verify-install
      / add-to-PATH-and-authenticate steps). A config-as-artifact test pins the cron, the Jira
      env wiring, and the acli PATH/auth step.
- [ ] A `jira_live` marker is registered and AUTO-APPLIED (via a collection hook keyed on
      `_live_jira_ready`) to every Jira-gated live module; the
      conftest hook FAILS an external session (with `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1`) iff ≥1 `jira_live`
      test was collected but zero executed (all skipped), PASSES when a `jira_live` test
      executed, and is a no-op when `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL` is unset — asserted by a behavioral
      test driving the hook over constructed report objects.
- [ ] [operator-attested] The operator configures the `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER` repo VARIABLES,
      the `JIRA_API_TOKEN` SECRET, and the `ACLI_VERSION`/`ACLI_SHA256` repo variables, and
      provisions a DEDICATED disposable Jira test project (these require GitHub repo-admin + a
      Jira project and cannot be done from the code change). CONCRETE evidence to record as a
      comment on this ticket: the URL of a manual `workflow_dispatch` run of the
      `external-integration` workflow whose `external` job is GREEN with the live Jira
      round-trip test EXECUTED (passed, not skipped) — i.e. the fail-on-all-skip canary did NOT
      fire — plus the run id/timestamp. A blank "done" without that run URL does not satisfy it.
- [ ] `make lint && make test` green.
finding: Adding `on.schedule` at the workflow level in `external-integration.yml` fires BOTH the `external` job AND the `langfuse-trace` job weekly. The `langfuse-trace` job spins up a full Docker-compose Langfuse stack (multiple containers, 60-second health poll, ClickHouse/MinIO), which is expensive and unrelated to the plan's stated goal of canary-ing the Jira round-trip. The plan never addresses this unintended side-effect. A clearly better structural alternative — a separate dedicated canary workflow or job-level scheduling — would scope the schedule to only the `external` job.
location: What / item 1 (workflow schedule trigger) + .github/workflows/external-integration.yml (langfuse-trace job, lines 74–164)
suggested_fix: Add `if: github.event_name != 'schedule'` to the `langfuse-trace` job, so `on.schedule` only runs the `external` (Jira/LLM canary) job. This is the minimal change and avoids the cost of a separate workflow file.

title: S3 — OIDC / JWKS JWT verifier
plan:
# S3 — OIDC / JWKS JWT verifier

## Why
JWKS-backed JWT validation is the "OAuth" half of the confirmed floor and the generic "plug into
any OIDC provider" path — one verifier covers AWS Cognito, Microsoft Entra ID, Auth0, Okta,
Keycloak, WorkOS, Google ID tokens, and GitHub Actions OIDC (all publish a JWKS and issue standard
JWTs). It is the highest-leverage verifier for the "wide variety of environments" goal.

## What
Add a **`JWKSTokenVerifier`** to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`, selected by the `jwt` token in
`mcp.auth_strategies`, with flat config keys it registers: `auth_jwt_jwks_uri`, `auth_jwt_issuer`,
`auth_jwt_algorithms` (default `RS256,ES256`), `auth_jwt_leeway` (seconds, default 60),
`auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` (seconds, default 30), `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (seconds, default
10), `auth_jwt_expected_typ` (default **unset**), and `auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host` (bool,
default `false`). It does **not** take a per-verifier audience — it validates
`aud == auth_resource_server_url` (the single resource from S2). Implemented with PyJWT
(`jwt.PyJWKClient` + `jwt.decode`); add `pyjwt[crypto]` to the `[mcp]` extra (the SDK pin lives in
S1). It validates the signature (pinned algorithms), `iss`, `aud`, `exp`/`iat`/`nbf`, optionally the
header `typ` (when `auth_jwt_expected_typ` is set — see Security), maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes, sets
`expires_at` from `exp` (a real positive value), and stamps `resource=auth_resource_server_url` so
the composite audience check passes. This story registers its keys in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` +
regenerates the reference docs.

## Approach & rationale
Use PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + `kid` lookup) and `jwt.decode` with an
explicit pinned `algorithms` allowlist — PyJWT's documented JWKS path and the same shape FastMCP's
`JWTVerifier` uses, giving provider-generic OIDC validation in a small module on the SDK seam. PyJWT
is already a transitive of `mcp`; `pyjwt[crypto]` makes the `cryptography` backend explicit for RS/ES
verification. **PyJWKClient's `lifespan` is a cache TTL, not an unknown-`kid` flood guard**, so this
verifier implements the unknown-`kid` refetch cooldown itself (see the concurrency-safe design in
Security) rather than relying on the library for it.

**Async-safety of the blocking JWKS fetch.** `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt()` (and its
underlying JWKS HTTP fetch) is **synchronous/blocking**, but the verifier's `verify_token` runs on
FastMCP's async event loop. The verifier therefore offloads every key lookup / JWKS fetch to a worker
thread via `asyncio.to_thread(...)` so it never blocks the event loop, and it serializes the
cooldown check-and-update with an `asyncio.Lock` (see Security) so the "at most one fetch per
cooldown window" guarantee holds under concurrent requests.

## Scope
The JWKS/JWT verifier only, on the S2 seam. Not introspection/proxy/custom. Subset of the epic.
Depends on S2.

## Security
Boundary-crossing. Hard requirements (RFC 8725 JWT BCP):
- **Algorithm pinning:** reject `alg:none`; never derive the algorithm from the token header. Also
  **refuse to start** if any **symmetric** algorithm is configured in `auth_jwt_algorithms` together
  with a JWKS source (asymmetric-only on the JWKS path) — this blocks the RS256↔HS256 confusion
  attack (signing HS256 with the public key as the HMAC secret). The symmetric set that triggers the
  startup refusal is the closed, explicitly-enumerated family **`{HS256, HS384, HS512}`** (case-
  insensitive); anything outside the asymmetric families `RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA` on a JWKS source is
  likewise refused.
- Mandatory `aud == auth_resource_server_url` (RFC 8707), `iss == auth_jwt_issuer`, and `exp`/`nbf`
  with bounded `leeway`.
- **Cross-JWT-confusion `typ` check (configurable, because OIDC providers vary):** when
  `auth_jwt_expected_typ` is set, the JWT header `typ` MUST equal it (RFC 8725 §3.11 — e.g.
  `at+JWT` per the RFC 9068 access-token profile), so an ID token or other same-issuer/same-aud JWT
  cannot be replayed as an access token. It defaults **unset** (check skipped, relying on
  `aud`+`iss`+`scope`) because many IdPs do not stamp `typ` on access tokens; S7 documents that
  operators SHOULD set `auth_jwt_expected_typ=at+JWT` for RFC 9068-compliant IdPs.
- **SSRF-safe JWKS fetch:** `jwks_uri` is fixed at construction from trusted config (never the token
  — no `jku`/`x5u`), **HTTPS-only** (an `http://` URI is rejected at construction), with a bounded
  HTTP timeout given by the named key **`auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (seconds, default `10`)**, passed as
  the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient` so a slow/hung JWKS endpoint cannot stall the verifier.
  Private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`, RFC 1918, loopback) are **rejected by default**;
  an operator with a legitimate internal IdP sets `auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host=true` to opt out.
- **Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe):** the JWKS is cached with a bounded TTL; the verifier
  caps refetch on an unknown `kid` to **at most one refetch per `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown`
  seconds (default 30)** (implemented by the verifier itself, not PyJWKClient's `lifespan`). Because
  `verify_token` runs concurrently on the async event loop, the cooldown's read-check-then-update of
  the last-refetch timestamp is a check-then-act (TOCTOU) hazard; it is therefore made **atomic under
  an `asyncio.Lock`** that serializes the check-and-update, so two coroutines seeing the same
  unknown `kid` cannot both trigger a refetch. Result: an attacker flooding random `kid`s cannot force
  more than one JWKS fetch per cooldown window even under concurrency, while a legitimate rotation
   still refetches after the cooldown.
  **Timestamp-on-failure policy:** the last-refetch timestamp is updated on every fetch *attempt* (success OR failure), not only on success, so a JWKS endpoint that errors cannot be used to bypass the flood guard by making fetches fail. The cost is bounded: a transient JWKS outage delays a legitimate refetch by at most one `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` window (default 30 s), and during that window a fetch failure raises → the composite denies (the request fails regardless), so no invalid token is admitted; operators seeing persistent outages lengthen the endpoint's availability, not this guard.
- A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny); a definitively invalid
  token returns None. All verify-path errors go through the S2 redaction helper so no token/secret
  substring is ever logged.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `pyproject.toml` adds `pyjwt[crypto]` to `[mcp]`; `make typecheck` resolves the imports.
- [ ] The `auth_jwt_*` flat keys (including `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout`) are registered in
      `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, appear in the regenerated reference docs, and
      `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` passes.
- [ ] Unit test (tier: unit against the real verifier; the irreducible boundary — the JWKS HTTP
      fetch — is a stateful fake serving a locally-generated key set) asserts: a JWT with correct
      `iss`/`aud`(=resource) → an `AccessToken` with the expected scopes AND
      `resource==auth_resource_server_url` and an `expires_at` **equal to the token's `exp` claim**
      (observable oracle: returned `AccessToken.expires_at` == the exact `exp` used to mint the JWT,
      not merely a positive value).
- [ ] Unit tests assert rejection for: wrong `iss`; `aud` ≠ `auth_resource_server_url`; expired
      `exp`; `nbf` in the future; `alg:none`; and an `HS256` token signed with the RSA public key as
      the HMAC secret — each a distinct negative control.
- [ ] Unit test asserts that with `auth_jwt_expected_typ=at+JWT` set, a token whose header `typ` is
      absent/mismatched is rejected while a matching one is accepted (observable oracle: accept vs
      reject); and that with `auth_jwt_expected_typ` unset the typ check is skipped.
- [ ] Unit test asserts the verifier **refuses to start** when `auth_jwt_algorithms` includes a
      symmetric algorithm (`HS256`/`HS384`/`HS512`) alongside a JWKS source (observable oracle:
      startup raises).
- [ ] Unit test asserts construction-time SSRF guards: an `http://` `jwks_uri` is rejected; a
      private/link-local host is rejected by default and accepted only with
      `auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host=true`; and the JWKS client is constructed with
      `timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (default `10` s) (observable oracle: for the SSRF rejections,
      construction raises; for the timeout, the test patches `jwt.PyJWKClient` and asserts it was
      called with keyword `timeout=10` — the concrete default value — so the assertion is exact and
      unambiguous, not merely 'a timeout is passed').
- [ ] Unit test asserts a JWKS **fetch failure raises** (→ composite non-acceptance/deny), distinct
      from a definitively-invalid token which returns None.
- [ ] Unit test asserts unknown-`kid` cooldown is concurrency-safe: a burst of concurrent requests
      with distinct unknown `kid`s within one cooldown window triggers **exactly one** JWKS fetch
      (observable oracle: the stateful JWKS fake's request count == 1 for the burst — not 0, which
      would mean the lock wrongly blocks all fetches, and not >1, which would mean the TOCTOU
      double-fetch was not prevented), while a token signed by
      a rotated key validates after the cooldown elapses (a legitimate rotation still refetches).
- [ ] Integration test (httpx `ASGITransport`) asserts a valid JWKS-issued token → 200 and a
      garbage bearer → 401 end-to-end through the transport.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.

## Compatibility
Additive verifier, opt-in via the `jwt` strategy. The `pyjwt[crypto]` add is backward-compatible;
the SDK pin already lives in S1.
finding: The plan mandates `typ`/token-class validation (RFC 8725 §3.11) but `jwt.decode()` does not enforce the `typ` header claim by default in PyJWT, and the plan provides no implementation direction for this check. The developer must either inspect the unverified header pre-decode or use a post-decode verified-claims check, both of which are non-obvious PyJWT usage patterns.
location: Security section ('a `typ`/token-class check (RFC 8725 §3.11)') and AC line 4 ('a wrong token class (`typ`)' as a negative control)
suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Because PyJWT does not validate the `typ` header claim during `decode()`, the verifier must explicitly check it: call `jwt.get_unverified_header(token)` and assert `typ` is in the set of accepted access-token type values (e.g., `{"at+JWT", "JWT"}` — or a configurable `auth_jwt_token_type` with default `at+JWT`) before passing the token to `decode()`. Reject tokens with a missing or disallowed `typ` by returning `None`.'

title: WS3: fold ensure-sweep into existing rebar fsck --repair + read-only ensures: N/M line + MCP startup
plan:
## Why
`origin/main` ALREADY has `rebar fsck --repair` (the A3 data-remediation verb: rebuild
snapshots, retire orphans; requires mutation; `--dry-run`/`--limit`). Rather than add a
colliding second mutation flag, fold the ensure-sweep into that existing "drive to healthy"
verb, and give plain read-only fsck an ensures report line. The server boot path should also
converge.

## What
WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`,
`registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, and `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]`. WS3 wires them into
three surfaces.

**Fold `run_ensures()` into `fsck --repair`.** In `fsck.py::fsck_cli`, the `if do_repair:` block
(~line 725) already calls `_repair_run()` (the A3 data phase) then re-scans. Add the ensure-sweep
there as a DISTINCT phase, only on a live (non-`--dry-run`) run: call
`ensures.run_ensures(tracker)` and append a short summary of the outcomes to the report
(`ensures: swept N unit(s); C changed, F failed`, plus a per-unit line for any non-`ok` unit).
`run_ensures` acquires AND RELEASES the store write lock INTERNALLY (per-sweep), so it does not
nest inside `_repair_run()`'s batch and is never held across anything else — no deadlock. The
ensure phase is log-and-continue and DISTINCT from data-repair: a failed unit (already caught by
`run_ensures` → `failed`) never aborts the data-repair and never rolls back committed ticket data.
NO new `--fix`/mutation flag is introduced. `--dry-run --repair` stays read-only (no sweep). This
means existing `fsck --repair` callers now ALSO converge config — an acknowledged behavioral change.

**Read-only `ensures: N/M applied` line.** In `_scan()` (the read-only path, shared by plain
`fsck` and the `--repair` re-scan), append one informational line (no `issue_count` change)
derived WITHOUT calling `run_ensures`: `M = len(ensures.registry_ids())`,
`N = len(ensures.applied_ids(tracker) & ensures.registry_ids())` (intersection so a stale marker
id can't inflate N). Print `ensures: N/M applied`; when `N < M` append ` — run `rebar fsck
--repair` to converge`. An absent/garbage marker yields `applied_ids == set()` → `ensures: 0/M
applied`. Because this lives in `_scan`, the library read-only surface (`rebar.fsck` /
`fsck_cli(..., no_mutate=True)`) prints the line but NEVER sweeps.

The line is INFORMATIONAL (lowercase `ensures:` tag), NOT a structural issue: like the existing
`a3-remediation:` repair lines and the `fsck complete` summary, it is INTENTIONALLY excluded from
`--output json` — `_transform_json` only lifts `^[A-Z_]+:`-tagged structural issues + `FIXED:`
lines into the JSON payload, so a lowercase `ensures:` line is (correctly) skipped there and never
inflates `issue_count` or flips the exit code. Uppercasing the tag would wrongly enroll a converged
store's report line as a JSON "issue"; text-only is the deliberate, precedent-matching choice. So
the `ensures: N/M` line appears in the human text surface only; it does NOT change the JSON schema.

**MCP server startup.** In `mcp_server.main`, AFTER the `--help` early-return and BEFORE
`build_server().run()`, best-effort converge the store: resolve the tracker
(`config.tracker_dir()`), and if it is an existing dir call `ensures.run_ensures(tracker, timeout=…, attempts=1)`
with a SHORT lock budget (a few seconds) so a contended lock skips rather than delaying boot.
`run_ensures` acquires and RELEASES its own write lock internally — it is NOT held across
`build_server().run()`, and the server boot itself runs under NO lock. Wrap the whole thing in a
`try/except` that log-and-continues: an ImportError, a missing store, or any sweep error must
never abort boot. (WS3 adds an optional `timeout`/`attempts` passthrough to `run_ensures` that
forwards to `write_lock`; default `None` keeps WS1's behavior unchanged.)

**Tests owned by WS3 (fsck + MCP surface).** There is NO existing byte-parity golden for the fsck
REPORT output — the only fsck goldens are the static `--help` text
(`tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__dashhelp.out` / `fsck__help.out`, exercised by
`tests/interfaces/facades/test_cli_argparse.py`), which the new report line does NOT change, and
`tests/interfaces/facades/test_library.py::test_fsck_runs` only asserts `"fsck"` is present. So no
golden/byte-parity file needs editing (verified: `python scripts/…` / rerun `test_cli_argparse`
stays green). WS3 adds a NEW `tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_ensures.py` asserting the surfaces
it owns — including the MCP startup: a test monkeypatches `mcp_server.build_server` to return a
stub whose `.run()` records that it was called, spies `ensures.run_ensures`, invokes
`mcp_server.main()` (argv without `--help`), and asserts `run_ensures` was called BEFORE
`build_server().run()`; a second case passes `--help` and asserts `run_ensures` is NOT called
(early return); a third patches `run_ensures` to raise and asserts `main()` still calls
`.run()` (boot never aborts).

## Scope
In: folding `run_ensures` into `--repair`, the read-only `ensures: N/M` line + its
marker/registry data-flow, `no_mutate` gating, MCP server-startup wiring (best-effort, short-lock,
never-under-boot-lock), the `run_ensures` timeout/attempts passthrough, and the new fsck test file.
Out: the registry/`run_ensures` (WS1), the write-path hint (WS2), the authoritative git-log
zero-commit / hot-path / concurrency / no_mutate-never-sweep behavioral suite (WS5).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `rebar fsck --repair` additionally runs `run_ensures()` as a distinct phase (folded into the existing A3 path; `run_ensures` takes + releases the store write lock internally); NO new mutation flag is introduced; `--dry-run --repair` does not sweep
- [ ] `rebar fsck --repair` on a pending store converges it (writes `.ensure-applied`) and reports the swept units; a second `--repair` reports no ensure changes (the fsck-level idempotency check; the authoritative git-log zero-commit assertion lives in WS5)
- [ ] Plain read-only `rebar fsck` prints `ensures: N/M applied` (M=`registry_ids()`, N=applied∩registry) WITHOUT calling `run_ensures`; an absent marker prints `0/M`; the library `no_mutate=True` surface prints the line but never sweeps
- [ ] The informational lowercase `ensures:` line is intentionally text-only: it is NOT emitted into `--output json` (consistent with `a3-remediation:`/`fsck complete`) and never inflates the JSON `issue_count` or changes the exit code; a test asserts `--output json` on a pending store still parses and reports the correct issue_count
- [ ] MCP server startup calls `run_ensures()` after the --help check and before `build_server().run()`, best-effort (log-and-continue, never aborts boot) with a SHORT lock budget; the lock is released before boot (never held across `build_server().run()`). Proven by a test that stubs `build_server` + spies `run_ensures` and asserts: (a) `main()` (no --help) calls `run_ensures` BEFORE `.run()`; (b) `main(["--help"])` does NOT call `run_ensures`; (c) when `run_ensures` raises, `main()` still calls `.run()`
- [ ] No existing fsck golden/byte-parity test changes (the report line is additive and the `--help` goldens are unaffected); WS3's new `tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_ensures.py` covers the N/M line, `--repair` sweep + fsck-level idempotency, and `no_mutate`-never-sweep
finding: The MCP server-startup `run_ensures()` call (AC3) has no concurrency/locking specification, while the `fsck --fix` call is specified to run 'under the write lock'. If a server boots while a `rebar fsck --fix` is in flight (or two servers boot simultaneously), the ensures write path races with no protection. The established codebase pattern is `lock.acquire(tracker, timeout=30, attempts=2, dual_window=True)`.
location: AC3 — 'MCP server startup runs run_ensures() after the --help check'; What section — 'fsck --fix runs run_ensures() (WS1) under the write lock'

title: Code-review finding-memory reader + artifact payload (session_id + reviewed-file content-hash map)
plan:
## Why
Code review reviews a DIFF fresh with NO memory across runs. The durable `code_review`
artifact (per Gerrit change/revision) today persists SURFACED findings write-only (a
calibration corpus). This story adds the READER + the content-addressed STATE the region-gated
novelty floor (blameless-grindable-noctule) needs, and extends the `code_review_result_v1`
payload with the two new fields that state.

## What
- **Payload extension (`code_review_result_v1`, `build_payload`).** Add two fields, both read
  off the verdict dict so they are populated once and flow to EVERY emit path unchanged:
  - `session_id`: TOP-LEVEL, nullable. `build_payload` reads `verdict.get("session_id")`
    (None for Gerrit reviews; set for local session-keyed reviews by the persistence story
    paradoxal-balsamic-bubblefish). Makes local memory queryable by session.
  - `deps`: a `{reviewed-file path: sha256}` content-hash map. `build_payload` reads
    `verdict.get("deps")` (defaulting to `{}`).
- **deps collector + computation seam (`produce_code_review_verdict`).** A thin collector hashes
  each reviewed-file path once and stashes the map on the verdict (`verdict["deps"]`) in
  `_run_code_review_gate`, computed UNCONDITIONALLY after the verdict is produced and BEFORE the
  sidecar emit. This requires importing `code_review.sidecar` (for the collector +
  `_cited_paths_code_review`) EARLIER in `_run_code_review_gate` — at the top of the finalize block
  rather than only inside the existing `if request.target_ticket:` block (an explicit new import in
  `gate_dispatch.py`, enumerated here). Because it lives on the verdict dict, BOTH
  emit callers pick it up via `build_payload` with NO change to either call site:
  - the produce-path emit (`_run_code_review_gate` → `sidecar.emit`), and
  - the Gerrit voter emit (`voter.py::emit_code_review_artifact` → `sidecar.emit`), which passes
    the SAME verdict object it received — so it inherits `deps`/`session_id` for free (voter.py
    stays OUT of scope; it needs no edit).
  - **Reuse boundary:** the collector REUSES the private `attest._hash_file(path, base=…)`
    primitive (raw file-bytes sha256; a missing/unreadable path → the `absent` sentinel, so a
    create/delete is itself detectable) and `attest._hash_basis(repo_root)` for the base. This
    reuse of a private symbol is EXPLICITLY sanctioned by the epic design ("private `_hash_file`
    primitive reused; NOT `dependency_hashes`, which is plan/ticket-coupled"). It does NOT reuse
    `dependency_hashes()` (that pulls ticket file_impact + plan-verdict citation buckets).
  - **Scope of `deps`** = the review's `changed_files` UNION its cited-file paths. NOTE:
    `attest._cited_paths(verdict)` is NOT reusable here — it reads a `citations` list with
    `kind == "file"`, but code-review findings carry no such list (they only have a `location`
    string, e.g. `path` or `path:line`). So define a small `_cited_paths_code_review(verdict)`
    in `code_review/sidecar.py` that extracts the FILE path (strip any `:line` suffix) from each
    finding's `location` across the `blocking` + `advisory` buckets, ignoring empty/non-file
    locations. `deps` = `sorted(set(changed_files) | _cited_paths_code_review(verdict))`.
  - **Basis:** hashed via `attest._hash_basis(repo_root)` at the emit point. Because deps are
    computed AFTER the workflow's `gate_read_root` context has exited, `current_code_root()` is
    already `None`, so `_hash_basis` resolves to the in-place WORKING TREE (`_config.repo_root`) —
    NOT the attested snapshot. This is correct for both keyspaces: a LOCAL `review-code` reviews the
    working tree (so the working tree IS the reviewed basis), and the Gerrit voter bot checks out
    the patchset revision (so its working tree IS the reviewed revision). The content-addressed
    region gate only ever compares hashes WITHIN one keyspace, and each keyspace hashes its own
    working-tree basis consistently across runs, which is all it needs.
  - **Failure posture:** the collector is best-effort — a per-file hash failure yields the
    `absent` sentinel (never raises); a total failure leaves `deps` empty and is logged. It must
    NEVER fail the gate (mirrors the sidecar's best-effort posture).
- **Artifact addressing (title schemes, TYPED keyspaces).** Local artifacts use title
  `code-review: session:{session_id}`; Gerrit artifacts keep `code-review: {change_id} @ {revision}`
  (unchanged). These are two disjoint keyspaces so a prior LOCAL review can never seed a change's
  FIRST Gerrit review.
- **Reader `latest_code_review_result(key, *, repo_root=None)`.** `key` is a TYPED string:
  `session:<id>` or `change:<id>`. Resolution by artifact title:
  - `session:<id>` → the artifact whose title == `code-review: session:{id}` (exact; one artifact).
  - `change:<id>` → STRIP the `change:` tag to get the bare `change_id`, then match artifacts whose
    title starts with `code-review: {change_id} @ ` (spans revisions of the same change; the change
    is the memory key, the revision only makes each artifact idempotent). An unknown key kind → None.
  Artifacts are enumerated via `rebar.list_tickets(ticket_type="code_review")` and matched by title
  in-process (no new list-filter API). Across the matched artifact(s), the reader returns the
  most-recent usable `code_review_result_v1` sidecar payload's SURFACED findings + its `deps` map.
  - **SURFACED-only (mandatory; see old-frilly-plankton).** `code_review_result_v1` stores findings
    in SEPARATE top-level buckets (`blocking`, `advisory`, `coaching`) with NO per-finding
    `decision` field (unlike `plan_review_result_v1`). Therefore "surfaced" is DEFINED as the union
    of the `blocking` and `advisory` buckets; the reader returns ONLY those two buckets (never
    `coaching`, never any future dropped bucket). Dropped findings are never persisted into
    blocking/advisory, so they can never re-enter the novelty prior set.
  - **Posture:** mirrors `plan_review.sidecar.latest_review_result` — best-effort, NEVER raises,
    schema-guarded (only `code_review_result_v1` payloads), walks newest→oldest over each artifact's
    sidecar events tolerating a malformed/foreign newest file, returns None when nothing usable.
    A reader/hash/compare error degrades to None (⇒ the floor runs un-narrowed = no drops).

## Scope
- `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` — payload extension (`build_payload`), the thin deps
  collector, and the `latest_code_review_result` reader.
- `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` — the `deps` computation seam in
  `produce_code_review_verdict` / `_run_code_review_gate` (stash on the verdict before emit).
- OUT of scope: local persistence wiring / setting `session_id` (paradoxal-balsamic-bubblefish);
  the region_gated_floor consumer (blameless-grindable-noctule); `voter.py` (inherits via the
  verdict — no edit).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `build_payload` adds a nullable top-level `session_id` (read from `verdict.get("session_id")`)
      and a `deps` `{path: sha256}` map (read from `verdict.get("deps")`, default `{}`).
- [ ] A thin deps collector reuses `attest._hash_file` (NOT `dependency_hashes`) and the `absent`
      sentinel; scope = `changed_files` ∪ `_cited_paths_code_review(verdict)` (paths parsed from
      each finding's `location`, since code-review findings have no `citations[kind==file]` list).
- [ ] The deps map is computed ONCE in `produce_code_review_verdict`, UNCONDITIONALLY (regardless of
      `target_ticket`), and stashed on the verdict — importing `code_review.sidecar` earlier in
      `_run_code_review_gate` — so both the produce emit and the Gerrit voter emit carry it via
      `build_payload` (voter unchanged).
- [ ] `latest_code_review_result(key)` resolves `session:<id>` (exact title `code-review: session:{id}`)
      and `change:<id>` (strip `change:`, title prefix `code-review: {id} @`); unknown kind → None.
- [ ] The reader returns SURFACED findings = union of the payload's `blocking` + `advisory` buckets
      (never `coaching`/dropped) plus the `deps` map; schema-guarded to `code_review_result_v1`.
- [ ] The reader is best-effort and NEVER raises; malformed/absent/foreign-schema → None (⇒ no drops).
- [ ] Tests: payload carries `session_id` + `deps`; deps collector hashes changed+cited paths with
      the `absent` sentinel for a missing file; reader resolves both typed keyspaces, returns
      surfaced-only, and degrades to None on a malformed/absent artifact.
finding: The plan treats SURFACED-only filtering (per old-frilly-plankton) as settled behavior the reader can rely on, but `surfaced_findings()` — the helper that was to implement that filtering — does not exist in the codebase yet (grep returns zero matches in src/). The bug ticket is `in_progress` with a comment describing an implementation, but no code is present, meaning the prerequisite contract the plan builds on is unverified.
location: What section: 'SURFACED-ONLY is mandatory (see old-frilly-plankton) so dropped findings never re-enter the novelty prior set'; AC: 'latest_code_review_result(key) returns SURFACED-only prior findings'
suggested_fix: Add a hard prerequisite note: 'Depends on old-frilly-plankton being merged (surfaced_findings helper must be in sidecar.py).' Alternatively, define the filter (decision in {block, advisory}) inline in latest_code_review_result without importing the helper, and note it should be refactored to use the shared helper once merged.

title: Operation certificates — asymmetric + environment-attributed + per-project required-environment
plan:
# Operation certificates — asymmetric + environment-attributed + per-project required-environment

## Context
rebar's operation certificates (plan-review and completion-verifier verdicts) are today **local
self-attestations**: signed with a per-clone **symmetric HMAC secret** and verified only by the
same clone (`foreign_key` if verified elsewhere — `src/rebar/signing.py`). Nothing at merge
(Gerrit `Verified` / CI) re-verifies them, so they are local discipline, **not enforceable
controls**, and no third party can verify one without the forge-capable secret. This epic migrates
operation-certs to **asymmetric** signatures on the attestation foundation, **attributes** each to
the signing **environment** (verify-by-anyone against that environment's public key), and lets a
project **optionally require** a certificate from a specific trusted environment. **Beneficiary:**
high-assurance projects that need a gate verdict they can trust was not forged by the developer who
wanted to pass it — while low-security projects keep the fast local-report behavior. This epic
delivers the end state in which **both** signature types are asymmetric.

> **Prerequisites shipped (drift note, 2026-07-13).** Both prerequisite epics have landed on `main`:
> the **foundation** epic (`2fd4` / brilliant-curly-songbird) shipped the attestation substrate at
> `src/rebar/attest/` (DSSE+PAE `dsse.py`, the source-pinned per-kind `POLICY` table in `registry.py`,
> the SSHSIG scheme `sshsig.py`, and HMAC-as-legacy `hmac_legacy.py`); the **identity** epic
> (`e68d` / gnu-whale-ichor) shipped an authenticated-authorship **merge-gate CI job**
> (`.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml`, which walks the *merged log* and grandfathers via
> `ENFORCE_SINCE`), an in-toto **merged-log ledger**, and **era-scoped commit-ancestry key validity**
> (`src/rebar/attest/authorship.py`: validity as commit-ancestry positions
> `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit`, compared with `git merge-base --is-ancestor`; verdict
> `key_not_valid_at_era` is already in `src/rebar/schemas/verify_signature_result.schema.json`). This
> epic was planned before those landed and originally described building the merge-gate, ledger, and
> era-scoped rotation itself. It now **reuses** that shipped machinery rather than building parallel
> copies — see the reuse notes inline below and the proposal comment on this epic.

## Approach (and why)
- **Environment-attributed, asymmetric op-certs.** Any environment signs with its own asymmetric
  Ed25519 key via the foundation's substrate (namespace `rebar.opcert.v1`); the certificate
  attributes to that environment and is verifiable by anyone against its public key. This matches
  the existing per-environment model (a dev machine and a shared server are both "environments")
  and restores the clean two-actor split (op-cert = **environment**; authorship = person, the
  identity epic). The two kinds stay **orthogonal in actor and threat model** — different key,
  trust-root, and namespace, so a signature is never valid across kinds — but the identity epic has
  since **shipped**, so this epic now **reuses** its delivered machinery (the `verify-identity`
  merge-gate CI job, the merged-log ledger, and the era-scoped commit-ancestry key-validity *rule*
  in `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py`) rather than building parallel copies. "Independent" here means
  the dependency is never inverted — op-certs are signed by environment keys, never by identity/person
  keys — **not** that no code is shared.
- **Per-project optional requirement.** A project pins trusted-environment public keys
  **out-of-band** (a Gerrit-protected / CI config path, **never** the auto-pushed tickets branch)
  and may require a specific environment's certificate for a gate. Low-security default: any/local
  certificate is a **process record**, not a control. High-security: the **merge gate** (Gerrit/CI)
  verifies the required environment's signature over the merged log. (Op-cert trust roots are
  **out-of-band and high-authority** — deliberately unlike the identity epic's in-band TOFU authorship
  keys; this distinction is preserved and is the reason op-cert cannot simply inherit identity's trust
  model wholesale even while it reuses identity's era/merged-log plumbing.)
- **Trusted remote execution for high assurance.** To obtain a required-environment certificate, a
  developer routes the gate operation to a trusted `rebar-mcp` server (e.g. on AWS) that runs the
  verifier and signs. **Integrity requirement (load-bearing):** that server MUST fetch the
  authoritative state itself (ticket state from the tickets branch; code at the specific commit from
  the review remote) and run the gate on THAT — never on client-provided inputs — or the signature
  certifies a lie. This is a security/latency tradeoff the project opts into; the default local path
  stays **offline and never wedges** a rebar operation.
- **Verify against the pinned key, not the claimed key_id.** "Require environment E" verifies the
  signature against E's out-of-band-pinned public key; the DSSE keyid is an unauthenticated hint.
  Certificates bind `{ticket id, material fingerprint, merged-log position}` so they cannot be
  replayed onto another ticket / stale state. The merged-log position is a **`main` commit SHA**
  (the commit at which the trusted environment ran the gate); there is no `intra_idx` symbol — the
  substrate's only intra-commit tiebreak is the event's `{timestamp}-{uuid}` position, which does
  not apply to op-cert's explicit-SHA model.
- **HMAC removal (contract phase).** Once op-certs verify asymmetrically, the legacy HMAC scheme
  (`src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py`, registered in the foundation's `attest/registry.py` `POLICY`
  table) is removed — completing the migration begun in the foundation epic. Authorship already signs
  asymmetrically (identity epic), so plan-review / completion-verifier op-certs are the last remaining
  HMAC consumers.

## Trust config & era-validity protocol (design resolution, 2026-07-13)
This section resolves the concrete mechanics the plan previously left abstract (epic plan-review
BLOCK, findings E2/F1/G6/T1/T8). It is the single schema children 368c/42d1/4214/ee0b/8f1d all read.

- **Trust config file.** Trusted-environment public keys are pinned in a **YAML** file at
  **`.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`** (repo-root, resolved relative to the review remote's
  checkout). This path is **out-of-band**: it lives on the code branch (`main`, Gerrit-gated +
  CODEOWNERS-protected), **never** on the auto-pushed `tickets` branch. An absent file means "no
  required environment" — the low-security default (local certs are records, not controls).
- **Schema.**
  ```yaml
  environments:
    - env_id: "<string>"            # logical environment name; MUST equal the DSSE principal (keyid)
      keys:
        - public_key: "ssh-ed25519 AAAA…"   # one OpenSSH ed25519 authorized-keys line
          added_at_commit: "<git-sha-on-main>"      # era start: the main commit at/after which this key is valid
          revoked_at_commit: "<git-sha-on-main>|null"  # era end (null = still valid)
  ```
  `require_environment` (per-gate) names one `env_id` that must produce the certificate.
- **Why explicit SHAs, not tickets-branch positions.** The identity epic's era mechanism resolves a
  key's add/revoke *position* to a commit by globbing `<ticket_dir>/<position>-*.json` **on the
  tickets branch** (`authorship.resolve_event_commit`). Environment keys are **not** on the tickets
  branch, so `resolve_event_commit` / `_keyring_for` **cannot** be reused verbatim. Instead the
  config records `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` as **explicit git SHAs on `main`**. What is
  reused is the **era-validity *rule*** — the `git merge-base --is-ancestor` ancestry comparison and
  the `key_not_valid_at_era` verdict — factored so both authorship (position→commit) and op-cert
  (explicit-commit) call one shared ancestry predicate. Children 368c must reuse the *rule/verdict*,
  not the event-file resolver.
- **Era-validity decision rule.** An op-cert binds a **merged-log position** = the `main` commit `P`
  at which the trusted environment ran the gate (the subject's bound commit). A config key is
  **valid at `P`** iff `git merge-base --is-ancestor(added_at_commit, P)` is true AND
  (`revoked_at_commit is None` OR `git merge-base --is-ancestor(revoked_at_commit, P)` is false). A
  certificate verifies iff **some** currently-valid key for the required `env_id` verifies its
  signature (overlapping rotation windows are permitted, exactly as authorship allows). A cert whose
  only matching key is past its rotation surfaces the shared `key_not_valid_at_era` verdict.
  Revoked keys are **retained in the config keyring** (with `revoked_at_commit` set) so the
  two-phase check's "any historical key" pass can still recognize a signature by a since-rotated
  key and classify it `key_not_valid_at_era` rather than `mismatch`.

## Scope
- **In:** asymmetric environment-signed op-certs on the foundation's substrate; the out-of-band
  `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` environment public-key trust config; per-project optional
  required-environment policy; merge-gate verification (a CI lane added to / mirroring the shipped
  `verify-identity.yaml` gate, **extending** its merged-log walk rather than a fresh walker); the
  trusted `rebar-mcp` remote-execution path that fetches authoritative state; certificate subject
  binding + explicit-SHA env-key era rotation (**reusing the identity epic's commit-ancestry
  validity *rule/verdict***); removal of the legacy HMAC scheme with an expand-contract migration;
  IaC for the trusted MCP-server deployment (IAM SigV4 endpoint auth, least-privilege IAM, Ed25519 key in SSM Parameter Store (SecureString));
  docs + ADR.
- **Out:** the signing substrate itself (foundation epic); person/authorship signing (identity
  epic); the merge-gate walker / ledger / era-validity *rule* itself (identity epic — this epic
  *reuses* it); transparency-log / keyless follow-ons; threshold/multi-sig (deferred idea 5100).

## Success Criteria
- Operation certificates are asymmetric and verifiable by any third party against the signing
  environment's public key; no shared secret is needed to verify.
- A project can require a certificate from a specific trusted environment for a gate; the merge gate
  enforces it over the merged log; without the requirement, local certificates remain a fast record.
- The trusted environment's certificate reflects the authoritative state it fetched itself, not
  client-provided inputs, so a developer cannot forge a passing verdict.
- The legacy HMAC scheme is removed; both operation-cert and authorship signatures are asymmetric.
- The default (low-security, local, offline) path never requires network and never wedges an op.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Operation certificates are produced/verified via the foundation's substrate with an Ed25519
      environment key (namespace `rebar.opcert.v1`), registered as a new kind in the
      `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` `POLICY` table alongside the foundation's HMAC-legacy and the
      identity epic's `rebar.authorship.v1`; a test asserts a certificate signed by environment E
      verifies against E's public key and fails against a different key.
- [ ] A project config pins trusted-environment public keys in the out-of-band, review-gated file
      **`.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`** (schema in "Trust config & era-validity protocol";
      NOT the tickets branch); a test asserts a certificate from an environment not in the pinned
      set fails the required-environment check, and that the check verifies against the pinned key,
      not the certificate's self-claimed `key_id`.
- [ ] A merge-gate CI job verifies the required-environment certificate over the merged log and
      fails when a ticket that must carry one does not; it **extends the shipped `verify-identity.yaml`
      merge-gate** (same merged-log walk + `ENFORCE_SINCE`-style grandfathering) rather than a
      parallel job; a fixture demonstrates a missing/foreign certificate fails the job. CI-trigger
      audit: enumerate the `on:` triggers of `.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml` (the gate the
      op-cert lane is added to) and classify each as INCLUDED (the op-cert check runs under that
      trigger) or NO_FILTER (no `paths`/`branches` filter on that trigger excludes an op-cert-bearing
      change); a comment records the classification so no merge path silently skips the gate.
- [ ] Certificates bind `{ticket id, material fingerprint, merged-log position (a `main` commit SHA)}`;
      a test asserts a certificate cannot be replayed onto a different ticket or a mutated state.
      Environment-key rotation uses **explicit-SHA** era-scoped validity per the "Trust config &
      era-validity protocol" (`added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` in the trust config, compared
      with `git merge-base --is-ancestor` against the cert's bound `main` commit), **reusing the
      commit-ancestry era *rule* and the `key_not_valid_at_era` verdict already in
      `src/rebar/schemas/verify_signature_result.schema.json` (shipped by the identity epic)** — not
      the tickets-branch `resolve_event_commit` resolver; a test asserts a certificate from a key
      valid at its era verifies while one past its rotation surfaces `key_not_valid_at_era`.
- [ ] The trusted remote path fetches ticket state and code-at-commit from authoritative remotes
      itself and runs the gate on that; a test asserts a client-supplied doctored input does NOT
      change the signed verdict. **Transport:** the existing `rebar-mcp` server is stdio-only and is
      NOT network-exposed; the trusted remote path is a **library-mode HTTP service** (rebar imported
      as a library, mirroring the proven `review_bot` pattern — no stdio-MCP transport work) fronted
      by **AWS API Gateway**, which is the SigV4 integration point. The AWS infra authenticates the
      endpoint via **AWS IAM (SigV4-signed requests) restricted to a named admin role**, over TLS;
      declares least-privilege IAM for the signing-key read + repo read; and stores the **Ed25519
      signing key material in AWS SSM Parameter Store as a SecureString (AWS-managed KMS key at rest; the free mechanism rebar already uses for /rebar/prod/* secrets)** — the server
      performs SSHSIG signing itself with that key. (NOT AWS KMS managed signing: KMS asymmetric
      SIGN_VERIFY supports only ECDSA/RSA, not Ed25519, so "KMS" here means envelope-encryption of
      the secret, not the signing operation.) The default **local path stays the offline stdio
      `rebar-mcp` and never wedges an op.**
- [ ] The legacy HMAC scheme is removed; a test asserts no code path signs or accepts an HMAC
      operation-cert. A named migration artifact **`docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md`** exists
      documenting the expand-contract cutover window / re-issue procedure (a test asserts the file
      exists, is non-empty, and describes the expand→contract phases — an `## Expand` and a
      `## Contract` section — so the cutover procedure is present, not effort-framed), rather than a
      flag-day break. Rollback:
      the required-environment policy is opt-in and defaults off (absent
      `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` / no `require_environment`); disabling it reverts to local
      behavior.
- [ ] Docs + ADR: a named ADR artifact `docs/adr/NNNN-opcert-asymmetric.md` exists documenting the
      environment-attribution model, low/high-security postures, the authoritative-state-fetch
      integrity requirement, out-of-band trust root, merge-gate enforcement, and HMAC removal +
      migration, and cross-references ADR 0046 (security posture; L2/L4 are op-cert-scoped). A test
      asserts the ADR file exists, is non-empty, and contains the strings `rebar.opcert.v1`,
      `trusted_environments.yaml`, and `ADR 0046`.

## Intended decomposition (children)
1. Asymmetric environment-signed op-cert kind on the foundation's substrate (+ subject binding
   + explicit-SHA env-key era rotation, folded in from former task 5893) — a new `rebar.opcert.v1`
   kind in `attest/registry.py`'s `POLICY` table; reuses the era-validity *rule/verdict*, not the
   tickets-branch event resolver.
2. Out-of-band `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` key config + required-environment policy +
   verify-against-pinned-key.
3. Merge-gate verification CI job over the merged log (+ new-prohibition consumer scan, CI-trigger
   audit) — **extend the shipped `verify-identity` gate; do not stand up a parallel merged-log walker.**
4. Trusted `rebar-mcp` remote-execution path that fetches authoritative state + its AWS infra (IAM SigV4 endpoint auth, least-privilege IAM, Ed25519 key in SSM Parameter Store (SecureString)).
5. ~~Env-key era-scoped rotation~~ — **folded into child 1** (former task 5893, dropped as
   superseded).
6. HMAC removal (contract) + expand-contract migration artifact (`docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md`).
7. Docs + ADR.

## Prior art / grounding
DSSE (keyid is an unauthenticated hint — verify against pinned keys); OpenSSH `allowed_signers`
validity intervals; git SSH-signing trust-file model; RFC 8725 (alg-confusion); key-transparency
lineage (equivocation) noted as a later option. Depends on the foundation epic's substrate
(`src/rebar/attest/`, shipped). Reuses the identity epic's shipped merge-gate
(`.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml`), merged-log ledger, and commit-ancestry era-validity
*rule* (`src/rebar/attest/authorship.py`).

## Producer-signing & compute-runtime design resolution (2026-07-13, round-6 convergence)

This section resolves the remaining design forks that blocked ee0b/8f1d plan-review convergence
(rounds 1–5): the producer-signing seam, double-signing, verdict-vocabulary normalization, the
compute runtime, and signer identity. It is the single schema the remaining children (the
producer-seam story, ee0b, the IaC/deploy story, 8f1d, 6a89) all read. It supersedes the
"Intended decomposition" list above for the not-yet-built half.

- **Uniform producer signing (the 8f1d fork, resolved: fork A).** Every environment holds its own
  Ed25519 op-cert key and the **gate producers themselves** mint `rebar.opcert.v1` certs: the
  plan-review sign step (`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` `sign_plan_review`, today →
  `signing.sign_manifest` HMAC) and the close-gate sign step
  (`src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` `_sign` path, today → `signing.sign_manifest` HMAC)
  are repointed at `signing.sign_opcert_manifest`. A local cert is a **process record** (exactly
  today's HMAC semantics, but verifiable-by-anyone against the environment's public key); a
  **trusted** cert is the same artifact signed by an environment whose key is pinned in
  `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`. **The trusted server is not a special signer** — what makes
  it trusted is (a) its pinned key and (b) its operational property of fetching authoritative
  state. Fork B (local gates stop signing entirely) is rejected: it would strip the claim/close
  gates' local-tamper evidence and make low-security attestations trivially forgeable (worse than
  HMAC today).
- **Double-signing dissolves by construction.** One signature per verdict, minted by the gate
  producer with the ambient environment key. The remote handler (ee0b) never signs separately and
  never passes `sign=False` — it invokes the same library gate, which signs with the server's key
  because the server is the ambient environment. The HMAC environment key is never provisioned in
  the server.
- **Local key provenance.** Per-environment **passphrase-free Ed25519 keypair** auto-generated on
  first signing use at `<tracker>/.opcert-key` / `.opcert-key.pub` (`ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N ""`,
  0600, git-ignored) — mirroring the HMAC `.signing-key` genesis it replaces. The DSSE
  principal/keyid defaults to the environment's existing `env_id`; `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`
  overrides (deployment config). If `ssh-keygen` (OpenSSH ≥ 8.9) is unavailable, signing degrades
  to the existing in-band `{signed: false, error: …}` record — a local op never wedges; a project
  whose gate *requires* the signature then blocks at the gate with a clear
  install-OpenSSH remediation.
- **No `verify.opcert_env_id` repo-config key (round-5 finding #4, resolved by removal).** The
  signer's identity is **deployment configuration** (env var / task definition), never the target
  repo's `rebar.toml` — a repo under verification must not choose the identity that signs for it.
  Consumer-side policy remains the two keys that already exist in `VerifyConfig`
  (`src/rebar/_config_schema.py`): `verify.require_environment` and `verify.opcert_enforce_since`.
  Client-side remote routing adds exactly ONE new repo key: **`verify.opcert_remote_url`**
  (`str | None = None`; absent = fully local, nothing remote is ever required to run).
- **Verdict vocabularies stay native per kind — no unified enum (round-5 finding #1).**
  plan-review: `PASS|BLOCK|INDETERMINATE`; completion-verifier: `PASS|FAIL`, with `LLMError`
  propagation. The remote response separates **transport status** from **gate verdict**:
  `{status: "completed"|"error", kind, verdict: <the kind's native enum>|null,
  envelope: <DSSE>|null, material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit, error: {class, message}|null}`.
  An op-cert `envelope` is present **only** on a `PASS` verdict. A raised
  `LLMError`/`LLMUnavailableError` maps to `status: "error"` with `verdict: null` — no fabricated
  verdict value.
- **Compute runtime committed (round-5 finding #3; cost-verified against the live AWS account,
  2026-07-13): the existing trusted VM + an API Gateway SigV4 front — zero fixed-monthly-cost
  increase.** An AWS account audit (June bill $90.94 total; rebar's footprint is ONE t4g.large
  `rebar-gerrit` box + Route 53 + free SSM Parameter Store secrets under `/rebar/prod/*`; the
  ECS/ELB/VPC/RDS spend belongs to the co-tenant snap-demo project) set the constraint: **no new
  fixed-monthly-cost resources.** The op-cert service runs as another docker-compose service on
  the existing `rebar-gerrit` box (`infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` — the service slot freed
  by the autolander's retirement, ADR 0047), reusing `review_bot`'s proven FastAPI ACK-fast +
  in-process `asyncio.Queue` + single-worker software pattern (`src/rebar/review_bot/app.py`).
  SigV4/IAM endpoint auth is preserved via an **API Gateway HTTP API** (no fixed monthly fee;
  ~$1/million requests ≈ $0 at gate volume) with an IAM authorizer restricted to a named admin
  role, HTTP-integrated to the box's nginx; the origin accepts op-cert routes only with the
  shared header API Gateway injects (defense in depth). The Ed25519 signing key lives in **SSM
  Parameter Store as a SecureString** (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` — the same free
  mechanism as the box's existing secrets, AWS-managed KMS key at rest); NOT SSM Parameter Store
  (+$0.40/mo/secret), NOT a customer KMS CMK (+$1/mo), and NOT AWS KMS managed signing (KMS
  asymmetric has no Ed25519). **Lambda rejected** (15-min hard ceiling + API Gateway's ~30s
  sync-integration timeout vs 30s–minutes gate runs). **A dedicated Fargate service rejected on
  cost** (~$36/mo always-on task + ~$16/mo NLB/VPC-link + secret/CMK fees ≈ a ~60% increase on
  the account's whole June bill) buying isolation ADR 0046 L4 does not require — the box is
  already the single controlled trusted environment (it holds the Gerrit admin and `LLM-Review`
  vote credentials; a box compromise already defeats the merge gate with or without the op-cert
  key there). **Async job API** (`POST /opcert/jobs` → 202 `{job_id}`; `GET /opcert/jobs/{id}` →
  status/result); job state is in-memory (a restart loses queued jobs; clients re-submit — cert
  minting is idempotent).
- **The server is stateless and store-read-only.** It returns the envelope in the response; the
  **client** persists it as the `SIGNATURE` event (auto-pushed on the tickets branch). This is
  safe because the DSSE envelope is self-authenticating — a client cannot alter the bound
  `{ticket, material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit, kind}` without breaking the signature the
  merge gate verifies. Least privilege: the server holds NO tickets-branch write credential; its
  read credentials are a read-only token for the tickets branch + review remote.
- **Verify-side authoritative-input rule (security finding #4, change 664 — the verify-side twin
  of ee0b's produce-side rule).** The merge gate must never source its EXPECTED values from the
  attacker-writable stored record: `rebar verify-opcert` currently passes the record's own
  `material_fingerprint`/`merged_log_commit` back into verification
  (`src/rebar/_commands/verify_opcert.py:163-178`), which proves only that the record is
  internally consistent — a circular check. The rule, uniform with the produce side ("the
  verifier trusts only signed envelope contents and state it fetched/computed itself"):
  (a) the gate **recomputes** `material_fingerprint` from the authoritative ticket state its own
  merged-log walk produced and requires the signed subject to bind THAT value;
  (b) `merged_log_commit` cannot be recomputed (only the producer knows when it ran), so it is
  **constrained** instead — read only from the signature-verified payload (the record's
  plaintext mirror is a display hint), required to resolve to a real commit that is an ancestor
  of the fetched `main` tip, and required to satisfy the era/enforcement floors
  (`verify.opcert_enforce_since`, the pinned key's `added_at_commit`); any unresolvable value
  fails closed. The produce side already complies by construction (the trusted server binds the
  tip it fetched itself). Residual accepted: a since-revoked trusted key backdating a cert to a
  commit inside its formerly-valid era — bounded by the recomputed-material requirement,
  revocation, and the enforce-since floor; full anti-rollback monotonicity stays deferred per
  ADR 0046 L1 (ticket 2422). Story 4214 carries this remediation (change 664 needs a new
  patchset).
- **Authoritative era anchor (security finding #5, change 664 rejected — SUPERSEDES the
  era-comparison rule in "Trust config & era-validity protocol" above).** The era key-validity
  check must NOT run at the certificate's self-chosen `merged_log_commit`: a holder of a revoked
  (or not-yet-added) environment key can freshly sign a cert that binds any pre-revocation
  ancestor commit — the ancestry constraint proves the commit is real, not that it is the right
  one, so revocation would be unenforceable at the gate (the anti-rollback gap; gittuf shipped
  the same log-position model and still needed a monotonic counter — CVE-2026-44544, noted on
  idea 2422). Resolution — **Option B, era-at-storage-anchor** (chosen over Option A
  "era-at-HEAD", which would collapse the era model and force re-certifying every historical
  ticket on each routine rotation):
  - The gate evaluates key validity at the cert's **storage anchor `S`** — the introducing
    commit of the envelope-bearing `SIGNATURE` event, resolved by the gate from its OWN
    merged-log walk with the same fail-closed resolver already used for the close anchor
    (unresolvable/compacted anchor → fail closed: the key must then be currently valid). `S` is
    attacker-uninfluenceable in the backward direction: the shared tickets branch is
    append-only (the ADR 0046 L1 bounding control), so a cert can be stored late, never early.
  - Era boundaries in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` become **tickets-branch log
    positions**, operator-stamped at add/revoke time (`added_at_log_position` /
    `revoked_at_log_position`; a `rebar trusted-env` helper stamps the current tickets tip) —
    same DAG as `S`, so the comparison is plain commit ancestry, no cross-DAG inference.
  - **Revocation semantics:** a revoked key with NO grandfather pin fails ALL its certs — the
    compromise kill-switch (re-certify what matters under the new key); a revoked key WITH a
    pin keeps certs stored at/before the pin valid — routine rotation, history intact.
  - The bound `merged_log_commit` (main DAG) is demoted to its code-state meaning only — "the
    gate ran against this main commit", still constrained to ancestor-of-gated-HEAD and the
    enforce-since floors — and carries NO key-validity semantics.
  - Produce side needs no change: the client persists the envelope as a `SIGNATURE` event, and
    its introducing position IS the anchor. Residuals stay as documented: append-only reliance
    (L1), no transparency witness (L2, idea 4e1d), single-env/no-threshold (L4, idea 5100).
  Story 4214 carries this remediation together with finding #4.
- **Revised decomposition (supersedes items 4–6 of the list above).**
  1. **Producer signing seam (expand phase)** — new story: local Ed25519 env keys; plan-review +
     close-gate producers mint op-certs; read path certifies envelopes and still accepts legacy
     HMAC records (write-new / read-both); MCP `sign_manifest` rides the same seam.
  2. **ee0b (narrowed)** — the trusted gate service itself: FastAPI async job handler,
     authoritative-state fetch, per-kind dispatch + verdict mapping, Secrets-Manager key
     materialization, container image, and the client command that requests a remote cert and
     persists the returned envelope.
  3. **AWS IaC + deploy** — new story: extend `infra/terraform/` (API Gateway HTTP API + IAM
     authorizer + the SSM SecureString key parameter + instance-profile grant) and
     `infra/compose/` (the opcert service + guarded nginx route); real deploy via the
     authenticated AWS CLI; live SigV4-authenticated integrity test; zero fixed-monthly-cost
     increase (a design-time constraint, not an AC).
  4. **8f1d (contract phase)** — remove the HMAC scheme for op-cert kinds once the producer seam
     ships; migration doc + re-issue procedure (re-run the gates; validity is computed on read).
  5. **6a89** — docs/ADR of the settled model.
finding: AWS KMS does not support Ed25519 asymmetric keys — the KMS key-spec catalogue covers ECC_NIST_P256/P384, ECC_SECG_P256K1, RSA variants, and SM2, but not Ed25519. The sshsig signing path (ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <keypath>) additionally requires the raw private-key bytes as a file, which is incompatible with a KMS HSM-backed non-exportable key. The plan's AC5 and Scope section say the AWS MCP-server infra 'stores the signing key in a secrets manager / KMS,' implying KMS-grade HSM-bound key protection for an Ed25519 key — a capability AWS KMS does not offer. The existing infra pattern uses SSM SecureString (confirmed: /rebar/prod/mcp-hmac-signing-key in ssm.tf), which does permit retrieval to disk but is explicitly not HSM-bound.
location: Acceptance Criteria §5 ('stores the signing key in a secrets manager / KMS'); Scope ('IaC for the trusted MCP-server deployment … KMS/secrets')
suggested_fix: Replace 'KMS/secrets' in AC5 and Scope with 'SSM SecureString (AWS-managed KMS key encryption at rest)' to match the existing infra pattern and the sshsig constraint — and add a note that HSM-bound non-exportable key storage is out of scope for this epic (consistent with the existing PoC-grade infrastructure posture noted in gerrit-aws-setup.md).

title: Additive, kind-keyed attestations (plan-review + completion + future coexist)
plan:
## Context
A work ticket carries a single attestation slot (`state["signature"]`), written
last-writer-wins by `process_signature` (`src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py`). The
plan-review attestation (claim time) and the completion verdict (close time) share that
slot, so closing destroys the plan-review attestation (bug grumpy-site-beard /
e6a0-ba9b-ae92-43d7). The two are independent proofs and must coexist; we also expect
further attestation kinds and per-ticket CI-gate consumption later.

## What
Add `state["attestations"]`, a map keyed by attestation kind (per-key LWW, mirroring
`process_workflow_run`): additive across kinds, latest within a kind. Kind is derived from
the already-signed `manifest[0]` (`plan-review` / `completion-verifier`), so no
`PAYLOAD_VERSION` bump and existing signatures still verify. Reuse the `SIGNATURE` event
(add an unsigned `data["kind"]` ONLY as a routing hint). Attestations are immutable signed
records; validity for a gate is computed on read via one
`compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind)` dispatcher.

## Kind identity + routing
- The SIGNED `manifest[0]` is AUTHORITATIVE for kind. Parse: the substring before the first
  ":" (e.g. "plan-review: PASS" -> "plan-review").
- `data["kind"]` is only a hint; `process_signature` uses it ONLY if it equals the
  manifest-derived kind, else ignores it (never trust the unsigned envelope over the signed
  manifest). A SIGNATURE event whose manifest is empty/unkindable (e.g. a legacy retired
  blank event) is ignored for routing (cannot key it) — its staleness is now handled by
  validity-on-read, not by clobbering.

## Compiled-state shape + the legacy mirror
- `process_signature` writes BOTH `state["attestations"][kind] = record` AND keeps
  `state["signature"]` as a LIVE mirror of the MOST-RECENT attestation (last SIGNATURE
