  event wins the mirror), in live compiled state — not only in SNAPSHOT output.
- Why the live mirror: four existing consumers read `state.get("signature")` directly
  (`signing.verify_signature`, the close-gate path, `fsck`/validate, and the
  soon-removed `retire_attested_pin`); the live mirror means they keep working unchanged
  while `attestations` is wired up, AND it makes the SNAPSHOT/rollback mirror automatic
  (compiled_state already carries `signature`). The record shape is unchanged
  (manifest, algorithm, signature, key_id, head_sha, signed_at, author).
- 114f owns the canonical per-record shape; 2c2d/cc1b/ed26 reference it.

## Migration (expand only; contract deferred) + rollback
- Events keep the UNCHANGED `SIGNATURE` shape (plus the optional hint `data["kind"]`),
  so old code replays them fine.
- Read fold-in: an OLD SNAPSHOT whose `compiled_state` carries only `signature` (no
  `attestations`) is folded into `attestations` on restore (kind from its manifest).
  Raw post-snapshot SIGNATURE events replay normally into the map.
- New SNAPSHOTs carry BOTH `attestations` and the `signature` mirror automatically (the
  reducer maintains the mirror), so a rolled-back / not-yet-upgraded clone still reads one
  attestation. ROLLBACK is therefore safe at any time.
- The "contract" phase (dropping the mirror) is OUT OF SCOPE here; tracked by follow-up
  352b-5097-9971-4dc1, gated on an all-clones-upgraded readiness check.

## Verification surface
- `verify_signature(ticket_id, kind="plan-review")` ALWAYS returns a SINGLE verdict dict;
  `verify_signature_result.schema.json` is UNCHANGED. `kind` defaults to `"plan-review"`,
  so every existing library/MCP/CLI caller that omits a kind keeps single-verdict
  back-compat. Internally it reads `state["attestations"][kind]`, falling back to the
  legacy `state["signature"]` when the map is absent.
- The verify-ALL map is a SEPARATE entry-point `verify_attestations(ticket_id) ->
  {kind: verdict}` (NOT a new return shape of `verify_signature`).
- CLI: `verify-signature <t> --kind K` = single-gate exit code (0 iff certified);
  `verify-signature <t>` (no `--kind`) = human-readable listing via `verify_attestations`
  (exit 0 iff all certified). MCP `verify_signature` stays single (kind defaults to
  plan-review); the full per-kind set is surfaced via `show`/ticket-state (ed26) — no MCP
  schema break.
- Callers migrated to pass an explicit kind: `claim_gate_check`, `drift_refresh_candidate`,
  `refresh_attestation` (attest.py) -> `plan-review`; `_child_closure_findings`
  (completion.py) -> `completion-verifier`.

## Validity model (computed on read; records never mutated)
- `compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind)` centralizes per-kind rules; the
  MATERIAL-CHANGE field set reuses the EXISTING plan-review material fingerprint
  (`orchestrator.material_fingerprint`: description/AC/file_impact/children) as the single
  source — no new separately-owned constant. "Computed on read" means NOT stored/mutated;
  it MAY do I/O (plan-review still performs its filesystem code-drift re-hash).
- DATA SOURCE for "not reopened since": the reducer records `state["last_reopened_at"]` =
  the timestamp of the most recent `closed -> open` transition (detected in `process_status`
  from the pre-update status == "closed" and target == "open"); null if never reopened.
  `compute_validity` checks `signed_at >= last_reopened_at` (or null) purely from compiled
  state.
- completion-verifier valid iff: certified AND status=="closed" AND not-reopened-since AND
  material-fingerprint-unchanged-since `signed_at` (the completion signer records the
  material fingerprint in its manifest, symmetric with plan-review).
- plan-review valid iff: existing freshness (material + filesystem code drift) AND
  not-reopened-since.
- `retire_attested_pin` (the blank-`SIGNATURE`-on-reopen mutation, signing.py) is REMOVED
  with its reopen call (transition.py); reopen invalidation is validity-on-read for both
  kinds. No lifecycle transition mutates any attestation record.

## Presentation (no secret leak)
- `public_state()` (`_present.py`) gets a DEDICATED `attestations` branch (the flat
  `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` cannot express a wildcard kind level): if `out["attestations"]` is a
  dict, replace with `{kind: {k:v for k,v in rec.items() if k != "signature"} for kind, rec
  in out["attestations"].items() if isinstance(rec, dict)}`. The legacy single-`signature`
  one-level hex strip is retained. This is the canonical strip path for all present/future
  kinds.

## Success Criteria
- A ticket simultaneously holds plan-review AND completion attestations; signing one never
  clobbers the other (in `attestations`); re-running a kind replaces only that kind.
- Compaction/SNAPSHOT round-trips all kinds; legacy single-signature tickets and old-clone
  snapshots still read/verify; a new snapshot is readable by old code via the mirror.
- A reopened ticket stops reading as completion-valid AND plan-review-valid (via
  validity-on-read) with no record mutation; re-closing/re-reviewing re-signs the kind.
- No HMAC hex appears in `show`/MCP output for any attestation kind.

## Validation
- Manual dogfood smoke-test: create a ticket, sign a plan-review PASS, sign a
  completion-verifier PASS, `rebar show` shows BOTH kinds (neither overwriting the other,
  no hex); reopen and confirm both read not-valid; `rebar verify-signature --kind K` per
  kind.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `state["attestations"]` (kind-keyed, per-key LWW) added; `process_signature` writes
      `attestations[kind]` AND maintains the live `state["signature"]` most-recent mirror;
      routing uses authoritative `manifest[0]` (data["kind"] only as a validated hint).
- [ ] Signed canonical payload unchanged; every pre-existing signature still certifies.
- [ ] Old-snapshot read fold-in (`signature` -> `attestations`); new SNAPSHOTs carry both
      `attestations` and the mirror; contract deferred to 352b.
- [ ] Reducer records `state["last_reopened_at"]` (most recent closed->open; null if never).
- [ ] `compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind)` centralizes per-kind validity,
      reusing the existing material fingerprint; completion = certified AND closed AND
      not-reopened-since AND material-unchanged-since; plan-review = existing freshness AND
      not-reopened-since.
- [ ] `retire_attested_pin` removed; no transition mutates any attestation record.
- [ ] `verify_signature(ticket_id, kind="plan-review")` returns a single verdict (schema
      unchanged, back-compat default); `verify_attestations(ticket_id)` returns
      `{kind: verdict}`; CLI no-`--kind` lists all (exit 0 iff all certified); claim gate
      reads `plan-review`, close + child-closure read `completion-verifier`.
- [ ] `public_state()` dedicated `attestations` branch strips the `signature` subkey from
      every per-kind record (no hex in show/MCP for any kind); legacy strip retained.
- [ ] `show`/MCP render the `attestations` map (omitted when empty).
- [ ] Tests: coexistence; within-kind replace; compaction round-trip; legacy + new-snapshot
      cross-read; reopen invalidates completion + plan-review validity without mutation;
      material-change invalidates completion; concurrent different-kind signs both survive;
      no HMAC hex in public output.
## compute_validity contract (pinned)
`compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None) -> {"valid": bool,
"reason": str}`. It ASSUMES the attestation record is already HMAC-certified (the caller
runs `verify_signature` first); compute_validity adds ONLY the lifecycle/freshness layer on
top (per-kind: completion = closed AND not-reopened-since AND material-unchanged-since;
plan-review = material + filesystem code-drift AND not-reopened-since). `reason` is a
human-readable explanation when `valid` is False (empty/"certified ..." when True).
Consumers: `claim_gate_check` (plan-review), the close gate + `_child_closure_findings`
(completion-verifier) call it and branch on `.valid`.
## Acceptance Criteria (compute_validity)
- [ ] `compute_validity(...)` returns `{"valid": bool, "reason": str}`; callers HMAC-verify first then branch on `.valid`.
finding: The `public_state()` mechanism in `_present.py` only strips one level of nesting (e.g. `signature.signature`), but the plan's `attestations.<kind>.signature` HMAC hex lives TWO levels deep. The plan's Design clarifications section says it 'must strip at the TWO-level path' but specifies no mechanism — the existing `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` dict-of-frozensets pattern cannot express a wildcard-kind second level, so a naive port of the old entry will either miss all kinds or require a dedicated code path. If unaddressed, the raw HMAC hex leaks into `show`/MCP output for every attestation kind.
location: Design clarifications section — '_present.py LEAK'; src/rebar/reducer/_present.py:30-52
suggested_fix: Add a dedicated branch in `public_state()`: after the generic `_INTERNAL_SUBKEYS` loop, if `'attestations'` is in `out` and is a dict, replace it with `{ kind: {k: v for k, v in rec.items() if k != 'signature'} for kind, rec in out['attestations'].items() if isinstance(rec, dict) }`. Document this as the canonical strip path for all present and future attestation kinds.

title: Stage-1 BM25F candidate generation over digests + graph-exclusion filter
plan:
# Stage-1 BM25F candidate generation over digests + graph-exclusion filter

## Why
Stage 1 narrows a few hundred tickets to ~20 candidates cheaply and deterministically, no LLM/embeddings, and must NOT surface a ticket's own already-related work (parents/children/siblings/linked) — the top source of "related-but-distinct" false flags. DERISKED (epic experiment comment): digest-BM25F recall@20 = 1.0 on 8 paraphrase pairs, STABLE 60→200 corpus; single-query latency 0.69 ms at 800 digests (~14× under budget) — so brute-force is correct and no ANN/vector-DB is needed.

## What
- Brute-force BM25F "More Like This" over `ticket_digest` records (S2). Bespoke ~40 LOC: `rank-bm25` provides only UNWEIGHTED BM25, but we need per-field weighting; at n<1000 bespoke is trivial and avoids a dependency (the recall+latency spikes validate this choice). Field weights are a module-level constant `_FIELD_WEIGHTS` in `retrieve.py` (`problem_keywords` 3.0, `component_or_area` 3.0, `key_entities` 1.0, `propositions` 1.0) — a stable algorithmic choice, deliberately NOT a config knob (a dict-typed config value has no `from_env` resolution helper, and BM25F weights do not need per-invocation tuning; if they ever do, that is a separate `_llm_json` helper story). Tokenize lowercased on non-alphanumeric, drop stopwords + corpus-boilerplate via `overlap_max_doc_freq`. Returns a typed `OverlapCandidate` `[{ticket_id, score, matched_terms}]` (the interface S7 consumes — stated so S7 isn't blind to it). Query side = the query ticket's freshly-extracted digest, passed by the caller.
- **Graph exclusion** BEFORE scoring: new helper `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker) -> set[str]` that loads states ONCE via `_load_all_states(tracker)` (the same helper `descendants.py` uses) and derives all four relation sets from that one dict: descendants (BFS on `parent_id`, or by calling `descendants.list_descendants(ticket_id, tracker)` and flattening its type-bucketed id lists — NOT by sharing its internal snapshot, which it does not expose), ancestors (walk `parent_id` up), siblings (same `parent_id`; a root ticket with no parent has NO siblings — handled explicitly), and linked tickets in BOTH directions (scan all tickets' `deps` for any edge whose source OR target is `ticket_id` — an incoming `blocks`/`depends_on` from another ticket is not in this ticket's own `deps`, so outgoing-only would miss it). It does NOT refactor `next_batch.py`. At n<1000 one state load is sub-ms (latency spike).
- Cold/stale: corpus tickets whose digest is absent or stale (S2 freshness: content-hash/model/version mismatch — not an age threshold) are SKIPPED; the skip-count is logged so a cold run is distinguishable from a healthy one.
- Overlap floor: a `min_should_match` fraction (default 0.15 of query terms) below which a candidate is not returned; `retrieve()` returns `[]` when nothing clears the floor.
- Errors: any graph-read/scoring exception is caught, logged, returns `[]` (never blocks review_plan).

## Config (LLMConfig, not VerifyConfig) — with explicit `from_env` wiring
Three SCALAR tunables on `LLMConfig` (`src/rebar/llm/config.py`, `[tool.rebar.llm]`,
`REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*`); field weights are a constant (see What), NOT config:
- `overlap_k: int = 20`
- `overlap_max_doc_freq: float = 0.5` (ignore terms appearing in >50% of digests)
- `overlap_min_should_match: float = 0.15`

**`LLMConfig.from_env()` MUST be edited** (it explicitly constructs `LLMConfig(...)` with
every field read via a helper — a new dataclass field not read there stays at its default and
is silently un-tunable). Add module constants `DEFAULT_OVERLAP_K = 20`,
`DEFAULT_OVERLAP_MAX_DOC_FREQ = 0.5`, `DEFAULT_OVERLAP_MIN_SHOULD_MATCH = 0.15`; add the three
dataclass fields; and in `from_env`'s `return cls(...)` add:
- `overlap_k=_llm_int(table, cli, "REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_K", "overlap_k", DEFAULT_OVERLAP_K)`
- `overlap_max_doc_freq=_llm_float(table, cli, "REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MAX_DOC_FREQ", "overlap_max_doc_freq", DEFAULT_OVERLAP_MAX_DOC_FREQ)`
- `overlap_min_should_match=_llm_float(table, cli, "REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MIN_SHOULD_MATCH", "overlap_min_should_match", DEFAULT_OVERLAP_MIN_SHOULD_MATCH)`

`_llm_int` exists (`config.py:388`); `_llm_float` does NOT — add it in `config.py` mirroring
`_llm_int` (env > `rebar -c llm.*` > `[tool.rebar.llm]` file > default, parsing via `float()`
with a clean fallback on ValueError). This is the only non-scalar-free helper needed (no dict
keys remain). `_config_schema.py` reserves the `llm.*` layer, so these are NOT on `VerifyConfig`.

## Dependencies
S1 (`ticket_digest` schema/fields), S2 (digest store + freshness). Digest fields are DEFINED in S1.

## Scope
- New `src/rebar/llm/overlap/retrieve.py` (BM25F + `OverlapCandidate` + `_FIELD_WEIGHTS` constant) and `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py` (`related_ticket_ids`).
- `src/rebar/llm/config.py`: add `_llm_float` helper, the three `DEFAULT_OVERLAP_*` constants, the three `LLMConfig` fields, and the three `from_env()` reads (enumerated above).
- OUT: judge (S6), wiring (S7). Interface for S7: `retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude:set[str]) -> list[OverlapCandidate]`.

## Testing
- `tests/unit/test_overlap_retrieve.py`: `test_topk` (order); `test_perf` (<10 ms at 300 digests); `test_graph_exclusion` (parents/children/siblings/linked dropped; root-ticket-no-siblings edge); `test_cold_skip` (absent/stale skipped + count logged); `test_boilerplate` (shared boilerplate ≠ match); `test_floor_empty` (nothing clears min_should_match → `[]`); `test_error_empty` (graph error → `[]`, no raise); `test_config` — sets `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_K`, `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MAX_DOC_FREQ`, `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MIN_SHOULD_MATCH` in the env, calls `LLMConfig.from_env()`, and asserts the three fields resolve to the env values (proving `from_env` wiring, not just dataclass defaults); `test_field_weights_constant` (retrieve uses the `_FIELD_WEIGHTS` constant).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Returns top-K (default 20) `OverlapCandidate`s by field-weighted BM25F, <10 ms at ~300 digests; `pytest ...::{test_topk,test_perf}`.
- [ ] Candidates EXCLUDE the query's parents/children/siblings/linked via `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker)` (reusing `descendants.list_descendants`); root-with-no-parent has no siblings; `::test_graph_exclusion`.
- [ ] Absent/stale digests skipped, skip-count logged; `::test_cold_skip`. Boilerplate doesn't drive matches; `::test_boilerplate`.
- [ ] `min_should_match` floor returns `[]` when unmet; a graph/scoring error returns `[]` (never blocks); `::{test_floor_empty,test_error_empty}`.
- [ ] `overlap_k`, `overlap_max_doc_freq`, `overlap_min_should_match` are read from the env by `LLMConfig.from_env()` (explicit wiring, `_llm_float` added for the floats); field weights are the `_FIELD_WEIGHTS` constant, not config; `::test_config`, `::test_field_weights_constant`.
finding: list_descendants(root_id, tracker) — the function the plan names as the core reuse for graph exclusion — internally calls reduce_all_tickets(tracker), a full O(n) disk scan over the entire store. Invoking it inside the retrieval path directly contradicts the <10ms performance AC. Additionally, list_descendants covers only descendants; the query ticket's own deps (linked tickets) require a separate reduce_ticket or reduce_all_tickets call that the plan does not name. The plan presents this as a cheap, transparent reuse but the actual integration surface has both a performance incompatibility and a coverage gap.
location: What section — 'A NEW helper src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids … that REUSES the existing public src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py::list_descendants'
suggested_fix: Define related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, all_states: dict[str, dict]) -> set[str] — the caller (retrieve()) already loads the corpus states for scoring and can pass the same dict in. Inside: (1) walk parent_id up from all_states[ticket_id] for ancestors; (2) collect children via the existing children_of index; (3) collect siblings via same parent_id; (4) collect dep target_ids from all_states[ticket_id].get('deps', []). This eliminates the extra disk scan entirely and makes the perf AC achievable.

title: S5a Container + compose service + flock single-instance + external state volume
plan:
## Why
Package the auto-lander loop service and wire it into the existing compose stack on the Gerrit box, single-instance. Split out of S5 (`perpetual-ultrasonic-raccoon`) to keep each unit loadable whole.

## Files
- `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` — NEW: service image (installs the stdlib loop; `HEALTHCHECK` added by S5d).
- `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` — EDIT: add the `autolander` service (labeled `autoheal=true`, per S5d) + the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar (Docker socket mounted **read-write** — autoheal issues `docker restart`, a daemon WRITE, so `:ro` would silently fail; the socket-write privilege is the accepted, inherent cost of autoheal) + declare the named state volume `gerrit_autolander` as `external: true`.
- `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` — EDIT: add `autolander` to `SITE_SUBDIRS` (the `volume_for_subdir()` helper prefixes `gerrit_` + maps `-`→`_`, so the subdir `autolander` yields the docker volume `gerrit_autolander` — the exact name used in `docker-compose.yml`). Incident-2731 drift gate: `config-check.sh` check 5 hard-fails CI when an `external: true` volume is missing from `SITE_SUBDIRS`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` (new) + the `autolander` compose service added to the existing `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`.
- [ ] **Single-instance guard:** the container acquires a POSIX advisory **file lock** (`flock` on the concrete lockfile **`autolander.lock`** at the root of its mounted state volume) on start; a second copy fails to acquire it and exits non-zero. This `flock` is the **sole** single-instance mechanism — do NOT rely on `deploy.replicas: 1` (a Swarm-only key silently ignored by plain `docker compose`).
- [ ] **State volume:** the named volume **`gerrit_autolander`** is declared `external: true` and its subdir `autolander` added to `SITE_SUBDIRS` in `compose-up.sh`; it holds `autolander.lock`, the heartbeat file (S5d), the emergency-stop sentinel (S5d), and `recovery.json` (S5d), so all survive container recreation.
- [ ] **Autoheal sidecar wired:** the `willfarrell/autoheal` service is present in `docker-compose.yml` with the Docker socket mounted **read-write** (required for its `docker restart` action), and the `autolander` service carries the `autoheal=true` label so the sidecar manages it (the restart behaviour itself is exercised by S5d's hang test).
- [ ] **Tests:** `docker compose config` validates (autolander + autoheal services, `gerrit_autolander` external volume, `autoheal=true` label, read-write socket mount present); **flock proof (concrete):** `docker compose up -d autolander` then a second `docker run` on the same image + volume asserts exit code ≠ 0 within 10 s (the second instance fails the `flock`); `config-check.sh` check 5 passes (`autolander` in `SITE_SUBDIRS`).
finding: The plan specifies the `autoheal=true` label as the signal for `willfarrell/autoheal` to manage the autolander container, but does not pin the autoheal image to a specific tag. The `willfarrell/autoheal` image has had version-specific label behavior (e.g., some builds require `autoheal.enable=true` rather than `autoheal=true`, controlled by the AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL env var). Without a pinned tag, the label-key contract between the autolander service's label and the sidecar's filter is unverified — a tag mismatch could cause the sidecar to silently not restart the autolander when it becomes unhealthy.
location: Files section — `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml` edit; AC item 4 ('the `autolander` service carries the `autoheal=true` label so the sidecar manages it')
suggested_fix: In the autoheal service definition in docker-compose.yml, use a pinned tag (e.g., `image: willfarrell/autoheal:v0.9.0`) and add `environment: [AUTOHEAL_CONTAINER_LABEL=autoheal=true]` to the autoheal service to make the label-key contract explicit and tag-independent.

title: op-cert: trusted gate service — authoritative-state fetch, per-kind dispatch, async job API (+ client command)
plan:
# op-cert: trusted gate service — authoritative-state fetch, per-kind dispatch, async job API (+ client command)

## Why
For high assurance, a developer routes a gate run to a trusted environment that fetches
authoritative state ITSELF (ticket state from the tickets branch; code at the review remote's
`main` tip), runs the gate, and returns an Ed25519-signed `rebar.opcert.v1` cert — so
client-supplied inputs cannot forge a pass (the SLSA non-falsifiable-provenance principle). This
story builds the **service and its client half only**; AWS IaC + the real deploy are the sibling
"AWS IaC + deploy" story, and the signing behavior itself ships in the "producer signing seam"
story this depends on. All design forks are settled in the epic's "Producer-signing &
compute-runtime design resolution" section — this story implements, it does not decide.

**Hard dependencies (not yet on `main`):** the op-cert substrate consumed here
(`signing.sign_opcert_manifest`, `src/rebar/attest/opcert.py`, `trusted_env.py`, `sshsig.sign` —
Gerrit changes 649/650/660, in review), the merge-gate consumer `rebar verify-opcert` (story 4214,
Gerrit change 664, in review — referenced by the client ACs below), and the producer signing seam
story must land first;
mentions of them below are forward references to that stack, enforced by this ticket's
`depends_on` links. `src/rebar/review_bot/app.py` (the pattern mirrored) IS on `main` today.

## What
**Service** (`src/rebar/opcert_service/`, library-mode: rebar imported behind a thin FastAPI app —
the `review_bot/app.py` software pattern; NOT the stdio `rebar-mcp`):
- **Async job API** (gate runs take 30s–minutes; no sync request can hold): `POST /opcert/jobs`
  validates, enqueues on an in-process `asyncio.Queue`, returns **202 `{job_id}`**;
  `GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id}` returns the job record. Single background worker drains serially
  under a bounded per-run timeout: default **900 s** (gate runs take 30s–minutes; the ceiling is
  generous), configurable via `REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS`. Job state is in-memory (a restart loses queued jobs; clients
  re-submit — cert minting is idempotent).
- **Request schema**: `{"ticket_id": str, "kind": "completion-verifier"|"plan-review"}` — ONLY
  identifiers. Any other client field (a `material_fingerprint`, a `commit`, an `env_id`) is
  IGNORED; the server derives every authoritative value itself.
- **Authoritative fetch**: the worker fetches the tickets branch (ticket state) and the review
  remote's `main` tip (code) with its own read-only credentials — sources named by env config:
  `REBAR_OPCERT_REVIEW_REMOTE_URL` (code) and `REBAR_OPCERT_TICKETS_REMOTE_URL` (tickets
  branch), credentials from the service env. It sets `merged_log_commit = <fetched main HEAD
  sha>` and computes the material fingerprint itself. Each job runs in an **ephemeral
  workspace clone** discarded after the job.
- **Per-kind dispatch + native verdicts (no unified enum)**: `plan-review` →
  `rebar.llm.review_plan(...)` (verdict `PASS|BLOCK|INDETERMINATE`); `completion-verifier` →
  `rebar.llm.verify_completion(...)` (verdict `PASS|FAIL`). Error handling is per-kind and
  ASYMMETRIC, matching the live code: `verify_completion` RAISES
  `LLMError`/`LLMUnavailableError` → the job ends `status: "error"` with the enum mapping
  below; `review_plan` does NOT raise on an LLM outage — it degrades IN-BAND to
  `verdict: "INDETERMINATE"` (unsigned), so the job COMPLETES with that verdict and
  `envelope: null`; only an unexpected exception maps a plan-review job to
  `status: "error", class: "internal"`. Never a fabricated verdict. Response/job schema:
  `{status: "queued"|"running"|"completed"|"error", kind, verdict: <native enum>|null,
  envelope: <encoded DSSE>|null, material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit, error: {class, message}|null}`
  — `envelope` present ONLY on a `PASS` verdict. `error.class` is a CLOSED enum mapped from the
  raised exception type: `"llm_unavailable"` (`LLMUnavailableError`), `"llm_error"` (other
  `LLMError`), `"timeout"` (per-run timeout exceeded), `"internal"` (anything else).
- **Signing = the producer seam, once; the service only PROVISIONS the key.** The gate
  producers mint the op-cert via the signing seam (producer-seam story); the handler itself
  never signs bespokely and never passes `sign=False`. Per-kind producer step: `review_plan`
  signs internally (`sign=True` default). `verify_completion` has NO `sign` parameter — the
  completion producer step lives in the close gate today (`transition_close.py`'s
  `_verdict_manifest` + seam call) — so this story EXTRACTS that pair into a reusable
  `sign_completion_verdict(result, ticket_id, repo_root)` used by BOTH `transition_close.py`
  (behavior unchanged) and this worker on a PASS verdict: the worker invokes the same producer
  function, not a bespoke signer. The hand-off protocol: this story
  adds one override to the seam's key resolution — `REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH` (an explicit key
  file path) takes precedence over the `<tracker>/.opcert-key` genesis. Before dispatching a
  job, the worker fetches the SSM SecureString (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`),
  **materializes it to a 0600 `mkstemp` temp file** (SSHSIG signing shells
  `ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <path>` and needs a file path), sets `REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH` to that
  path and `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` as the principal for the gate run (the principal is the
  environment's logical id string — it MUST exactly equal the environment's `env_id` entry in
  the consuming project's `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, since it becomes the DSSE keyid
  the merge gate pins on; a non-empty printable token, e.g. `nava-opcert-prod-1`), and **deletes the file in a
  `finally`** — never persisted, and the seam signs exactly once with the provisioned key. The
  key MUST be passphrase-free (`ssh-keygen -Y sign` is non-interactive). The legacy HMAC
  environment key is never provisioned in the server.
- **Stateless / store-read-only — reconciled with the seam's `sign=True`.** Running a gate with
  signing on appends a `SIGNATURE` event to the store it runs against; on the server that store
  is the job's ephemeral workspace clone, with `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` and NO tickets-branch
  write credential — so the event lands only in the discarded workspace, never on the shared
  branch. The worker reads back the freshly-appended record by the NAMED mechanism —
  `show_ticket(ticket_id)["attestations"][kind]` in the ephemeral workspace (the reduced record
  of the just-written `SIGNATURE` event; `opcert.opcert_from_record` decodes its `envelope` +
  bound fields) — and returns the envelope in the response; the CLIENT persists it to the shared tickets branch. Nothing is
  ever pushed by the server (asserted by a test on the workspace remote config). Endpoint authn
  (SigV4) is terminated at API Gateway (the IaC story); the app additionally requires a shared
  secret as defense in depth — same posture as `review_bot`'s check (which uses a `?token=`
  query parameter, `review_bot/app.py`); the opcert app checks an `X-Opcert-Guard` **header**
  instead (value from the `REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD` env var, materialized from SSM by the deploy
  story), because API Gateway injects static request headers on the integration.
- **Container image**: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (build context = repo root, per the
  review_bot Dockerfile pattern) installing **OpenSSH ≥ 8.9** and running
  `uvicorn rebar.opcert_service.app:app`.

**Client** (`src/rebar/_commands/remote_cert.py`, CLI `rebar remote-cert <ticket-id> <kind>`).
Wiring is enumerated (the drift gates that bit change 664 before): the `argv[0] ==` intercept
ladder in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` gains the `remote-cert` arm; a help file is added under
`src/rebar/_cli/help/`; `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py`'s curated `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` dict is
updated in the same change (its set-equality test fails otherwise); and every new
`REBAR_OPCERT_*` env var is regenerated into `docs/env-vars.md` (`scripts/gen_env_registry.py`
scans `src/rebar`; the CI env-var drift gate fails otherwise).
- Reads `verify.opcert_remote_url` (new `VerifyConfig` key, `str | None = None`; unset → clear
  error; nothing remote is ever required for local operation). SigV4-signs the request with
  ambient AWS credentials, submits the job, polls to a terminal status, and on `PASS` **persists
  the returned envelope + bound fields as the `SIGNATURE` event** (the same event shape
  `sign_opcert_manifest` appends; auto-pushed with the tickets branch). Safe because the envelope
  is self-authenticating — tampering with the bound values breaks the signature the merge gate
  (`rebar verify-opcert`) verifies. Non-PASS / error → non-zero exit with the job's error class.

## Scope
- In: the FastAPI service, dispatch/verdict mapping, authoritative fetch, SSM-parameter key
  materialization + the `REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH` seam override, Dockerfile, the `remote-cert` client command, `verify.opcert_remote_url`.
- Out: Terraform/API Gateway/SSM resources + the real deploy + live round-trip
  (the "AWS IaC + deploy" story); the producer signing seam itself (dependency); HMAC removal
  (8f1d).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `POST /opcert/jobs` ACKs 202 `{job_id}` and `GET /opcert/jobs/{id}` reaches a terminal
      status via the background worker; a test drives the round-trip against a fake authoritative
      source with the request/response schemas above.
- [ ] Integrity test (the load-bearing one): a request carrying a **doctored
      `material_fingerprint`** and a doctored `commit` is accepted, the extra fields are ignored,
      and the signed op-cert's bound `material_fingerprint`/`merged_log_commit` equal the values
      the server derived from its own fetch — a client cannot influence any bound value.
- [ ] Per-kind verdict mapping: tests assert `plan-review` jobs surface
      `PASS|BLOCK|INDETERMINATE` natively, `completion-verifier` jobs surface `PASS|FAIL`, a
      raised `LLMError` yields `status="error"` with `verdict=null`, and `envelope` is non-null
      ONLY on `PASS` (BLOCK/FAIL/INDETERMINATE/error all return `envelope: null`).
- [ ] Key materialization: a test asserts the SSM SecureString value is written to a 0600
      `mkstemp` file for the `ssh-keygen -Y sign` invocation and that NO key file remains after
      signing (including on a raised exception — the `finally` path).
- [ ] The server appends no tickets-branch event: a test asserts the store is unchanged after a
      completed job; `rebar remote-cert` persists the returned envelope as a `SIGNATURE` event
      that `verify_signature`/`rebar verify-opcert` then certify, and a tampered envelope is
      rejected (`mismatch`).
- [ ] Offline default preserved: a test asserts every local op-cert sign/verify path works with
      `verify.opcert_remote_url` unset and no network — the remote path is opt-in and its absence
      never blocks a local operation.
- [ ] `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` installs OpenSSH ≥ 8.9 and starts the service; a test
      asserts the Dockerfile declares both.
- [ ] Origin guard: tests assert a request missing the `X-Opcert-Guard` header, or carrying a
      value that does not match `REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD`, is rejected (403) before any work is
      enqueued; a matching header is accepted.
- [ ] Server never pushes: a test asserts the job workspace runs with `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` and
      no push remote/credential, and that the shared store is byte-identical after a completed
      PASS job (the SIGNATURE event exists only in the discarded workspace).
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` green.
finding: The plan asserts `signing.sign_opcert_manifest` is a hard dependency 'not yet on main' (Gerrit changes 649/650/660), yet `resolve_symbol` reports it EXISTS in the installed environment while a grep of `src/rebar/signing.py` (the named file) finds no such function. The plan's scope description also says the seam adds 'REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH takes precedence over the `<tracker>/.opcert-key` genesis' — but neither `opcert-key` genesis logic nor `REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH` appears anywhere in the current repo. The discrepancy between the installed env and the file-level source means implementers cannot know which interface contract (function signature, key-resolution order) to implement against without consulting the out-of-repo Gerrit changes.
location: Why section — Hard dependencies paragraph; What section — 'Signing = the producer seam' paragraph

title: WS3: Enumeration-overlay pattern (code-grounded router -> dedicated overlay)
plan:
## Why
Two defect classes concern the INVISIBLE AFFECTED SET (existing sites a plan silently breaks). A lexical trigger over-fires on descriptive prose; LLM routing is more reliable, and each overlay runs as its own criterion call (its own context window) -- preserving the intent behind the earlier "router->overlay" framing.

## What (REDESIGNED per review to be codebase-native)
The earlier "G1G2 emits an OVERLAY TRIGGER that orchestrator.py spawns" framing is dropped: verified that G1G2 produces findings (list[dict]), not a typed signal; route_criteria (orchestrator.py:332) routes overlays by criterion metadata, and the parallel Pass-1 fan-out has no G1G2->overlay ordering hook. Codebase-native path instead:
- Register TWO new LLM-routed overlay criteria (overlay_routing: llm, matching T10/T11) handled by the EXISTING route_criteria path -- zero new inter-pass signal:
  - G-5 prohibition-enumeration overlay: triggers on a plan introducing a NEW prohibition (enforce/block/require-before/must-pass); enumerates the outlawed behavior's existing call sites and classifies each MIGRATED / EXEMPTED / UNCOVERED (define this enum in the prompt).
  - G-10 CI-trigger/release-infra overlay: triggers on a new git ref / event source / schedule; enumerates every workflow trigger filter as INCLUDED / EXCLUDED / NO_FILTER (define enum) + release-infra coverage. Abstain-with-coverage on unparseable CI (polyglot fail-open).
- Each overlay is LLM-routed (reliable, not lexical) and runs as its own criterion call (the "own context window" the intent wanted). Enumerate consumers of criteria_routing.json schema before adding.

## What (edit targets)
criteria_routing.json (two new overlay criteria, overlay_routing: llm); new plan_review_G5_prohibition.md + plan_review_G10_citrigger.md prompts; orchestrator.py route_criteria only if a new overlay_routing value is needed (prefer reusing T10/T11's existing llm overlay path).

## Provenance
Gap-report labels (G-*/FP-*/R-*/WS-N) are tags from ~/dso_rebar_plan_review_gap_report.md + session log brief-bride-rash (95f1-1b3b-a867-4020), NOT codebase symbols. Symbols introduced by a SIBLING story (e.g. committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim from WS1) are target-state, not pre-existing; the dependency is recorded.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Two overlay criteria registered (overlay_routing: llm) + their prompts. Proof: `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.registry validate-routing` exits 0.
- [ ] MIGRATED/EXEMPTED/UNCOVERED and INCLUDED/EXCLUDED/NO_FILTER enums DEFINED in their prompts.
- [ ] G-5 recall case: a require-tests-before-merge plan yields UNCOVERED `gh pr merge` call sites. Proof: author `test_g5_prohibition` in tests/unit/test_plan_review_evals.py; `pytest -k g5_prohibition` green.
- [ ] G-10 recall case: a new ref pattern yields an EXCLUDED workflow; plus a fail-open abstain case on unknown CI. Proof: `pytest -k g10_citrigger` green.
- [ ] False-positive case: a plan that merely DESCRIBES enforcement (no new prohibition) does not fire. Proof: false-fire case in the eval suite.
finding: The plan asserts that `plan_review_G1G2.md` 'detects the pattern and emits an OVERLAY TRIGGER', but reading the actual file reveals it is purely a consumer-enumeration rubric (Glob/Grep to verify file/symbol existence and enumerate callers) with no overlay-trigger emission logic of any kind. The existing overlay trigger system in the codebase is `registry.overlay_triggers()`, a deterministic function entirely separate from G1G2.
location: What section: 'plan_review_G1G2.md (code-grounded) detects the pattern and emits an OVERLAY TRIGGER'
suggested_fix: Add a structured output field (e.g. `overlay_triggers: list[str]`) to the G1G2 agentic response schema, and specify in the plan how the orchestrator reads this field from the G1G2 agent output to decide whether to spawn the enumeration overlay.

title: Persist ticket digest as a content-hash-keyed record on the ticket (shared cache)
plan:
# Persist ticket digest as a REVIEW_RESULT-style sidecar (shared cache)

## Why
The digest is a billable LLM artifact we never want to recompute (~$1.90 to enrich the whole 793-ticket store — see epic experiment comment). Storing it in the SHARED store (auto-pushed) makes one client's enrichment a cache-hit for everyone. rebar's exact precedent is `REVIEW_RESULT`: a reducer-IGNORED, preserved-and-ignored, per-ticket LLM-derived sidecar with a schema sentinel + a `prune` retention. We mirror it.

## What — payload schema (fully specified)
A `TICKET_DIGEST` sidecar event. Its `data` payload:
`{"schema": "ticket_digest_v1", "digest": {problem_keywords, component_or_area, key_entities, propositions}, "content_hash": "<hex>", "model": "<model-id>", "digest_hash_version": <int>}`.
The `schema` sentinel `"ticket_digest_v1"` guards reads (mirrors REVIEW_RESULT keying on `payload['schema'] == 'plan_review_result_v1'`) so a foreign/older payload is skipped, not mis-parsed. `content_hash`, `model`, `digest_hash_version` are the three freshness tags (named fields in the payload).

## Content hash — a LOCAL, self-consistent normalizer (no cross-layer import)
`content_hash = sha256(_normalize_text(state))` where `_normalize_text(state)` is a small
module-LOCAL function in `digest_sidecar.py` doing the canonical join (title + description +
comment bodies, `"\n".join`, empties skipped). It is deliberately LOCAL and NOT an import of
`rebar._engine_support.gates._ticket_text`: no `rebar.llm.*` module imports `_engine_support.gates`
(that would be a layering violation — the same reason S5/`ee3d` inlines its own join), and the
content hash is this story's OWN internal freshness key, so it need not be byte-identical to
any other module's normalizer. What matters is INTERNAL consistency: the SAME `_normalize_text`
is used both when writing (`emit`) and when checking freshness (read), so a hash computed at
write equals the hash recomputed at read for unchanged state. `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` (module
constant in the sidecar) is bumped only if the hashed field-set ever changes, invalidating all
cached digests deterministically. (No dependency on S5's inline join — S5 does not hash; only
this story computes/consumes the content hash.)

## Freshness (computed on read, fail-closed)
The digest is CURRENT iff ALL of: stored `content_hash` == `sha256(_normalize_text(current))`
(via the SAME local `_normalize_text` used at write), stored `model` == active model, stored
`digest_hash_version` == current constant. Any mismatch → stale. If current state can't be read
(partial read) → NOT-current (fail-closed; never falsely fresh).

## Scope (exact edit targets — every registry a new event type touches)
- `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py`: the `_cmd_show` freshness render (detailed below). (No `gates.py` edit — the content-hash normalizer is local to `digest_sidecar.py`, see Content hash.)
- `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`: add `"TICKET_DIGEST"` to the `EVENT_TYPES` write allow-list AND its enumerated string in the StoreError message (lines ~97-103).
- `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py`: add `"TICKET_DIGEST"` to `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (NOT `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES`) — preserved-and-ignored, never compacted into SNAPSHOT, no fsck WARN (mirrors REVIEW_RESULT).
- New `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` (the package) + `src/rebar/llm/overlap/digest_sidecar.py` with `emit(digest, ticket_id)` (appends + calls `prune`), `prune(ticket_id, keep=1)`, `latest_ticket_digest(ticket_id)` (guards on the `ticket_digest_v1` sentinel) — all modeled on `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`.
- `show` freshness render (CLI-show-ONLY — must NOT touch the library state shape):
  the parity invariant `test_show_list_search_share_one_shape`
  (`tests/interfaces/store/test_reducer_single_source.py:59`) asserts the **library** returns
  are identical — `set(rebar.show_ticket(id)) == set(list_tickets element) == set(search element)`
  and `show == lst == srch`. So `show_state`/`show_ticket` and `reducer/_present.py::public_state`
  (the SHARED key-stripping filter used by show/list/search/ready/MCP — NOT a show-only
  renderer) must be left untouched. The freshness flag ("present-fresh"|"present-stale"|"absent")
  is therefore added ONLY in the CLI show arm `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py::_cmd_show`
  (~line 144-171): after `state = show_state(...)`, `_cmd_show` computes freshness via
  `overlap.digest_sidecar.latest_ticket_digest(id)` + the freshness check and injects
  `digest_freshness` into the dict it prints (`json.dumps(state, ...)` and the `to_llm(state)`
  `--output llm` arm) — a CLI-presentation-only field that never enters the library
  `show_ticket`/`list`/`search` returns, so the parity invariant holds. No new field in
  `ticket_state.schema.json`; the digest stays a sidecar (like REVIEW_RESULT).
  `test_cmd_show_renders_freshness` asserts the CLI `show` stdout carries the flag;
  `test_library_shape_unchanged` asserts `show_ticket`/`list`/`search` still share one shape
  with a digest present (parity invariant held).
- Prune invocation: `emit` calls `prune(keep=1)` on every write — EXACTLY the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar pattern, nothing more. There is NO special archive/delete lazy-prune (that novel behavior is dropped: it has no `REVIEW_RESULT` prior art and `show_state` does not cleanly signal "gone" for archived tickets). An archived/deleted ticket's digest simply lingers as a harmless sidecar, bounded to ONE record per ticket by `keep=1`, and is never read into any hot path (like a stale REVIEW_RESULT). `latest_ticket_digest` does no existence check on the ticket.

## Dependencies
S1 (indecisive-callous-armyworm) — produces the `ticket_digest` payload. (The drain that decides to re-enrich lives in S4; this story owns only WRITE + READ + freshness of the sidecar, not the skip decision.)

## Testing
- `tests/unit/test_ticket_digest_store.py`: `test_roundtrip`; `test_freshness_all_triggers` (stale on content-hash OR model OR digest_hash_version change); `test_hash_write_read_parity` (the content_hash written by `emit` equals the hash recomputed by the freshness check for UNCHANGED state — proving both paths call the SAME local `_normalize_text`; and an edit to title/description/comments flips it stale — proving the normalizer actually covers those fields); `test_fail_closed` (unreadable → not-current); `test_latest_wins` (two appends, latest-by-HLC); `test_older_binary_ignores` (fsck no WARN); `test_cmd_show_renders_freshness` (CLI `show` stdout carries the flag); `test_library_shape_unchanged` (show_ticket/list/search still share one shape with a digest present); `test_prune_keep1` (a second `emit` supersedes the first — exactly one `TICKET_DIGEST` retained per ticket).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `TICKET_DIGEST` write→`latest_ticket_digest` read roundtrip with the `ticket_digest_v1` sentinel guard; `pytest ...::test_roundtrip`.
- [ ] Freshness flips stale on ANY of content-hash / model / digest_hash_version mismatch; `::test_freshness_all_triggers`. The content_hash uses a LOCAL `_normalize_text` (no `gates` import); the write path and the freshness-check path use the SAME normalizer (write-hash == read-hash for unchanged state); `::test_hash_write_read_parity`.
- [ ] `TICKET_DIGEST` in `EVENT_TYPES` + `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (not `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES`); older binary preserves-and-ignores, no fsck WARN; `::test_older_binary_ignores`.
- [ ] Unreadable current state → NOT-current (fail-closed); `::test_fail_closed`.
- [ ] The CLI `show` stdout renders digest presence + freshness (not raw text) via `_cmd_show`; `::test_cmd_show_renders_freshness`. The library `show_ticket`/`list`/`search` returns still share one shape with a digest present (parity invariant `test_show_list_search_share_one_shape` untouched); `::test_library_shape_unchanged`. `emit` keeps exactly one `TICKET_DIGEST` per ticket (`prune(keep=1)`, REVIEW_RESULT pattern; no archive/delete special-casing); `::test_prune_keep1`.
finding: The plan says `digest_freshness` is injected into the dict `_cmd_show` prints, covering both the default and `--output llm` arms, but does not specify whether `digest_freshness` should appear in the `--output llm` output. Since `to_llm(state)` passes unrecognized keys not in `OMIT_KEYS` through verbatim (confirmed in llm_format.py), injecting `digest_freshness` into `state` before the format branch causes it to appear in LLM output too — creating a show-vs-list LLM-output shape divergence the plan claims not to introduce. The plan explicitly invokes 'parity invariant' logic but leaves this edge case unresolved.
location: show freshness render section; `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py::_cmd_show` (~line 168-171); `src/rebar/reducer/llm_format.py::to_llm`
suggested_fix: Scope the `digest_freshness` injection to the `else` (default JSON) branch only: after `if fmt == 'llm': print(json.dumps(to_llm(state), ...))` / `else: state['digest_freshness'] = ...; print(json.dumps(state, ...))`. This keeps LLM output shape identical between `show` and `list` and matches the `managed_refs` selective-pop precedent.

title: Decommission deployed auto-lander infra (container, compose, terraform, SSM, alarm, nginx)
plan:
## Why
Remove the running auto-lander and all its cloud/host resources — cost + no longer used.
## What (repo)
- [ ] infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander — delete.
- [ ] infra/compose/docker-compose.yml — remove the autolander service, autoheal sidecar (if only for it), and gerrit_autolander + gerrit_autolander_tickets external volumes.

## What (live — operator, staged, careful)

- [ ] terraform apply: destroy the rebar-autolander-errors CloudWatch alarm + the SSM autolander-gerrit-token param (via the deleted .tf) — frontier creds, quiet window.
## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] terraform plan is empty after apply; make lint/typecheck/test green.
finding: The nginx template header (lines 4–11 of rebar.conf.template) documents `${AUTOLANDER_PORT}` as a substitution variable in its envsubst example comment. The plan removes only the `/autolander/status` location block body, leaving the header comment referencing AUTOLANDER_PORT — which becomes a stale, misleading instruction after decommission.

suggested_fix: Replace `${REVIEW_BOT_PORT} and ${AUTOLANDER_PORT}` with `${REVIEW_BOT_PORT}` in line 4, remove `AUTOLANDER_PORT` from the `export` line (line 6) and the `envsubst` argument (line 7), and update line 11 to read 'Only ${REVIEW_BOT_PORT} is templated'.

title: Enable pip caching on all setup-python steps in CI workflows
plan:
## Summary

No CI workflow enables pip dependency caching, so every job/matrix cell
re-downloads and rebuilds the heavy `.[dev]`→`[agents]` stack
(langchain/langgraph/anthropic/pydantic-ai/inspect-ai/opentelemetry). Measured
cold-vs-warm install into a fresh venv with an isolated pip cache: cold ~19s →
warm ~9s locally (cache ~52 MB) — caching roughly halves install time by
avoiding the re-download. The per-job saving is modest but multiplies across the
~10+ jobs/cells that install deps.

## What

Add `cache: 'pip'` + `cache-dependency-path: pyproject.toml` to EVERY
`actions/setup-python` step that subsequently installs project deps, across ALL
`.github/workflows/*`. Deps live in `pyproject.toml` (no separate lockfile), so
the cache is keyed on it: a dependency bump changes the key and invalidates the
cache automatically. Caching is purely additive — a cache miss behaves exactly
as today.

## Scope (files)

Every workflow with a dep-installing `setup-python` step:

- `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` (require-ticket, build-and-test, golden-path)
- `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` (reusable; 4 steps)
- `.github/workflows/test.yml` (lint, pip-audit, test, external, golden-path)
- `.github/workflows/eval.yml`, `.github/workflows/eval-plan-review-container.yml`
- `.github/workflows/external-integration.yml`
- `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml`, `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge-canary.yml`
- `.github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml`, `.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `cache: 'pip'` + `cache-dependency-path: pyproject.toml` is set on EVERY
  `actions/setup-python` step that installs project deps across all
  `.github/workflows/*` — verifiable by grep: no dep-installing `setup-python`
  step lacks the two `cache*` inputs.
- [ ] The cache is keyed on `pyproject.toml` content (via
  `cache-dependency-path`), so a dependency change mints a new cache key —
  verifiable from the config alone (no lockfile involved).
- [ ] YAML remains valid (`python -c "import yaml; ..."`) and each affected
  install step is otherwise byte-for-byte unchanged apart from the two added
  `cache*` inputs on its `setup-python`.
- [ ] [operator-attested] A repeat CI run on an unchanged `pyproject.toml` logs a
  pip cache hit (the `setup-python` "restore cache" step reports a cache
  restore), recorded as a comment citing the specific run.
- [ ] Change passes CI (`Verified +1`) and lands via Gerrit.
finding: The plan's 'What' section asserts it covers 'every actions/setup-python step that installs deps', but the AC explicitly scopes to only gerrit-verify.yaml, _optionality.yml, and test.yml. A grep of the workflows directory shows external-integration.yml has two dep-installing setup-python steps (line 35: installs '.[dev]'; line 141: installs a second job's deps) that are not mentioned in the AC. The plan's all-inclusive premise conflicts with its explicit scope.
location: Plan 'What' section / AC first bullet
suggested_fix: Add `.github/workflows/external-integration.yml` to the AC's list, or add a sentence like 'Manually-triggered workflows (`external-integration.yml`, `eval.yml`, etc.) are out of scope for this change' to make the exclusion explicit and intentional.
